And plenty of visible whale penises ‘about as long as this cabin’, Mayo said, spreading his arms wide. The only trouble is that he and others have seen the whales going at it at the ‘wrong’ times of year, considering that calves are born December through March. Which raises all kinds of questions: whether estimates of their gestation period are off, whether females might be able to store sperm and become pregnant at some later point, whether the whales might mate just for kicks. When it comes to whales, Hunt said: ‘There’s no other animal on the planet where we just know that little about basic reproduction.’ In 2012, as research in keratin-based tissues percolated in labs around the world, Raphaela Stimmelmayr, a biologist and wildlife veterinarian with North Slope Borough in Barrow, Alaska, who had been sending Hunt and Rolland bowhead poop for years, contacted the pair to suggest applying the keratin approach to baleen. ‘This suddenly got really exciting as soon as we realised we might be able to get a pregnancy signature out of the baleen,’ Hunt said. ‘There’s really been no way to reconstruct the history of an individual whale.’ Hunt, Rolland and several colleagues began with a pilot study published in 2014 of bowhead whales killed by native Alaskan hunters. Bowheads were a logical species to start with because these subsistence hunts are the one sustainable, fresh, reliable source of baleen, which you can get only from a dead whale. Also, bowheads, which are right whales’ closest relatives, have the longest- and slowest-growing baleen. An adult’s plate appears to capture 25 years of growth, longer than any other keratin-based tissue that has been studied. The team was able to detect the stress hormone cortisol and the reproductive hormone progesterone in the baleen, and could clearly distinguish between males and females, and pregnant and non-pregnant females. Since baleen grows continuously, it doesn’t produce annual rings like a tree does; instead, the researchers used existing estimates of baleen growth rates as a chronometer for their samples. Best of all, when they looked by intervals at older baleen from mature females, they found changes in progesterone that neatly matched bowheads’ estimated gestation length. The baleen seemed to document past pregnancies. What they needed to cross-check the technique was baleen from whales with known pregnancy histories. That’s where Stumpy and another ship-struck female North Atlantic right whale nicknamed Staccato came in. Both whales had a nearly 30-year-long file of regular sightings, including two recent pregnancies. And the carcasses of both happened to be found and dissected by scientists who had the foresight to collect plates of their baleen and tuck it away. ‘They’re really valuable plates,’ Hunt said. Sure

She reclaims the narrative, giving voice to the voiceless, illuminating the experiences of women, immigrants, and marginalized communities.

Title: A Disappointing Visit to Tasty Trails Food Truck Park on a Special Occasion

Let me start by saying, I was really looking forward to our family outing at Tasty Trails Food Truck Park. We chose this place for my son's birthday, hoping it would be a unique and enjoyable experience. But alas, the reality was far from what we expected.

Upon arrival, we were met with an incredibly challenging parking situation. The lot was jam-packed, and we had to circle around several times before finally finding a spot. This delay in our plans set a rather frustrating tone for the day.

Once settled, we approached the food trucks with anticipation, only to be greeted by long lines at each one. It was nearly impossible to decide where to eat, as every queue seemed just as lengthy as the next. We eventually opted for the truck that offered the shortest wait, but the disappointment didn't end there.

The food we ordered wasn't terrible, but it certainly wasn't worth the wait or the price. My son had been looking forward to trying their signature dish, which turned out to be a letdown. I can't help but think of the delicious meal we could have enjoyed at a nearby restaurant, given the same amount of time and money spent here.

The atmosphere was less than ideal as well. The park was crowded and noisy, making it difficult for us to find a quiet spot to enjoy our meal together. The tables were sticky and lacking in character, which added to the overall lackluster experience.

In a minor contradiction to my negative review, I must mention that the staff members we encountered were friendly and helpful. They did their best to accommodate our needs and make our visit as pleasant as possible, given the circumstances.

As the sun began to set, I found myself wishing we had chosen a different location for my son's special day. Tasty Trails Food Truck Park may have sounded like a fun and exciting option, but it left us wanting more on this particular occasion.

If you're looking for a food truck park experience, I would suggest giving Tasty Trails another try during a less busy time or on a different day of the week. Just be prepared for the parking situation and potential long lines at each food truck. On the other hand, if you're seeking a memorable family outing with excellent food, I would recommend looking elsewhereperhaps on a quieter street in a cozier restaurant.

In conclusion, Tasty Trails Food Truck Park had potential, but unfortunately fell short during our visit. Here's to better food truck experiences in the future!

In the labyrinthine dance of life, I find myself grappling with the intricate tango between intuition and logical thinking. It's a dance that ebbs and flows, often leaving me in a perpetual state of wonderment about our human existence.

Take for instance, the humble bee. To some, it's nothing more than a mindless automaton, a reflex machine devoid of consciousness. But what if we are misreading its mechanical movements as lack of awareness? What if its seemingly predetermined actions are not mere reflexes but intuitive responses born from a depth of understanding that surpasses our comprehension?

And then there's tarot, the ancient deck of cards that whispers secrets and promises. To some, it's a tool for therapy, a means to tap into the subconscious and heal. To others, it's mere superstition, a relic of a bygone era. Yet, I can't help but feel drawn to its mystique, its whispers of wisdom that resonate deep within my soul.

It's in these moments, when I'm torn between the cold logic of science and the warm embrace of intuition, that I feel most alive. It's as if I'm standing at the crossroads of two worlds, one grounded in reason, the other steeped in emotion. And it's here, at this intersection, where I believe we uncover the deeper truths about our existence.

But what are these truths? Are they hidden in the intricate dance of a bee's flight patterns or the enigmatic symbols etched onto tarot cards? Or perhaps they lie within us, waiting to be unearthed by our intuitive senses. I don't know. Perhaps the answers are as elusive as the secrets that tarot cards hold, shrouded in layers of complexity and mystery.

As I continue my journey through life, I find myself drawn back to these questions, seeking answers that remain just out of reach. It's a dance that continues, an endless tango between intuition and logic, a dance that reveals the depth and beauty of our human existence.

I can only hope that one day, the music will stop and I'll find myself in the arms of understanding. Until then, I'll continue to dance, to explore, to wonder, and to seek out the deeper truths about our human life.

As I push open the creaking door of the neighborhood caf, the scent of freshly brewed coffee greets me like a warm embrace. Settling into my favorite corner seat, I watch as people come and go, each one a unique tapestry of human experiences, emotions, and thoughts. This simple act, this everyday occurrence, is but a small window into the complex web of empathy and altruism that binds us all together.

Empathy and altruism, two concepts often intertwined yet distinct, are deeply rooted in our biological makeup, evolving over millions of years to help ensure our survival as social beings. At the heart of these phenomena lies the neural circuitry of the brain, specifically the limbic system and the prefrontal cortex.

The limbic system, with its amyggdala and hippocampus, is responsible for processing emotional information, triggering responses such as empathy when we witness another's pain or joy. Meanwhile, the prefrontal cortex, the seat of our highest cognitive functions, enables us to make decisions based on those emotions, guiding us toward altruistic acts.

Yet, these mechanisms are not static; they adapt and change over time. As we grow and interact with the world, our empathic and altruistic responses become more nuanced, shaped by our experiences, beliefs, and culture. For instance, a young child may react with immediate tears upon witnessing another's distress, while an adult might pause to consider the situation before offering assistance.

The intricate dance between biology and environment continues as we age, leading us down paths of empathy and altruism that differ for each individual. However, the essence remains: a desire to connect, to understand, and to help.

Recent studies have shed light on the fascinating interplay between our brains and our behaviors, particularly in relation to empathy and altruism. For example, neuroscientists have discovered that our immune systems play a crucial role in these processes. The presence of cytokines, immune-stimulating elements, within neurons has been linked to normal functioning of memory and learning, suggesting that our bodies' responses to illness and stress may influence our capacity for empathy and altruism.

These findings, though still in their infancy, underscore the intricate interconnectedness of our biological systems and our social behaviors. They challenge us to reconsider the traditional boundaries between 'mind' and 'body', urging us to view ourselves as holistic organisms rather than separate entities.

As I finish my coffee, I ponder these concepts, contemplating how my own experiences have shaped my empathic and altruistic responses. I wonder what new discoveries await us in the future, and how our understanding of empathy and altruism will continue to evolve. And as I step back out into the world, I'm reminded that every interaction, every encounter, holds the potential to reveal more about these fundamental aspects of human nature.

text:
In our ceaseless pursuit of understanding, we often find ourselves at a crossroads, questioning the essence of something fundamental: conscious experience. What makes us tick? What drives our thoughts and actions? Is there more to this world than what meets the eye?

For centuries, philosophers have grappled with these questions, offering various theories that range from the soul being a physical entity to it being an ethereal, spiritual force. Yet, as we delve deeper into the intricacies of the human mind and the workings of the universe, the answers seem to elude us, slipping through our fingers like quicksand.

Take, for instance, the concept of free will. We believe that we make choices based on our thoughts and desires, but are we truly in control? Or is it all predestined, a cosmic dance choreographed long before we were even born? The more we learn about the brain and its functions, the less clear-cut these questions become.

Neuroscientists have spent decades studying the brain, peering into its labyrinthine recesses to unravel the mysteries of our thoughts, emotions, and actions. And yet, for all their efforts, they seem no closer to understanding the nature of conscious experience. We know that neurons fire in specific patterns when we think or feel something, but what is it that propels those neurons into action? Is it the electric currents racing through our synapses, or is there something more?

Consider the intricate dance of proteins within a cell. They jostle and interact, creating a symphony of molecular movements that underpin life itself. But what drives these proteins to move in such precise patterns? Is it some unseen force guiding their actions, or is it simply the laws of physics at work?

And then there are the connections between cells, tissues, and organs - the complex web of communication that allows our bodies to function as a cohesive whole. How do these signals travel from one cell to another, from one organ to another, allowing us to feel pain or pleasure, love or hatred? Is it through the electrical impulses that zip along our nerves, or is it something more intangible, like the very essence of our being?

As we continue to explore these questions, we find ourselves at a fascinating juncture. The more we learn about the molecular-scale workings of our bodies, the less sense it makes to attribute consciousness to a single gene or protein. Instead, we are beginning to see that consciousness is not a simple blueprint but rather a complex interplay of proteins, cells, and signals. It's a dance, an ever-changing symphony of interactions that defies easy categorization.

But even as we uncover the intricacies of this dance, we are left with more questions than answers. How do these molecular interactions give rise to our thoughts and emotions? How do they allow us to experience the world around us in such rich, vivid detail? And perhaps most importantly, what is it that binds us all together, allowing us to connect, empathize, and form the intricate tapestry of human society?

As we grapple with these questions, one thing becomes increasingly clear: our understanding of conscious experience will continue to evolve as we explore new territories, delve deeper into the mysteries of the universe, and push the boundaries of human knowledge. And perhaps, in time, we will come to see that consciousness is not a singular entity but rather an ever-changing, evolving force that defines us all. But for now, we can only marvel at the complexity of this dance and wonder what it means for our lives, our relationships, and our place in the universe.

text: more practically urgent was the desire to remake and redeem British identity in this time of decolonisation in terms of the communitarian values of the working class – rather than the long-vaunted paternalistic values of the (imperial) ruling class, as Orwell had done in the time of fascism. EP’s book was in this sense completely shaped by the context of decolonisation, however silently. From the Age of Revolution that was his subject, rebellion abroad had influenced British radicalism. Post-Second World War anticolonial rebellion likewise ruptured British thought and activism. EP wrote the book in the shadow of anticolonial disillusionment with the West. Shortly after he and the Jamaican-born cultural theorist Stuart Hall founded the New Left Review in 1960, he left in high dudgeon as devotees of theoretical history took over the editorial board. He particularly objected to the journal’s adoption of a ‘Third Worldism’ in which ‘the entire “West”… stands impeached by its complicity with colonialism,’ as he wrote in his (only recently published) resignation letter in 1963 – the year that The Making of the English Working Class was published. Affirming the identity of interests of peoples across the alleged West-Third World divide, EP addressed the judgments of Frantz Fanon’s The Wretched of the Earth (1961), which, like Tagore and Gandhi earlier, cautioned forcefully against ‘mimicry’ of a West whose culture and institutions were manifestly morally and practically bankrupt, with Europe headed ‘into the abyss’. Sympathetically conceding that the text’s context made it ‘not only comprehensible but inevitable’, EP nevertheless fervently contested the conclusion that the ‘West’ had ‘nothing to offer’. His effort in his work to recover alternative ‘English’ values – radical humanistic ones – was partly in a spirit of defiant redemption. ‘For us,’ he explained, ‘the “European game” can never be finished’: ‘If “our” tradition has failed … then it is for us to put it in repair’ rather than hastily conclude that ‘the humanist values discovered in the West are corrupted beyond recall’. He strove to discover a different possible England whose values didn’t lead ineluctably to empire. In the labyrinth of our daily lives, we often grapple with choices, some seemingly inconsequential, others life-altering. Yet, the role of abstract theory in these decisions remains a paradoxical enigma. On one hand, theories feel distant, impersonal, and academic - a far cry from the urgency of real-life dilemmas. On the other, they offer a lens through which we can dissect, analyze, and potentially master the complexities surrounding us.

Take, for instance, the decision to move houses. It's a personal, emotional journey that involves factors like location, affordability, and comfort. But what about the hidden forces at play? The dynamics of gentrification, the invisible hand of the market, or the impact of urban sprawl - abstract concepts that, when understood, can illuminate the broader context of our seemingly individual choices.

Or consider relationships. They are built on emotions, compromise, and trust. Yet, psychology offers theories like attachment theory or the stages of intimacy, providing frameworks to navigate the intricacies of human connection. These theories can help us understand why we cling, why we pull away, or why our partnerships evolve over time.

However, it's important to remember that these theories are not universal truths. They are tools, lenses through which we view the world. Just as a kaleidoscope changes with every twist and turn, so too do the applications of abstract theory in our lives. What remains constant is our ability to adapt, to learn, and to grow - skills honed through the exploration of these theories and their application to our lived experiences.

But here's the rub: Theories can be deceptive mirrors. They reflect what we want to see, what we fear, and what we aspire towards. As such, they can lead us astray if we don't acknowledge their limitations. They are not prescriptive; they are descriptive, offering insights into patterns rather than dictating outcomes.

As I navigate the maze of decisions that life presents, I find myself drawn to abstract theory, not as a compass but as a map. It helps me see the terrain, understand the contours, and perhaps, make wiser choices. But it doesn't tell me where to go or what to do; that, ultimately, is up to me. The journey continues...

Title: A Heatwave Disappointment - Toshi's Ramen Bar

I've been a ramen fan for years now, and I thought I'd give Toshi's Ramen Bar a try during this sweltering heatwave. Boy, was that a mistake!

First things first, the service speed here is painfully slow. I know it's hot outside, but it's not like they're cooking ramen over an open flame or something. It took forever for my order to arrive, and by the time it did, I could hardly taste anything because I was so famished.

Now, let me talk about the ramen itself. I ordered their signature bowl - the Fireball Ramen. Sounds good, right? Well, it wasn't. The broth was bland, and the noodles were overcooked. It felt like they just warmed up some pre-made stuff and called it a day. And don't even get me started on the pork slice - it tasted like it had been sitting out for hours.

I also got an iced coffee to go with my ramen, but that was just as disappointing. The drink was way too sweet, and the ice melted almost immediately, turning it into a watery mess.

The ambience here is...well, let's say it's not exactly what I was expecting. It's all dark and moody inside, which would be fine if the air conditioning worked properly. But nope, it's just as hot in here as it is outside. The only thing that kinda caught my eye were the Japanese posters on the walls - they looked cool, but they didn't make up for the lackluster experience.

So, if you're looking for a good ramen spot during a heatwave, I'd suggest giving Toshi's Ramen Bar a miss. I think I'll stick to my favorite ramen joint next time. At least they know how to make a decent bowl of noodles soup!

In the labyrinthine complexities of the human mind, a paradox unfolds: the very capacity that defines our species - neuroplasticity - is also the source of its limitations. This elusive trait, the brain's remarkable ability to reorganize and adapt itself throughout life, has been hailed as the cornerstone of our resilience and adaptability. Yet, it seems to have bequeathed upon us a peculiar predicament that colors our perception and shapes our potential.

Consider this: while we pride ourselves on our cognitive prowess, our minds are biased towards the negative. The mundane acts of daily life - a compliment received, a sunset witnessed - often fade into obscurity, eclipsed by the vividness of our pain and struggles. Our memories retain traumas with an uncanny tenacity, while moments of joy are fleeting and transient.

It is this peculiarity that has earned us the title of 'the negativity bias species.' We process negative information more swiftly and intensely than positive, and it leaves a deeper imprint on our minds. The echoes of a harsh word linger longer than the strains of a sweet melody, a bitter memory lingers more potently than a cherished moment.

This inherent negativity bias has its roots deep within us, in the very structure of our brains. Our amyggala, the primal sentinel that alerts us to danger, is geared towards bad news, storing it with alarming persistence. This is why a bad day casts a shadow over the following ones, while a good day is quickly forgotten.

This bias shapes our interactions with the world and each other. We invest more energy in avoiding a negative reputation than in cultivating a positive one, prioritizing the prevention of harm over the pursuit of happiness. Our emotional landscape is dotted with potholes and detours, marked by fear and doubt.

Yet, this negativity bias does not exist in isolation. It coexists with another facet of our neuroplasticity - our capacity for growth and change. This contradiction, between our tendency to cling to the negative and our ability to transform ourselves, is at once our curse and our salvation.

In the grand tapestry of human potential, the threads of neuroplasticity are intricately woven. They stretch from the cradle to the grave, coloring our experiences, shaping our perceptions, and defining our reality. Yet, they remain elusive, a tantalizing enigma that beckons us forward, urging us to explore, understand, and harness this extraordinary gift.

In the meantime, we tread the fine line between adversity and resilience, between suffering and transformation. We grapple with our past, seeking solace in its lessons, and find hope in our capacity for change. And as we journey through life, each of us becomes a canvas upon which neuroplasticity paints its unique masterpiece.

According to Kripke, when a proper name is introduced, it’s typically attached directly to an individual. There might be a baptism in which we say: ‘By “Aristotle” we’ll mean him [pointing].’ Our intention is that the name should then pick out that person in any actual or possible situation, including situations where they happen not to fit the descriptions we associate with the name. Consider a possible situation in which Aristotle goes in for gardening, while Aristotle’s brother is the person taught by Plato and who teaches Alexander. That didn’t happen, but it might have happened. Kripke points out that if ‘Aristotle’ just means ‘the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander’, then ‘Aristotle’ would pick out the wrong person in that possible situation. Indeed, it seems that the actual situation could turn out to be one in which Aristotle has few if any of the properties we associate with him. Historians could conceivably discover that Aristotle was a fraud who claimed credit for the achievements of others – including being taught by Plato and teaching Alexander – and somehow got away with it. But if that’s true, then the name ‘Aristotle’ can’t just mean ‘the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander’. For then it would be a trivial truth, not a potentially contestable historical fact, that Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. Kripke argues that many common nouns, such as ‘gold’, ‘water’ and ‘tiger’, also refer directly, rather than by way of some sort of mental checklist. We define ‘gold’ by saying: ‘Gold is that substance’ (pointing to a sample) and allow the world, rather than any associated description, to determine the conditions under which something qualifies as a sample of gold. The most famous illustration of how the name of a substance is supposed to function in this way is provided not by Kripke, but by Putnam, another leading proponent of the ‘theory of direct reference’. Putnam asks us to imagine a Twin Earth – just like our Earth – which contains doppelgängers of us humans. The only difference between the two Earths is that on Twin Earth the clear, thirst-quenching, etc liquid that fills the oceans, lakes and rivers is not the chemical substance H2O, but another substance – XYZ. Suppose it’s 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered. On both Earths, the inhabitants call their liquid ‘water’. And, because it’s 1750, they associate the same mental checklist with that term: both think of ‘water’ as the substance that’s clear, thirst-quenching, boils at 100°C and so on. Last week, I found myself standing before a traffic court, my heart pounding as I awaited the judge's decision. I had been given a ticket for running a red light, an action I now regretted deeply. As I stood there, a question swirled in my mind: was this simply an instance of human error, or did my actions reflect something deeper about my character?

The legal system, with its black-and-white rules and rigid structure, can sometimes feel at odds with the complexities of human morality. The law is built on clear guidelines, punishments for violations, and a focus on justice served. Yet, our actions, thoughts, and motivations are rarely so straightforward. We are not just the sum of our genetic makeup or the product of our environment; we are dynamic, ever-changing beings, influenced by a myriad of factors that defy easy categorization.

Take, for instance, the recent case of a man who, despite having a 'perfect' genome for athletic prowess, struggled with addiction and self-destructive behavior. Or consider the artist whose groundbreaking work was created amidst personal turmoil and pain. These examples serve as a reminder that life, and by extension, human behavior, is not merely a collection of deterministic causes and effects. It is a complex web of interactions, influences, and emergent properties that defy easy explanation or prediction.

In the courtroom, as I awaited my fate, I couldn't help but reflect on these ideas. Was I simply a product of my genes, or was there more to me than that? Was my actions at the intersection merely a momentary lapse in judgment, or did they reflect deeper character flaws?

As a society, we grapple with similar questions every day. How do we balance the need for laws and regulations to maintain order and protect citizens, with the need to allow individuals the freedom to make their own choices and develop their own character? It's a delicate dance, one that requires empathy, understanding, and a willingness to see the complexity in each individual case.

As I left the courtroom, the judge's decision still hanging in the air, I couldn't help but feel humbled by the intricacies of human existence. We are not mere machines, running on predetermined programs. We are complex beings, capable of great good and great evil, shaped by a myriad of factors that stretch far beyond our genetic makeup or environment.

And so, as we navigate this complex world, let us strive to remember the inherent complexity in each individual case. Let us seek to understand, rather than judge, and to forgive, rather than condemn. For it is only through empathy and understanding that we can hope to create a just and fair society for all.

Two centuries later, John Calvin reached the same conclusion while reflecting on the passage in I Kings in which God ‘wills that the false king Ahab be deceived’, sending the Devil to fulfill this wish ‘with a definite command to be a lying spirit in the mouth of all the prophets’. For Calvin, this bit of scripture was particularly powerful. Ever since Augustine, theologians had sought to absolve God from whatever evil might be laid at his feet by invoking a distinction between what God wills and what God permits. God does not will evil things to happen, he merely allows them to happen. No good here, Calvin would retort, because scripture states that God does much more than merely allow the Devil to lie to Ahab. Chalk it up to God’s infinite and infinitely inscrutable nature, Calvin writes, a God who hates all liars, commands us never to lie, yet seems to lie all the same. God might well never lie, Calvin concludes with palpable frustration, but how this is so is forever beyond our comprehension. much like the Devil, God makes a bad deal sound good as he tempts the Devil to crucify the innocent Jesus Even those thinkers who asserted that God by definition could not deceive, that deception was incompatible with God’s divine perfection, found themselves compelled to admit that God does sometimes deceive. Historians often miss this sort of theological backpedalling because it almost always takes place off the philosophical stage, so to speak, not in theological treatises but in commentaries on the Bible, in sermons and in letters, especially when those commentaries, sermons and letters concern the life of Christ. We have untrammelled access to our own minds, so the argument goes, and this immediacy of introspection means that we are rarely wrong about ourselves. This is known as the ‘privileged access’ view of self-knowledge, and has been dominant in philosophy in various guises for much of the 20th century. René Descartes relied on self-reflection in this way to reach his conclusion ‘I think, therefore I am,’ noting along the way that: ‘I know clearly that there is nothing that can be perceived by me more easily or more clearly than my own mind.’ An alternative view suggests that we infer what we think or believe from a variety of cues – just as we infer what others think or feel from observing their behaviour. This suggests that self-knowledge is not as immediate as it seems. For instance, I might infer that I am anxious about an upcoming presentation because my heart is racing and my breathing is heavier. But I might be wrong about this – perhaps I am just feeling excited. This kind of psychological reframing is often used by sports coaches to help athletes maintain composure under pressure. The philosopher most often associated with the inferential view is Gilbert Ryle, who proposed in The Concept of Mind (1949) that we gain self-knowledge by applying the tools we use to understand other minds to ourselves: ‘The sorts of things that I can find out about myself are the same as the sorts of things that I can find out about other people, and the methods of finding them out are much the same.’ Ryle’s idea is neatly summarised by another New Yorker cartoon in which a husband says to his wife: ‘How should I know what I’m thinking? I’m not a mind reader.’ Many philosophers since Ryle have considered the strong inferential view as somewhat crazy, and written it off before it could even get going. The philosopher Quassim Cassam, author of Self-knowledge for Humans (2014), describes the situation: Philosophers who defend inferentialism – Ryle is usually mentioned in this context – are then berated for defending a patently absurd view. The assumption that intentional self-knowledge is normally immediate … is rarely defended; it’s just seen as obviously correct. But if we take a longer view of history, the idea that we have some sort of special, direct access to our minds is the exception, rather than the rule. For the ancient Greeks, self-knowledge was not all-encompassing, but a work in progress, and something to be striven toward, as captured by the exhortation to ‘know thyself’ carved on the Temple of Delphi. But what is obvious in the words of both Macdonald and Pratt are the ideas that informed them: that the white man knows what is best for Indigenous children – better than their own families and communities – and what is best is assimilation, against which the ‘contaminating’ influences of family, culture and tradition must be held at bay. The intention was to play out these ideas until the very end. During a parliamentary committee in 1920, Canada’s Deputy Superintendent of Indian Affairs, Duncan Campbell Scott, explained the state’s ultimate goal: ‘Our object is to continue until there is not a single Indian in Canada that has not been absorbed into the body politic.’ 13141Girls at Kamloops Indian Residential School (date unknown) 13142Kamloops Indian Residential School (date unknown) 13143Children at Kamloops Indian Residential School, 1931 13144A classroom at Kamloops Indian Reservation School (date unknown) Today, some might wonder why separating children from their families and cultures could have seemed like a good idea as recently as a century ago. We forget that these practices of separation and forgetting weren’t incidental, or historical accidents. Instead, they were deliberately positioned as progressive and philanthropic. And residential ‘schooling’ hasn’t been the only means by which these progressive and philanthropic practices of separation and re-education have been implemented. Forcible adoptions and ‘care’ systems – as in the ‘Stolen Generations’ in Australia and the ‘Sixties Scoop’ in Canada – show that those ‘progressive’ practices have continued and diversified. The fact that Indigenous children today are disproportionately highly represented in care systems worldwide suggests that these practices are still being implemented. Peoples of colour were left in an intermediate position between Europeans and the animal world The ideas that informed the Indigenous residential ‘school’ systems did not spring up in a vacuum. They are part of a longer history of Eurocentrism – whether informed by Christianity, Social Darwinism or today’s neoliberalism – in which peoples of colour are deemed to be ‘less than’ or ‘Other’. One of the sources of this way of thinking was the papal bull Inter caetera (1493), issued by Pope Alexander VI, which deemed non-Europeans as pagans whose souls would be damned without intervention from Christians. The Inter caetera explicitly allowed ‘full and free power, authority, and jurisdiction of every kind’ to colonising Europeans, effectively permitting the dispossession, enslavement and mass murder of Indigenous peoples. The papal bull is part of the so-called ‘doctrine of discovery’, a set of legal and religious precepts that certain European nations saw as giving them carte blanche to colonise the world. This dovetails with a cognitive-emotional phenomenon called ‘psychic numbing’, which the psychologist Paul Slovic describes as the inability to appreciate losses of life as they become larger. The importance of saving one life is great when it is the first, or only, life saved, but diminishes marginally as the total number of lives saved increases. Thus, psychologically, the importance of saving one life is diminished against the background of a larger threat – we will likely not ‘feel’ much different, nor value the difference, between saving 87 lives and saving 88, if these prospects are presented to us separately. Now imagine that the number isn’t 87 or 88 deaths but 8 billion – the total human population on Earth today. As a Washington Post article from 1947 quotes Joseph Stalin as saying, ‘if only one man dies of hunger, that is a tragedy. If millions die, that’s only statistics,’ which is often truncated to: ‘A single death is a tragedy, a million deaths are a statistic.’ The point is that an extinction-causing catastrophe would be horrendous to a degree that our puny minds cannot even begin to grasp, intellectually or emotionally, although simply understanding this fact can help us better calibrate assessments of its badness, by compensating for this deficiency. In my opinion, the terribleness of such catastrophes – events with the highest possible body count – is more than enough reason to prioritise, as a species, projects aimed at reducing such risk. The default view about Going Extinct is even more profound, and its implications even more compelling, than one might initially believe. But I also think that Being Extinct would be regrettable for many reasons. As Mary Shelley wrote in her novel The Last Man (1826) – one of the very first books to address the core questions of existential ethics – without humanity there would be no more poetry, philosophy, painting, music, theatre, laughter, knowledge and science, and that would be very sad. I feel the pull of this sentiment, and find myself especially moved by the fact that our extinction would bring the transgenerational enterprise of scientific understanding to a screeching halt. It would be a monumental shame if humanity had popped into existence, looked around at the Universe in puzzlement and awe, pondered the Leibnizian question of why there is something rather than nothing, and then vanished into the oblivion before knowing the answer. Maybe the answer is unknowable, but even discovering this fact could provide a degree of intellectual satisfaction and psychological closure, an ‘ah-ha’ moment that relieves and vindicates one’s prior frustration. However, when we think of other people, particularly people in need, we rate their physical needs as more important, Juliana Schroeder and Nicholas Epley found. Likewise, when we think of what motivates us, our ambitions, principles and ideals come to mind – yet we tend to believe that others are more motivated by external rewards such as money, prestige or reputation. We reflect on our own actions in light of how well they were performed and whether they met our objectives, but on those of others in terms of their interpersonal and moral consequences. Are these asymmetries simply biases that come from our entrenched egocentrism? Perhaps. But a simpler, and kinder, interpretation is that they’re the result of what’s immediately available to us. We can access others only through what they express by means of their bodies, whereas our own thoughts, feelings and sensations simply are. We know that others have rich inner lives just like we do, but our immediate experience is of their expressive bodies. Victims find the perpetrator’s intentions unfathomable. Perpetrators think the victim helped provoke the action Asymmetries also appear in how we visually recall our past experiences. Research pioneered by Georgia Nigro and Ulric Neisser shows that, although we usually recall an event from inside the scene, more or less how we experienced it, we sometimes recall seeing ourselves. It’s common for people to remember themselves swimming from a point above themselves, for example. Why this is the case is a bit of a puzzle. Lisa Libby and her colleagues found a clue when they discovered that taking a different perspective on oneself allows a person to represent different aspects of a situation. When we represent ourselves in our own memories, we focus on contextual factors, such as the larger significance of actions and events, the larger setting, and how we and our actions appear to others. But when we recall an event from the point of view we experienced it from, the details of the immediate environment are clearly represented, as are our bodily reactions and our feelings, and the where and when of the event. Many of us are intimately familiar with the asymmetries that can arise in disputes with spouses and friends. In interpersonal conflict, it’s easy to detect large differences in how a situation is constructed. The social psychologist Roy Baumeister has found (perhaps predictably) that, when it comes to ordinary wrongdoing such as breaking promises or betraying secrets, perpetrators minimise the importance of what they’ve done, whereas victims maximise it. But a more surprising difference is that victims find the perpetrator’s intentions unfathomable, describing them as ‘incoherent, contradictory, arbitrary, or senseless’. Perpetrators, on the other hand, lean towards thinking that the victim helped provoke the action, that the action was actually justified given the circumstances, or that it simply couldn’t be helped. text:
In the realm of dreams, lies a fascinating intersection between philosophy and lived experience - lucid dreaming. This phenomenon, where one becomes aware they are dreaming and can exert some control over their narrative, challenges our understanding of reality and consciousness.

Lucid dreaming, in many ways, echoes philosophical discussions about the nature of reality and selfhood. For instance, it questions Descartes' famous assertion 'Cogito ergo sum' (I think, therefore I am). If one can think within a dream, are they truly not? Yet, the dream world is indisputably not the physical reality we inhabit. This dichotomy raises intriguing questions about the essence of self and consciousness.

However, it's not all philosophical ponderings. There's a tangible, experiential aspect to lucid dreaming that transcends theory. It allows us to engage with our own minds in ways previously thought impossible. We can manipulate our dreams, interact with dream characters, and even question the reality of our surroundings - experiences that are hard to reconcile with traditional understandings of consciousness and self.

Yet, amidst this whirlwind of possibilities, a doubt creeps in. Is lucid dreaming truly a bridge between theory and experience, or merely an illusion within another illusion? Can we trust the 'reality' of a dream when our waking understanding is so often questioned by philosophers?

These questions prompt us to reconsider what constitutes reality and selfhood. Lucid dreaming forces us to confront these concepts head-on, offering a unique perspective that few other experiences can match. It pushes the boundaries of our understanding, challenging us to question our assumptions about consciousness and the nature of reality.

As I delve deeper into this fascinating realm, I find myself increasingly enthralled by its potential implications for philosophy. Yet, I am also haunted by doubts, unsure if these dreams are merely illusions within an illusion. Regardless, the experience of lucid dreaming offers a unique lens through which to view age-old philosophical questions, and for that, it is truly remarkable.

Title: A Disappointing Visit to the Galaxy Theater

I've always enjoyed movies, but my recent visit to this theater was far from enjoyable. I needed a quick stop before a film, having just come from surgery. I hoped for a comfortable, clean environment to rest and gather myself. Unfortunately, that wasn't the case here.

The place seemed busy, which usually isn't a problem, but today it felt overcrowded. Lines at the ticket counter and concession stand were long, adding to my unease. I finally got my tickets and headed to the restroom before settling into my seat.

Oh, the restroom! It was less than ideal. The toilets looked like they hadn't been cleaned in a while, and the sink area was cluttered with random items. I wish I could say it was just the men's room, but it seemed as though the women's facilities weren't well-maintained either.

I managed to find an empty seat towards the back and settled in, trying to focus on the previews. The seat itself wasn't comfortable  hard and worn, like someone had been sitting there for hours. I couldn't imagine being here for a full movie under these conditions.

Now, let me talk about the popcorn. I usually love popcorn at the movies, but this was just sad. It tasted stale, and there were barely any kernels in the bag. I should have known better than to expect much from a theater like this.

As I sat there, feeling let down by the whole experience, I couldn't help but wonder what made this place popular. Sure, it might be conveniently located, but is that enough to justify such poor service and lack of cleanliness? I doubt I'll be coming back anytime soon, and I hope they improve their offerings for the sake of other moviegoers like me.

Such ideas might no longer be explicit, but the drug concept certainly carries a murky sense of the foreign and alien even now. That’s why it rarely applies to the psychoactive substances that we see as part of normal life, whether caffeine in the west, coca in the Andes, or ayahuasca in the Amazon. During the first years of the 20th century, opium, morphine and cocaine became less socially acceptable, rather as tobacco has in our era. Their use was now viewed through the prism of medical harm, and their users correspondingly started to seem feckless or morally weak. The drugs themselves became, in a sense, ‘legal highs’: not technically prohibited but retreating into the shadows, available only under the counter or from those in the know. And then, once their sale was formally banned in the years around the Great War, ‘drugs’ became a term with legal weight: a specified list of substances that were not merely medically dangerous or culturally foreign, but confined to the criminal classes. The banning of drugs occasioned strikingly little public debate, certainly compared with the prohibition of alcohol in the US. Then again, the ‘drug problem’ was pretty marginal at that point, and confined to subcultures (ethnic, bohemian, criminal) without a public voice. The only organised resistance to this new language of condemnation came from the pharmaceutical industry, concerned that its legitimate trade was being tarnished by unfortunate associations. What’s now the American Pharmacists Association, pressured by its major corporate sponsors such as Johnson &amp; Johnson, complained about the casual use of terms such as ‘drug evil’, ‘drug fiend’ and ‘drug habit’, and lobbied newspapers to specify the drugs in question as ‘narcotics’ or ‘opiates’. But ‘drugs’ was too vague and too useful to replace with more precise terms. It conveyed not simply particular chemicals, but a moral position on the use of them by certain people and for certain purposes. This position was eventually enshrined in the legal frameworks that emerged to prohibit them. The 1961 UN Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, the founding document of the international drug laws, is unique among UN conventions in using the word ‘evil’ to describe the problem it seeks to address. Legislators celebrated the 1961 Convention as the culmination of a 50-year battle to prohibit drugs, a battle that had begun at the Hague Opium Conference of 1911. Yet with hindsight, 1961 was the moment at which the consensus around the evils of drugs began to fracture. An adventurous postwar generation, the first to be raised as truly global consumers, was awakening to the realisation that alcohol was not the world’s only intoxicant. text:
In the grand tapestry of our interconnected world, we often marvel at the wonders that unfold before us - a testament to human ingenuity and resilience. Yet, amidst this kaleidoscope of progress, there are stark reminders of the depths of inequality that continue to plague our societies. One such reminder comes in the form of Malala Yousafzai, a beacon of hope for girls' education worldwide.

Malala, a young Pakistani activist, has fearlessly challenged tradition and defied adversity, advocating for the right to education for millions of girls silenced by circumstance. Her journey is a poignant reminder of the power of individual courage and determination in a world that often seems indifferent to suffering.

But as I sit here, penning down these thoughts, I find myself grappling with doubt. How can one girl change the course of history? Isn't it the collective responsibility of us all to ensure that every child has access to education? These questions linger in the back of my mind, yet they do not diminish the impact Malala has made.

In a world where screens offer glimpses into the lives of others, Malala serves as a beacon - a symbol of hope and resilience that transcends geographical boundaries. She stands tall amidst the towering edifices of indifference, her voice echoing through the chambers of power. Her activism is not merely about securing education for girls; it is a call to action, a clarion call that rings loud and clear - we must do better.

Yet, as I contemplate this global impact, I am struck by the stark contrast between Malala's world and my own. In my corner of the world, girls attend school without fear or prejudice. We take for granted the freedom to learn, to dream, to aspire. But in other parts, girls are denied this basic right, their dreams shattered by societal norms and tradition.

Malala's story serves as a stark reminder of the vast chasms that separate us - not just geographically, but in terms of opportunity and freedom. It is a call to action, a clarion call that resonates deep within me. How can I remain complacent when there are girls out there who yearn for an education, who crave the chance to learn and grow?

As I continue to ponder these questions, I am filled with a sense of urgency. The world needs more Malalas - more individuals who dare to challenge the status quo, who refuse to be silenced by fear or tradition. We must strive to create a world where every girl can attend school, where every child has an equal opportunity to learn and grow.

In the end, perhaps the greatest impact of Malala's activism lies not in the number of girls she has managed to educate, but in the change she has sparked within us - a change that compels us to question, to challenge, to act. Her story serves as a reminder that we are all connected, that our actions have ripples that extend far beyond our immediate reach.

As I close this segment of my thoughts, I am left with a sense of hope - hope for a world where every girl can attend school, where education is not a privilege but a right. Malala's story is a testament to the power of the human spirit, a reminder that change is possible if we dare to dream, to act.

And so, I continue my journey, my thoughts swirling with images of classrooms filled with eager students, their eyes alight with curiosity and aspiration. I am reminded once again of the power of education, of its ability to transform lives, to shatter barriers, to create a brighter future for all. And I am resolved - resolute in my belief that we must do better, that we must strive for a world where every girl can attend school, where every child has an equal opportunity to learn and grow.

text:
In the realm of modern political philosophy, few figures loom as large as John Rawls. His seminal work, A Theory of Justice, has left an indelible mark on contemporary discourse, shaping societies and influencing discussions about justice and fairness. Yet, like a river that carves new paths over time, Rawls' theory has evolved and adapted, reflecting the complexities of our ever-changing world.

At its core, Rawls' Theory posits an idealized social contract, whereby individuals come together to form a society guided by principles of justice. The veil of ignorance - a thought experiment in which decision-makers are unaware of their own socioeconomic position or advantages - is used to ensure that the principles established are fair and just for all members of society. This concept has sparked widespread debate, with some heralding it as a beacon of egalitarianism, while others criticize its idealistic nature and abstract assumptions.

In practice, Rawls' theory has influenced policy-making in numerous ways. For example, the concept of 'difference principle' - which suggests that social inequalities should serve the greatest benefit of the least advantaged - has been invoked to justify progressive taxation systems and welfare programs designed to uplift those most in need. Yet, critics argue that these policies can often perpetuate dependency and disincentivize hard work, creating a cycle of poverty rather than alleviating it.

Moreover, Rawls' theory has been applied in various contexts beyond traditional political arenas. In business ethics, for instance, the veil of ignorance is used to encourage companies to prioritize the welfare and rights of their employees, customers, and the broader community over short-term profits. Similarly, in international relations, the principles of justice and fairness are invoked to argue for global redistribution of resources and greater accountability from powerful nations.

However, Rawls' theory is not without its flaws and criticisms. Some argue that it overlooks cultural diversity, as it assumes a universally shared conception of justice. Others contend that the veil of ignorance oversimplifies the complexities of human decision-making, especially in an era characterized by globalization, migration, and rapidly evolving technologies.

As we navigate these debates, it is essential to recognize that Rawls' theory is not a static construct but rather a living, breathing philosophy that continues to shape and be shaped by the world around us. In the face of pressing issues like income inequality, social justice, and global governance, Rawls' ideas offer valuable insights and guideposts for those seeking to create a more just society.

Yet, as with any theory, it is crucial to approach Rawls' work with humility and an open mind, acknowledging its limitations while continuing to engage in constructive dialogue and debate. After all, the pursuit of justice is a never-ending journey, one that demands our constant vigilance, critical thinking, and willingness to adapt in the face of new challenges and realities.

text: it thrives today. Philosophers, for their part, have until recently occupied themselves mainly with apolitical fields such as logic, metaphysics and epistemology. On a philosophical level, however, Cold War philosophy has some obvious problems. Its ‘ethics’, for example, is not a traditional philosophical ethics at all. From Plato to the pragmatists, philosophical ethics has concerned the integration of the individual into a wider moral universe, whether divine (as in Platonic ethics) or social (as in the pragmatists). This is explicitly rejected by Cold War philosophy’s individualism and moral neutrality as regards to ends. Where Adam Smith had all sorts of arguments as to why greed was socially beneficial, Cold War ethics dispenses with them in favour of Gordon Gekko’s simple ‘Greed is good.’ Another problem with Cold War philosophy’s ethics concerns what I will call ‘disidentification’. Whatever I choose has at least one alternative; otherwise there would be no choice. But if I identify myself at the outset with any of my plurality of alternatives, I cannot choose any alternative to it; doing that would end my identity and be suicidal, physically or morally. Therefore, any alternative I consider in the course of making a rational decision is something I can walk away from and still be me. This is not an issue for rational choice theory, which concerns cases where my identity is not at stake, such as choosing which brand of toothpaste to buy, or (usually) which candidate to vote for. But when rational choice theory becomes Cold War philosophy, it applies to everything, and everything about me becomes a matter of choice. Free markets are wonderful tools for enhancing human life; so are MRIs: but both need proper installation and tending This in turn leads me to abandon my own identity, in the following way: suppose that what I am choosing is my religion, and that my alternatives are Catholicism and Hinduism. If I am already a Catholic, however, Hinduism cannot be a serious alternative, because one’s religion is (usually) part of one’s identity. If I am to choose between Catholicism and Hinduism, I must put both at a distance. I must ‘disidentify’ with them. And since Cold War philosophy bids us to take this stance on all things, at the limit the moral agent must be disidentified from everything, and can have no other fundamental identity than being a rational chooser, ie someone who first orders her preferences according to transitivity and completeness, and then opts for the highest utility. That is a pretty thin identity. Everyone has certain characteristics that they simply cannot or will not relinquish under any circumstances. What else is there to live for? The widespread success of rational choice theory, coupled with the problems of Cold War philosophy, suggests that the problem lies in what differentiates the two: Cold War philosophy’s claim, inherited from Allen, to universal, and indeed sole, validity

text: Let's start by disclosing that I grew up in New Orleans, went to school in Lafayette (home of the Ragin Cajuns), and REALLY know good New Orleans/cajun food. I grew up eating great po-boys, gumbo, red beans, and wouldn't consider eating any of these if not well prepared. That said, I can truly say that Jennifer and the Flavors of Louisiana gang make cajun food as good as any you'll find in New Orleans--SERIOUSLY. The po-boys are spot on (though the bread could be a little crustier) and the chicken & andouille gumbo is even better than mine--and, I assure you, that's DAMN GOOD. Frankly, I've never had better. The only thing that is not "as good as it gets" is the red beans and rice. They're good, but not quite the creamy "Washday Monday" staple that I grew up on. My cajun grandmother has Jennifer beat on that one, but not by much. But, if you want a special treat and want to taste something that kills all, get a sausage po-boy. Hot sausage or smoked sausage po-boys are a New Orleans staple and my favorite po-boys of all. But, Jennifer blows them all away with her home-made andouille. Simply put, I've never had better sausage--ever. It's the reason her gumbo is soooo good and her po-boy the best ever. I've eaten at Flavors many times, even though I live across town. Every time I go, I eat like a pig and bring some home to eat later. I have no affiliation with the restaurant, but wanted to post this as a review from someone who really knows New Orleans food and as a thank you to Jennifer and her crew for giving me a source for the best flavors in the World (not just Louisiana). As a matter of fact, I even overlook the fact that Jennifer is from Baton Rouge, instead of New Orleans or Lafayette and for not having any USL/UL Lafayette paraphernalia on the LSU/Saints wall. At least that is, so long as she keeps her promise to open another restaurant on the east side of town--that is, a lot closer to where I live. If you want the ONLY good cajun food in Phoenix, treat yourself to Flavors of Louisiana. For me, it's just like a visit to any of my favorite po-boy shops in the old neighborhoods. It really is that good.

She came back on the phone, informed me that she had a crew that was working close by and that she would ask one of the crew to swing by and pick up the screens for repair. text:
In the quietude of my study, I find solace amidst a sea of books - each one a testament to human thought, an exploration of our collective consciousness. Yet, it is the evolution of individualism, a recurring theme throughout these tomes, that captivates me most.

The concept of individualism has undergone metamorphoses as profound as the seasons themselves. From the self-reliant yeoman farmer of medieval Europe to the enlightened philosophe of the Age of Reason, from the rugged pioneer of the American frontier to the modern-day global citizen - we have traversed uncharted territories, each step shaping our understanding of ourselves and our place in the world.

In the 16th century, John Locke wrote about life, liberty, and property as inherent rights of every individual. Yet, fast forward to the 20th century, and we saw the rise of totalitarian regimes that sought to erase individuality, subsuming it within the collective. The irony was palpable; the very concept that Locke had championed became a weapon wielded against its proponents.

Fast-forward to our present era, and we find ourselves grappling with a paradoxical reality. On one hand, we celebrate individualism - the freedom to express oneself, to innovate, to create. On the other hand, we are increasingly aware of the interconnectedness of our lives, the need for collective action to address global challenges. We are at once individuals and part of a larger whole.

This tension between individuality and collectivity is not new. It echoes in the works of philosophers like Nietzsche and Marx, who wrestled with similar paradoxes. Yet, it seems that our current era has brought these paradoxes into sharper relief. We live in a world where the actions of one can have far-reaching consequences for many - a world where individualism must coexist with responsibility towards the collective.

As I gaze at the books that surround me, I am reminded of the words of Heraclitus: "You cannot step into the same river twice." The concept of individualism is no exception. It has evolved, adapted, and will continue to do so as we navigate the complexities of our world.

In the end, perhaps it is not about choosing between individualism and collectivism, but finding a balance - recognizing that we are both individuals and part of something greater than ourselves. As I close this essay, these thoughts linger in my mind, unresolved yet intriguing. The journey of understanding continues.

text: In one experiment from 1999, two groups of women took the same test, with only one group being told that the test is likely to show a gender difference. That group performed less well than the women who had not been told this. Three separate analyses of stereotype-threat research, however, have concluded that this effect is at best very weak and perhaps entirely nonexistent. Overall, the evidence simply does not support the idea that stereotypes exert a significant influence on the behaviour of the stereotyped in this way. Where does this leave de Beauvoir’s version of the existentialist idea that internalised gender stereotypes shape the person you become? Her theory does seem to predict that the association between mathematics and maleness should influence the mathematical achievements of women overall. But it does not predict that the stereotype has the kind of influence that the stereotype-threat experiments look for. De Beauvoir’s theory is not that being reminded of the stereotype will temporarily lower a woman’s mathematical abilities. Rather, it is that internalised stereotypes shape the goals we pursue in childhood, adolescence and adult life. De Beauvoir argues that, because we all internalise the stereotypes of gender from a young age, and because we develop interests and experience according to our expectations of ourselves and of one another, those stereotypes shape whom we become. The analyses that show stereotype threat to have little or no influence on behaviour are consistent with de Beauvoir’s theory of how stereotypes shape our lives. These analyses have indeed found a gender disparity at the highest levels of performance on mathematics tests. It is not at all clear why there is such a disparity. Could it be the effect of gender stereotypes shaping the abilities that people develop, as de Beauvoir suggests? Evidence that stereotypes do shape relevant abilities and experiences is provided by a recent study of 1,400 teachers in Italy and their students. This study measured the teachers’ gender stereotypes using a standard psychological experiment. The teachers were asked to respond to names and academic subjects as these appeared on a screen. They were to press one button for male names and another for female names. Those two buttons were also used to sort the academic subjects between sciences and humanities. Each teacher took two versions of the experiment, with the order of these determined randomly. In one version, one button was used for male names and sciences, and the other button for female names and humanities. In the other version, one button was used for male names and humanities, the other for female names and sciences. The experimenters measured the difference in response speed in the two versions. Title: A Delightful First Visit to The Cheesecake Factory during Happy Hour! 

Hey fellow foodies, I finally got around to trying The Cheesecake Factory during their happy hour and oh boy, it was a treat! Let me walk you through my experience.

I arrived at the restaurant just as the sun was setting, and I must say, the outdoor seating area is absolutely charming. It's perfect for those who enjoy dining al fresco while basking in the golden glow of twilight. Parking was a breeze too; there were plenty of spots available, which always puts my mind at ease when I'm out and about.

Once inside, I was greeted warmly by the staff and seated promptly. Happy hour offers some fantastic deals on drinks and appetizers, so my friend and I couldn't resist starting off with a couple of refreshing cocktails and the Bruschetta Classico. The drinks were perfectly mixed, not too sweet or strong, just right for our taste buds. The bruschetta was bursting with flavorfresh tomatoes, basil, and garlic mingling harmoniously on crunchy, toasted bread slices.

For my main course, I opted for the Thai Crunch Salad. It's a mouthwatering blend of romaine lettuce, grilled chicken, Mandarin oranges, almonds, wontons strips, and peanut dressinga zesty fusion of flavors that left me craving more! My friend chose the Cajun Jambalaya Pasta, which was a generous helping of shrimp, scallops, chicken, and Andouille sausage in a creamy sauce. It looked absolutely divine, and I'm already planning my next visit to give it a taste myself!

Now, let's talk about dessertthe pice de rsistance at The Cheesecake Factory. I couldn't resist the White Chocolate Raspberry Truffle cheesecake, and oh my goodness, it was heavenly! The creamy texture of the cheesecake paired perfectly with the tartness of the raspberries, and the white chocolate drizzle added a delightful touch.

To wrap up our meal, we took home leftovers for later, and I have to say, the packaging was top-notch. Everything stayed fresh and tasty until the next day, which is always a plus when it comes to takeout.

In conclusion, my first visit to The Cheesecake Factory during happy hour was nothing short of fantastic! From the friendly service, delicious food, and scenic outdoor seating, I can't wait to go back and try more of their offerings. If you haven't visited yet, do yourself a favor and give it a tryI promise you won't be disappointed!

*ExxonMobil, the American multinational oil and gas corporation, has agreed to be acquired by its European rival, Royal Dutch Shell, in a deal worth over $100 billion. This merger, set to close in 2023, will create the world's largest oil company, surpassing Saudi Aramco.*

This merger follows a trend of consolidation within the oil industry, driven by falling demand due to global efforts towards carbon reduction and renewable energy adoption.*

text:
**NEW YORK: JPMORGAN CHASE EXPANDS INTO RENEWABLE ENERGY SECTOR**

JPMorgan Chase, the multinational banking and financial services holding company, has announced its latest business venture: a foray into the renewable energy sector. The move comes as the global push towards green energy gathers pace.

According to sources within the company, JPMorgan Chase plans to invest heavily in wind and solar energy projects across the United States and Europe. The bank aims to provide long-term financing solutions to companies developing renewable energy infrastructure, helping them reduce their carbon footprint and transition towards a greener future.

"We see this as a strategic move for our business," said Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase, in a press statement. "The renewable energy sector is growing rapidly, and we want to be part of that growth. By providing financing solutions, we can help companies make the transition towards cleaner, greener energy."

JPMorgan Chase has not yet disclosed the exact amount it plans to invest in this new venture, but industry insiders estimate it could run into billions of dollars over the next decade. The bank has already been active in the renewable energy sector, providing financing for several large-scale solar and wind projects in recent years.

The move comes as many large corporations are under pressure to reduce their carbon emissions and become more sustainable. With increasing public concern about climate change and growing regulatory scrutiny, companies that fail to adapt risk facing reputational damage and financial penalties.

JPMorgan Chase is not the only major player in the financial sector to enter the renewable energy sector. Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and Morgan Stanley have also made significant investments in this area in recent years. However, with its vast resources and global reach, JPMorgan Chase's entry into the market could accelerate the transition towards a greener economy.

Only time will tell whether this strategic move by JPMorgan Chase pays off. But one thing is certain: the renewable energy sector is set to grow, and companies that invest in it now may reap significant rewards in the years to come.

Title: A Vegan Haven in the Heart of the Heatwave - Green Leaf Cafe, a Pleasant Surprise 

I must confess, I was initially apprehensive about this vegan cafe due to my affinity for all things greasy and meaty. But oh boy, did Green Leaf Cafe prove me wrong!

Tucked away in a quiet corner of the bustling downtown area, this small gem offers a serene escape from the relentless heatwave outside - a welcome respite indeed. The minimalist decor with its earthy tones adds to the tranquil ambiance, and the soft, ethereal music playing in the background made it feel like an oasis amidst the urban jungle.

On this particular day, I opted for a takeout order since the heat was unforgiving. The friendly staff handed me a sturdy, eco-friendly bag filled with my meal, ensuring everything stayed warm and fresh. As I opened the container, I marveled at the vibrant colors and enticing aromas emanating from the dishes.

I went for their signature Buddha Bowl, and let me tell you, it was a symphony of flavors! The quinoa base, generously laden with avocado, black beans, sweet potato, corn salsa, and tofu, was an explosion of textures that harmoniously melded together. The tangy lime dressing tied everything together, making every bite a delightful experience.

Now, about the packaging quality - it's worth mentioning because it was impressive! Despite the heat outside, my meal arrived unscathed and well-preserved, which speaks volumes about their thoughtfulness and attention to detail.

As I sat under the shade of a tree in the park, enjoying my delicious lunch, a quirky sight caught my eye - a group of hipsters engrossed in a heated discussion about the best vegan cheese brands. It was amusing to observe this passionate exchange, and it further cemented Green Leaf Cafe's status as a haven for plant-based enthusiasts.

In closing, I highly recommend Green Leaf Cafe to anyone seeking a delightful dining experience that caters to their taste buds without compromising on health or sustainability. Despite the noise level being slightly higher than ideal (perhaps due to the lively chatter of patrons), the exceptional food and inviting atmosphere more than make up for it.

So, my fellow meat-lovers, fear not! Green Leaf Cafe offers a tantalizing taste of the plant-based world that even skeptics like myself can appreciate. Give it a try, and you just might find yourself returning again and again.

These catastrophes had been made possible by the progress of science and technology, progress that had brought the very existence of our world into jeopardy. The advent of the nuclear age had transformed peace into the perpetual preparation for war, and, in an interesting reversal of Carl von Clausewitz’s dictum that war is a continuation of politics by other means, threatened to cancel politics altogether by ensuring the mutual destruction of the belligerents. Anders deplored tacticians’ and politicians’ collective blindness, their unconsciousness in seeking to instrumentalise the threat of annihilation for political purposes – a gamble that relied on their very ignorance. In the early 1940s, he wrote (translations from the French my own): None of us has a knowledge commensurate with what an atomic war could be … which means that, in this field, no one is competent and that the apocalypse is therefore, by essence, in the hands of the incompetent. The modern use of nuclear power had blurred the distinction between civilian and military, and rendered the possibility of disaster omnipresent. A threshold had been irredeemably crossed when mankind deliberately hung a sword over its head and created the conditions for its self-annihilation. For Anders, the disasters of the 20th century were simply the logical outcome of a pernicious process that had already been underway for many years, involving the gradual exclusion of mankind from all production processes – and, ultimately, from the world created by those processes. The real catastrophe in this regard, which Anders hoped to make ‘visible for the first time’, lay in the transformation of the human condition, a transformation that had become as naturalised and imperceptible as it was destructive. ‘The atom bomb,’ he argued, was ‘thus the ultimate emblem of an unearthly, unsettling and haunting force channelled by complex technological objects: it illuminates that the more “our” technological power grows, the smaller we become; the more unconditional and unlimited the capability of machines, the more conditional our existence; the more machines connect us by virtue of their very existence, the more we are also singled out as being expendable and inadequate.’ Günther Anders on his way to Hiroshima in 1958. As the will to deepen my attention for chess was dying, a part of myself was dying, too If we can’t concentrate, we will not be able to enjoy the state of consciousness – called flow – that is part of the chess experience. Flow was conceived and popularised by the Hungarian-American psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, and it is a mental state characterised by intense absorption, loss of self-consciousness, goal-related feedback from the world and an altered sense of time. Flow experiences are deeply rewarding, and they arise when our skill level and challenge level are optimally matched; too little challenge and we get bored, too much and we feel anxious. Chess is a great way to access flow, yet – as a lodestar for living – flow has limitations. Mostly, it describes a quality of consciousness, not a method for obtaining it. Ultimately, flow is not a virtue but a form of pleasure. While flow is a desirable state of mind, promoting it might not lead to desirable qualities of character; just as likely it could yield an atomised society of sophisticated hedonists with gaming addictions and virtual-reality sickness. Unlike attention or flow, concentration prompts an awareness of mood, even a commitment to meaning, and an appreciation for method. As a young player, I leant on various methods to deepen concentration, including taking long walks before games and listening to favourite music tracks. They worked mostly because the purpose of the concentration was never in doubt. However, in early December 2008, in a generic hotel room in Palma, Mallorca, I vividly remember trying to prepare for a game while feeling unusually displaced from myself. As generations of American football coaches have put it: ‘The will to win is not as important as the will to prepare to win.’ And I noticed that I had lost that. It felt as if the underlying motivational vector for concentration had collapsed; and, as the will to deepen my attention for chess was dying, a part of myself was dying, too. The will to sustain the identity that perpetuated my desire to win had gone, and I knew that it was time to concentrate on what the game symbolised rather than on the game itself. Chess is an arena where system meets psyche, and the world needs a way to make better sense of the nature of that encounter today. To make good chess moves, you need to see the whole position in all its reverberating dynamism. But you also need to see your own mind, and know its power and limitations; the personal is indeed political, and vice-versa. text: of development of a distinction between self and world. Another objection needs to be addressed before we credit too many animals, and only animals, with consciousness. This is the fact that much human behaviour depends on subconscious processing, of which we are often unaware. Our actions in the world rely to a surprising extent on stimuli we haven’t consciously noticed. Moreover, the experience of ‘volition’ has been found to follow our actions after a time-lag, rather than preceding them or being simultaneous with them. Some have interpreted this to mean that consciousness has no effect on behaviour and is purely ‘epiphenomenal’. Instead, maybe the brain collects and weighs current environmental stimuli and data from memory, computes the best behavioural option, and makes the choice for us by initiating an action. If consciousness is causally ineffective, the argument that animals need it for living is unavailable. Or perhaps what we need consciousness for is fully automated in them. However, these arguments do not diminish the case for consciousness being widespread in the animal kingdom. Despite the wonders of unconscious processing, it’s obvious that no human being can nourish herself, escape predation, reproduce, engage in a social life or find the way to a new destination when she is not conscious of a world outside her own body. Although there are impressive examples of ‘blindsight’ – where subjects with a damaged visual cortex can make visual discriminations better than a mere ‘guess’ – the blindsighted are not totally unconscious. Of course, sleeping and damaged brains are not doing their usual job of collecting, weighing and computing. But there is no reason to think that consciousness could be ‘subtracted’ from a brain that’s doing its usual job successfully. It is the organism with a working brain and consciousness that normally faces the challenges of the world. Consciousness is an evolutionary invention like wings or lungs. It is useful to us; it’s therefore most likely to be useful to other organisms with traits deeply homologous to ours. They share with us the difficulties of moving, probing the environment, remembering, predicting the future and coping with unforeseen challenges. If the same behavioural and cognitive criteria are applied as to much larger-brained vertebrates, then some insects are likely to qualify as conscious agents – with no less certainty than cats or Descartes’ dog.
