text: why we take protective measures in the present. Taken together, these pieces of evidence strongly suggest that the octopus can mentally represent time. But how might the octopus structure its representation of time? If embodied interactions with our surroundings ground human metaphors for time, one can only imagine the metaphors an octopus might use. Given it has equal competence interacting with objects behind or in front of its body, past events could be, metaphorically speaking, just as manipulable as future ones. Expressions like ‘let’s put the past behind us’ would be meaningless for the octopus. Moreover, given the octopus can change its visual perspective of its surroundings by swimming upward, all events in time – the past, present and future – could be surveyed from one metaphorical wide-view vantage point, allowing for the identification of patterns in events that occur across long periods time. If humans were to adopt octopus-inspired metaphors for time, our relationship to our personal past might become more meaningful. It would allow our past to be causally linked to the present. It might allow our attention to shift away from the culturally conditioned obsession with future-focused progress. It might allow historical ideas to be excavated and used as sources of innovation for everything from medicine to politics. And it might allow a more expansive conceptualisation of events that can be considered meaningful, all due to metaphors of time that are free from constraints. One way of considering how this might be possible is through research on the narratives we form about our lives. According to narrative psychologists, a stable sense of self depends on a unified narrative that structures life experiences into a coherent story, connected in time. Traumatic experiences can rupture a narrative, resulting in dissociative identity disorder in which a person adopts a different personality altogether following an experience that is too difficult to integrate. Treatment for dissociative identity disorder involves flexibly moving through the past and future – analogous to how the octopus moves through space – to first rebuild the individual’s disrupted past sense of self, and then shift focus toward a desired future self. Cultural theorists have written about dissociative identity disorder scaling up to the level of society, following periods of mass trauma such as war. In 1945, Carl Jung wrote that the central task of the German people was to confront the collective guilt associated with the atrocities their country had just committed during the Second World War in order to move forward, towards a more liberal future. Similarly, in the modern-day United States, writers like Ta-Nehisi Coates lay bare historic injustices committed against African Americans, arguing that the only way the country can address racial inequality now and move forward is to acknowledge and repair past wrongs. It would ditch the false dichotomy of dependence and independence and acknowledge that, in a complex human society, we are all necessarily interdependent. Above all, it would argue not that women should live more like men, but that everyone, regardless of gender, should live more like they want to. Title: A Pleasant Lunch Rush Experience at Hardware Haven! 

Let me share my recent adventure at this gem of a hardware store during the lunch rush. Now, I must admit, I don't often find myself here for tools or home improvement stuff, but their vegetarian/vegan offerings are worth writing about!

The parking situation is quite manageable, with ample spots available both on the street and in a nearby lot. You won't have to worry about circling around aimlessly searching for a place to park.

Upon entering, I was greeted by a warm and inviting ambiance that instantly made me feel at ease. The menu items were displayed prominently, and as a regular vegetarian, I was excited to see a variety of options to choose from. They offer everything from hearty grain bowls to flavorful curries, salads, and even vegan burgers!

On this particular day, I went for their signature chickpea curry, which was absolutely scrumptious. The aromatic blend of spices was delightfully fragrant, and the dish was served with a warm flatbread that perfectly complemented the richness of the curry.

As for cleanliness, this place truly outshone many other eateries I've visited. From the gleaming counters to the spotless tables, everything looked impeccable, making me feel confident in my choice to dine here.

The staff was attentive and friendly, ensuring that each customer felt valued. They were more than happy to explain the ingredients of each dish for those who might have dietary restrictions or preferences.

In a slight tangent, I should mention that while they don't serve alcohol, there's a charming little caf nearby where you can grab a refreshing drink before or after your meal. It's called The Coffee Bean and it's just a stone's throw away from the hardware store!

All in all, I highly recommend giving Hardware Haven a try if you find yourself in need of a hearty vegetarian/vegan meal during your lunch break. Their offerings are not only delicious but also varied enough to keep you coming back for more!

The balance between law and moral fairness, I find myself pondering these days. It's a delicate dance, isn't it? A dance that has shaped human behavior for centuries, perhaps millennia. But as I delve deeper into its intricacies, the more I realize the limits of language to fully capture its essence.

Take for instance those mercenaries, these 'contractors,' who have been a part of our history since the Middle Ages. They were there in northern Italy in 1377, serving under Pope Gregory XI, and they are here today, in the troubled corners of the world. They serve as a reminder of the complexity that surrounds law and moral fairness. On one hand, they provide security, fill a void left by official forces, and help maintain order. On the other, their actions can be questionable, causing harm to innocent civilians, and tarnishing the reputation of those who employ them.

I find myself in a quandary when I think about them. Are they villains, as some might label them? Or are they merely tools used by those in power to achieve their objectives? And if they are tools, then who is responsible for their actions? The employer or the employees themselves?

These questions lead me down a path of uncertainty, a place where the lines between law and moral fairness blur. It's a place where I struggle to find answers, where my mind refuses to be confined by the constraints of black-and-white thinking. I am reminded of Rousseau, who found his own solutions in unconventional ways - running away from his city at 16, seeking solace in books and music.

Like Rousseau, I too grapple with my own restlessness when faced with the complexities of law and moral fairness. I find myself wandering among the rocks and woods of thought, ideas swirling like a storm within me. But unlike him, I cannot seem to arrange these ideas into any sort of order in my head. They circle there obscurely, stirring emotions within me, causing my heart to palpitate, yet I cannot write a word, I must wait.

As I continue my exploration, I am reminded that the dance between law and moral fairness is not one with a clear resolution. It's a dance that will continue to shape human behavior, revealing both the beauty and the chaos of our existence. And as I stand on the sidelines, watching this dance unfold, I can only marvel at its complexity, its elegance, and its enduring mystery.

Title: A Disappointing Experience at Serenity Spa (2 Stars)

I've always been wary of massage spas, especially when I travel. So, when I stumbled upon Serenity Spa during my recent vacation, I decided to give it a try, albeit reluctantly. The pictures online were inviting, and the location right on the beach sounded perfect. Little did I know, this was going to be a massive letdown.

Upon arrival, I was taken aback by the crowded lobby. It wasn't just packed; it felt like a sardine can. I waited for what seemed like an eternity before being called. A friendly woman approached me and introduced herself as Sarah. She handed me a waiver to sign, which I did, still hoping for a redeeming experience.

I opted for the 60-minute Swedish massage, which promised relaxation and rejuvenation. I was shown to my room where I found the bed unmade and towels crumpled on the floor. I couldn't help but wonder if they had just left the previous client in a rush.

The therapist arrived, and I must admit, she was pleasant and professional. However, the massage itself lacked the soothing touch that I expected. It felt more like an aggressive workout than a relaxing spa treatment. My muscles were tensed up before I even began, and by the end of it, I was more stressed than before I arrived.

One saving grace was their takeout packaging. Although I couldn't bear to stay any longer, I picked up some lotions and creams for a future massage attempt. The containers were sturdy and sealed tightly, which is always appreciated when traveling with liquids.

The outdoor seating area looked inviting, but it was so crowded that there wasn't a single empty chair. I could see people enjoying their post-massage tea or coffee, but I had no desire to linger any longer.

In the end, the only thing I can say for certain is that I won't be returning to Serenity Spa. The experience was far from relaxing and rejuvenating, as advertised. If you're looking for a massage spa during your vacation, I would suggest giving this one a miss. There are better options out there, trust me on that.

SINGAPORE: In a surprise move, tech giant NVIDIA announced an aggressive cost-cutting plan today, aiming to save $1.4 billion by 2023. The Silicon Valley company, known for its graphics processing units and artificial intelligence technology, has been hit hard by the global semiconductor shortage and increased competition from rivals like AMD and Intel.

The cost-cutting measures include a workforce reduction of approximately 10%, or about 6,600 employees. This comes after a record year for NVIDIA, where revenue grew 53% to $16.7 billion in fiscal 2021. The company's CEO, Jensen Huang, emphasized that the layoffs would be focused on areas with redundancies and not in key growth sectors such as artificial intelligence and gaming.

The move is expected to have significant implications for the tech industry, as NVIDIA's actions could prompt its competitors to follow suit. The semiconductor shortage has already forced many companies to delay product launches or increase prices. Despite the layoffs, NVIDIA remains optimistic about its future. In a statement, Huang said, "We are making these changes now so we can bring more extraordinary products, services, and experiences to our customers when they're needed most." The company's share price rose 3% in after-hours trading following the announcement.

The exact details of how the savings will be achieved remain unclear. However, it is likely that NVIDIA will focus on streamlining its operations, reducing travel and office space costs, and reevaluating its supply chain management strategies. The company has already taken steps to improve efficiency, such as consolidating its manufacturing facilities in 2019.

As the tech industry continues to evolve and face challenges like the ongoing semiconductor shortage and increased competition, NVIDIA's cost-cutting plan serves as a reminder of the importance of adaptability and agility. When the impasse is so deeply intrenched, how is the cycle of coercion to be broken? The problem is no simpler when considered from the standpoint of women: Surrounded by trivialities from youth onwards, tied up with trinkets, chastened by coercion, and held back by an idleness that feels like comfort, how can, how should, a woman’s mind penetrate through this fourfold fog and find the light?This is no rhetorical question. Given that true progress cannot be made while one half of the human race lies in chains, Holst’s answer is that the fate of the human vocation lies in the ‘higher intellectual development’ of woman. Her text lays down two fundamental principles of woman’s higher development. First, higher development does not stem from an impure source, such as the vain desire to shine (the only motive that her male interlocutors seem to be able to imagine for a woman’s desire for higher learning). It stems ‘from the only true source: humankind’s duty to develop all its powers and to contribute to the wellbeing of the whole as an active member.’ Second, higher intellectual development is ‘entirely free’. A woman must not be confined to gendered elementary books but allowed to follow her genius wherever it leads. Holst contends that the crown of a woman’s higher intellectual development is ‘the philosophy of history’. The philosophy of history is neither a collection of facts nor a matter of a priori speculation. It consists in ‘tracing the course that human inclinations and passions have taken at all times’ and investigating ‘the course of the gradual development of the predispositions and capacities of the human mind from the first stage of culture to its zenith.’ The philosopher of history considers the conditions and institutions in which Enlightenment is advanced and frustrated, and thereby comes ‘to feel their value from a philosophical perspective’. Holst’s goal is not to reject existing institutions but to redefine the normative definition of woman within them Higher intellectual development thus enlivens women to their position as full participants in the human vocation, and thereby empowers them to understand why they presently take a subordinate social role. By grasping their historical standpoint at the zenith of culture, women discover that departing from the state of nature was always nature’s intention. Nature determined that human beings should not remain locked in violent contagion but ‘develop all of their powers’: We only want to be free to fulfil the first duty of humankind, which is to train all of one’s powers in the most beautiful harmony to the highest perfection. We share this duty equally, and it is as much our responsibility as it is that of men. Before we are man or woman, male or female

text:
In the grand tapestry of human civilization, art and political power have always been intricately intertwined, their threads entwining in a complex dance that shapes society, culture, and our very values. This relationship, though often elusive, can be observed through the prism of history, revealing patterns that compel us to reconsider our deeply ingrained beliefs about power, art, and ourselves.

Consider the murals adorning the walls of ancient Egypt or the frescoes found in the palaces of Italy's Medici family. These works were not mere decorations, but rather, statements of political might, visual narratives that legitimized the ruling class and reinforced their authority. The artist, in such cases, was not just a craftsperson, but an architect of power, wielding influence over the collective psyche through the manipulation of visual symbolism and narrative.

Fast-forward to the modern era, and we find ourselves in the midst of the Russian Revolution. Leon Trotsky, a revolutionary leader and founding member of the Soviet Union, once mused that art under socialism would become "not a mold but a steel, not a decoration but an armor." Here, we see the power dynamic reversed: the artist was no longer the servant of the ruling class but an agent of change, shaping society according to new ideals. In this instance, the relationship between art and political power forced us to reconsider our values, challenging the status quo and pushing us towards a new world order.

And yet, in our contemporary moment, we find ourselves grappling with yet another shift in the relationship between these two forces. Art has become weaponized, used as a tool of oppression by those in power, while also serving as a means of resistance for marginalized voices seeking to challenge systems of inequality and injustice.

Take, for example, the controversial exhibition at the Louvre, where a curator chose to display a series of works that glorified colonialism and imperialism. The exhibition was met with outrage, with critics arguing that it served to reinforce harmful stereotypes and whitewash the violence and exploitation inherent in these systems. In this instance, art was used as a tool of power, reinforcing dominant narratives and legitimizing oppressive structures.

Conversely, consider the role that art has played in the ongoing struggle for racial justice in America. From the powerful protest art created during the civil rights movement to the contemporary works addressing police brutality and systemic racism, art has served as a means of resistance, giving voice to those who have been silenced by power structures.

In exploring this relationship between art and political power, we are forced to confront uncomfortable truths about ourselves and our society. We must question whose narratives are being reinforced through art, whose stories are being told, and who is being marginalized or excluded. We must ask ourselves what role we, as consumers of art, play in this dynamic, and how we can use our agency to challenge harmful power structures.

In the end, the relationship between art and political power remains a complex and ever-evolving dance, one that forces us to constantly reconsider our values, our beliefs, and our place in the world. As we grapple with this dynamic, we must remain vigilant, asking ourselves who holds the power and whose stories are being told. Only then can we hope to create a more just and equitable society, one where art serves as a means of liberation rather than oppression.

text:
Desmond Tutu's legacy and South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) have left an indelible mark on the world. They challenged us to confront our darkest past, extend forgiveness where it seemed impossible, and strive for a future defined by compassion and justice. Yet, as I ponder these events, I can't help but feel a gnawing discomfort, a sense of unresolved tension that lingers in the shadows.

The TRC was hailed as a beacon of hope, a model for nations grappling with their own histories of violence and oppression. But as we celebrate its successes, we must also acknowledge its limitations. It offered amnesty to perpetrators in exchange for truth, a bargain that many find morally questionable. We ask: Is it justifiable to let those responsible for heinous acts go unpunished, simply because they confess? Can justice truly be served when there are no consequences for wrongdoing?

Moreover, the language of reconciliation can feel hollow and inadequate when faced with the raw pain of victims. Words like forgiveness and healing can ring false when uttered without the context of tangible change. Can we ever truly heal when the systems that allowed atrocities to occur remain intact? Can we forgive when the consequences of our actions continue to reverberate through generations?

These ethical dilemmas are not unique to South Africa or the TRC, but they serve as a stark reminder of the limits of language. Words can comfort, inspire, and bring people together, but they cannot undo the damage caused by violence and oppression. They cannot erase the pain etched into the hearts and minds of victims.

As we grapple with these questions, I find myself returning to Tutu's words: "If you are neutral in situations of injustice, you have chosen the side of the oppressor." In the face of these ethical dilemmas, it is our responsibility to choose justice, compassion, and healing. To do otherwise would be to side with those who perpetrate violence and oppression.

But how do we move forward? How do we reconcile when the pain is still so raw, when the systems that allowed atrocities to occur remain in place? These questions linger, unanswered, as I continue my journey of thought. The path ahead is uncertain, but I am reminded that it is not our job to find easy answers, but to keep asking difficult questions. To keep pushing for justice, compassion, and healing, even when the answers seem elusive.

text:
Dear Reader,

As we delve into the labyrinthine tapestry of knowledge transmission through time, let us first take a moment to appreciate the vast expanse of history we stand upon. From the oral traditions passed down from generation to generation, to the written records that have shaped our collective memory, the ways in which we share and preserve knowledge are as varied as the stories themselves.

Yet, amidst this rich diversity lies a common thread - the ever-present influence of technology. Consider, for instance, the transition from manuscripts to printed books, or the more recent shift towards digital media. Each leap forward has not only altered the landscape of knowledge dissemination but also shaped our perception of truth and reality.

Take, for example, the advent of video technology. Once reserved for the most significant moments in life, it is now ubiquitous, captured by everyone with a smartphone. The ghost stories that once captivated us, with their elusive spectral emanations, now seem quaint relics of a past era. Today, we are confronted with an excess of information, much of which lacks credibility or significance. This is not to say that skepticism is unwarranted, but rather, that it has become a reflexive response, often applied indiscriminately.

Consider the case of whale reproduction, about which we know so little despite their size and prominence in our oceans. The mysteries surrounding their mating habits serve as a stark reminder of the limitations of our understanding, even in the age of advanced technology. Yet, these gaps in knowledge coexist with a growing skepticism that threatens to undermine not only belief in the paranormal but also our empathy for those who suffer.

As we navigate this complex landscape of information, it is crucial that we remain vigilant and discerning. We must strive to separate the wheat from the chaff, the truth from the illusion. But let us not fall into the trap of nihilism, dismissing all that lies before us as mere deception. Instead, let us approach each discovery with curiosity, each challenge with resilience, and each mystery with a sense of wonder. For it is in these moments that we truly grasp the beauty and complexity of our shared history.

And so, dear reader, our journey through the annals of knowledge transmission continues. As we unravel the threads of time, let us remember to appreciate the richness of our past, embrace the challenges of our present, and look forward to the unknowns of our future.

text: strong>A celebration of Muhammad Ali's life continued on Wednesday in the late boxing champion's hometown of Louisville, where a daylong "I Am Ali" Festival passed his legend on to a new generation through stories and arts and crafts.</strongCraig Davidson, 45, of Louisville, brought his 15 year-old sons to the festival to pay their last respects."I grew up as a kid at their age watching his fights," Davidson said. "I knew about his stance during the Vietnam War. ... I just wanted them to know more about him."Ali, whose boxing prowess, showmanship and public opposition to the Vietnam War made him one of the world's most famous people in the turbulent 1960s and '70s, died last Friday of septic shock in an Arizona hospital. Before the festival started on Wednesday, fans gathered at the arena to claim tickets for the service. President Bill Clinton and comedian Billy Crystal. Actor Will Smith, who portrayed Ali in a 2001 movie, and former heavyweight champion Lennox Lewis will be among the pallbearers. The festival, at the Kentucky Center for the Performing Arts, was aimed at children, celebrating Ali's life with music, dance, and art and educational activities. Children had the chance to color butterfly and bee masks, an homage to Ali's famous quote about his boxing style, "Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee. The hands can't hit what the eyes can't see.""Today is about making sure they've got that connection to Muhammad Ali," Louisville Mayor Greg Fischer said in an interview on Wednesday. "As they grow up they can reflect back on this, so his legacy can live for multiple, multiple generations."The festival opened with the release of 30 Monarch butterflies into the air. Before Ali won the boxing heavyweight title or an Olympic gold medal, he was a young boy named Cassius Clay in Louisville who had his bicycle stolen, according to a story shared with festival attendees. When Ali turned to the police for help, he was introduced to the sport of boxing.

Title: A Fancy French Bistro Experience During the Dinner Rush - 3 Stars

Oh, I've been wanting to try this place for ages! Le Coeur de Paris, they call it. So, here I was, finally stepping into this quaint little bistro during the dinner rush.

First things first, the wait time wasn't too bad. A mere 15-minute wait before we were shown to our table, tucked away in a cozy corner. The ambiance was authentic, with soft jazz playing in the background and the faint scent of freshly baked baguettes wafting through the air.

All in all, Le Coeur de Paris is worth a visit if you're in the mood for some authentic French cuisine. Just be prepared for a bit of a wait during dinner rush hours and some inconsistent service. Title: A Disappointing Experience at the Overpriced Haven Cafe - 1 Star

text:
In the vast tapestry of life, we often find ourselves grappling with concepts that are as complex as they are intriguing - two such ideas being intelligence and wisdom. While they might seem interchangeable, a closer look reveals they are distinct entities, each with its unique nuances.

Intelligence, in its simplest form, can be seen as the ability to acquire and apply knowledge and skills. It's like a sharp tool that helps us navigate the world more efficiently, solve problems, and make sense of things around us. However, it often operates within a confined realm, focusing on logic and reason, sometimes devoid of emotional understanding or empathy.

On the other hand, wisdom is not merely about accumulating knowledge but about applying that knowledge in a way that promotes growth, harmony, and well-being. It's like a mature tree, its roots deeply entrenched in life experiences, with branches reaching out to encompass compassion, understanding, and foresight. Unlike intelligence, wisdom recognizes the interconnectedness of all things and takes into account not just what is logical but also what is ethical and meaningful.

But here's where it gets interesting. Isn't wisdom a form of intelligence, albeit a higher form? Doesn't wisdom require the application of intelligence to life's experiences, learning from them, and growing wiser as a result? This question stirs a sense of doubt within me. Is wisdom simply a refined version of intelligence, or does it hold an essence that transcends our understanding of intelligence altogether?

As I delve deeper into this exploration, I find myself drawn to the idea that wisdom might indeed be more than just a refined form of intelligence. It seems to encompass not just knowledge and skills but also a deep-seated understanding of the world, a sense of what truly matters, and a commitment to act accordingly. It's about knowing when to use one's intelligence and when to let go, when to stand firm and when to bend, when to speak and when to listen.

Yet, this thought is still a work in progress, an idea that needs more time, more reflection, and perhaps even more life experiences to fully understand. For now, it serves as a fascinating point of contemplation, a reminder of the depth and complexity of our existence, and a call to never stop learning and growing.

text: If you ask philosophically minded researchers – in the Anglophone world at least – why it is that science works, they will almost always point to the philosopher Karl Popper (1902-94) for vindication. Science, they explain, doesn’t presume to provide the final answer to any question, but contents itself with trying to disprove things. Science, so the Popperians claim, is an implacable machine for destroying falsehoods. Popper spent his youth in Vienna, among the liberal intelligentsia. His father was a lawyer and bibliophile, and an intimate of Sigmund Freud’s sister Rosa Graf. Popper’s early vocations draw him to music, cabinet making and educational philosophy, but he earned his doctorate in psychology from the University of Vienna in 1928. Realising that an academic post abroad offered escape from an increasingly antisemitic Austria (Popper’s grandparents were all Jewish, though he himself had been baptised into Lutheranism), he scrambled to write his first book. This was published as Logik der Forschung (1935), or The Logic of Scientific Discovery, and in it he put forward his method of falsification. The process of science, wrote Popper, was to conjecture a hypothesis and then attempt to falsify it. You must set up an experiment to try to prove your hypothesis wrong. If it is disproved, you must renounce it. Herein, said Popper, lies the great distinction between science and pseudoscience: the latter will try to protect itself from disproof by massaging its theory. But in science it is all or nothing, do or die. Karl Popper, 1987. Photo by Süddeutsche Zeitung/AlamyPopper warned scientists that, while experimental testing might get you nearer and nearer to the truth of your hypothesis via corroboration, you cannot and must not ever proclaim yourself correct. The logic of induction means that you’ll never collect the infinite mass of evidence necessary to be certain in all possible cases, so it’s better to consider the body of scientific knowledge not so much true as not-yet-disproved, or provisionally true. With his book in hand, Popper obtained a university position in New Zealand. From afar, he watched the fall of Austria to Nazism, and commenced work on a more political book, The Open Society and its Enemies (1945). Shortly after the war, he moved to the UK, where he remained for the rest of his life. For all its appealing simplicity, falsification was quickly demolished by philosophers, who showed that it was an untenable way of looking at science. In any real experimental set-up, they pointed out, it’s impossible to isolate a single hypothetical element for disproof. Yet for decades, Popperianism has nonetheless remained popular among scientists themselves, in spite of its potentially harmful side-effects. Why should this be? It was a group of biologists that gave Popper his first scientific hearing. How does the work of Ruth Bader Ginsburg embody the paradoxical nature of human beings  a constant striving for progress while grappling with our past? This question, much like the justice herself, defies simple answers and invites us to delve deeper into the complexities of gender equality and the human condition.

Ginsburg's legal battles for gender equality were not just about dismantling discriminatory laws; they were a testament to our capacity for change, our ability to learn from our mistakes, and our responsibility towards future generations. Her work, like a well-crafted time capsule, serves as a reminder of where we came from while propelling us forward, challenging us to question the status quo.

Take, for instance, her fight against gender discrimination in education. In the 1970s, she argued that denying equal educational opportunities to women was not only unjust but also economically irrational. Today, we see the fruits of her labor  women occupy positions of power and influence in fields once deemed off-limits. Yet, despite this progress, we are reminded of the past by persistent wage gaps, underrepresentation in leadership roles, and pervasive gender stereotypes.

This contradiction  the simultaneous strides forward and lingering vestiges of the past  is a mirror image of our own human nature. We are creatures who can conceive of a better tomorrow while being shackled by our history, who yearn for progress while grappling with tradition. Ginsburg's work in the courts serves as a stark reminder of this paradox.

In my younger days, I remember attending a protest rally, holding a placard that read 'Women's Rights are Human Rights'. It was a poignant moment, a testament to the power of collective action. Yet, as I looked around, I saw faces etched with lines of age and exhaustion  a testament to decades of struggle. This image has stayed with me, a symbol of the ongoing battle for gender equality, a battle that is as much about our past as it is about our future.

Ginsburg's work highlights this contradiction in human nature, inviting us to consider not just where we are going but also where we have come from. It challenges us to reexamine the narratives we construct about ourselves and our history, to question the assumptions that guide our actions, and to recognize the responsibility that comes with knowledge.

In the Aymara language, tomorrow is called 'q'ipi uru', or 'the day at my back'. In this sense, Ginsburg's work calls us to look behind us, not in regret but in reflection, to learn from our past and use it as a stepping stone towards a more equitable future. As we grapple with the complexities of gender equality, let us remember Ruth Bader Ginsburg  a testament to human resilience, a symbol of progress, and a reminder of the paradoxical nature of our own existence.

text:
As I ponder the intricate tapestry of human intelligence, I find myself drawn to its frayed edges, where our limitations seem to unravel. The mind's capacity for complexity and depth is undeniable, yet the boundaries of this capacity remain shrouded in mystery.

Consider the works of Samuel Beckett, a writer who masterfully dismantled narrative structure and coherent plotlines. In his 'trilogy', we find characters grappling with their own existence, their stories collapsing under the weight of self-doubt and uncertainty. Molloy's narration, for instance, is a labyrinthine maze of consciousness, a testament to the human mind's inability to fully grasp its own narrative.

This exploration of incoherence and powerlessness seems to echo Beckett's quietist experiments from the 1930s. Yet, it also hints at something deeper, a subtle acknowledgment that our understanding is inherently limited. Molloy, in his quest for meaning, ultimately finds none, but not because he has failed to find 'the answer'. Rather, it appears that there simply is no answer, no grand narrative that can explain the human experience.

This idea resonates with me, as I consider another example - the translations of classic works from one language to another. The process reveals the subjective nature of truth and meaning, and the impossibility of capturing the essence of a work in another language. Different translators, armed with different philosophies, produce vastly different results. Some prioritize fidelity to the original text, while others aim for a more poetic or intuitive interpretation.

Yet, even the most faithful translation cannot replicate the original, for the simple reason that languages are not interchangeable. They are unique, each with its own nuances and idiosyncrasies. This is evident in the case of Georges Perec's lipogrammatic novel 'La disparition', a work that, due to its absence of the letter 'e', defies straightforward translation.

As I contemplate these examples, I am left with more questions than answers. Is our intelligence limited by the constraints of language? Or is it the other way around - does language limit our intelligence? And if so, what does that mean for our pursuit of knowledge and understanding? These are questions that may never have definitive answers, yet they continue to provoke thought and inspire exploration.

As I delve deeper into these ideas, I find myself drawn back to Beckett's words: 'Im working with impotence, ignorance.' Perhaps our intelligence is not a source of power, but rather a tool for grappling with the unknown. A tool that, in its very limitations, reveals the boundless mysteries of the human experience.

In the labyrinth of life, we often find ourselves faced with difficult moral dilemmas - moments where the path forward seems fraught with conflicting choices and consequences. These crossroads can stir a tempest within us, as we grapple with the tension between our yearning for freedom and the desire to belong.

Take for instance, the story of Sophie Scholl, a German student who defied Hitler's regime during World War II, distributing anti-Nazi literature at her university. She knew that her actions could lead to imprisonment or worse, yet she chose to stand up for what she believed in, ultimately paying the ultimate price. In another time and place, Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat on a bus, igniting the Montgomery Bus Boycott and playing a pivotal role in the civil rights movement. Both women made choices that defied societal norms, choosing freedom over conformity, but at great personal cost.

Yet, there are times when we may choose to compromise our beliefs or values, not out of fear, but from a need to belong, to fit in with our peers, our community, our family. We may find ourselves questioning our decisions, wondering if we sold out, or if we stood firm, would the world have collapsed around us?

This tension between freedom and belonging is not just an individual struggle, but a societal one as well. In our increasingly interconnected world, we are faced with complex moral dilemmas on a global scale. Climate change, for example, requires immediate action, yet it seems almost insurmountable due to the collective actions of billions of people. The need for unity and cooperation is paramount, but so too is the freedom to make choices that promote sustainability and preserve our planet for future generations.

As I ponder these questions, I can't help but wonder if we are heading towards a tipping point, where our need to belong will outweigh our desire for freedom, or if we will find a way to balance the two, creating a world that is both unified and free. It's a daunting task, but one that I believe humanity is capable of achieving, if only we can find the courage to stand up, to speak out, and to make the difficult choices that our moral compasses guide us towards.

In this ever-evolving dance between freedom and belonging, I find myself questioning my own convictions, my own beliefs, and my own actions. Am I doing enough? Could I do more? These questions linger in the back of my mind, serving as a constant reminder that we must never lose sight of our values, even when the path forward seems uncertain or fraught with danger.

As I continue on this journey, I find solace in the knowledge that I am not alone, that there are countless others out there who share my concerns, my hopes, and my dreams for a better world. Title: A Delightful Evening at The Food Truck Park  - Despite a Few Hiccups

I've been meaning to check out this food truck park for quite some time now, and I finally got the chance last weekend! It was a lively atmosphere with so many trucks to choose from. Don't let me start on the noise level though; it was a bit too much for my sensitive ears, especially when trying to decide what to eat 

My dietary restrictions meant I had to be selective about where to eat. Luckily, I found one truck that offered gluten-free options! The line wasn't too long, and the staff were friendly and accommodating. I ordered a chicken wrap with extra veggies (I'm all about those greens) and asked for it to be packed carefully since I don't want any cross-contamination. They wrapped it up neatly, and off we went!

The food was fantastic; the chicken was tender, and the vegetables were crisp and fresh. The only downside was that it got a little cold during our drive home (it's always better when it's hot and fresh out of the truck!). I also noticed there wasn't much seating available, so we ended up eating in the car.

One thing that really impressed me was the parking situation. The lot was well-lit, clean, and easy to navigate. There were signs directing us to accessible spots, which is always a plus for someone like me who needs them.

I'd be remiss if I didn't mention the interaction I had with one of the staff members. This woman was so kind and patient as she explained the gluten-free options available and made sure everything was prepared correctly. It really made my dining experience that much better!

All in all, I'd give this food truck park 4 stars. The noise level could be improved, and I wish there were more seating options. However, the food was delicious, the parking situation was great, and the staff were super friendly and accommodating. I'll definitely be back to try out some of the other trucks! If you have dietary restrictions like mine, don't hesitate to give this place a try.

Fukuji’s wife called to him, over her shoulder: ‘I am married now, to this man.’‘But don’t you love your children?’ Fukuji cried out in reply. His wife paused at that, and began to sob. Fukuji looked sadly at his feet for a moment, not knowing what more to say. When he looked up, the woman and the man had drifted away. From Tōno Monogatari or Legends of Tōno (1910) by Kunio Yanagita, author’s translation This is a true story. Or so the man who wrote it down wanted his readers to believe. John had no prior musical training, yet through persistent practice and determination, he was able to master complex pieces and even perform in public. This transformation is a testament to neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to rewire itself and form new connections to accommodate new skills and knowledge.

But what about those who suffer from traumatic experiences? Can they also harness the power of neuroplasticity for healing? The story of Christine, a woman who was sexually assaulted as a teenager, offers a compelling perspective. After years of silence, she decided to speak out, to share her story with the world. Through therapy and self-reflection, she was able to reframe her experience, to find meaning in the trauma, and to heal. This is another manifestation of neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to adapt and change in response to challenge and adversity.

Yet, the story of neuroplasticity is not a simple one. It is a complex dance between nature and nurture, between genetics and environment. For instance, research has shown that while anyone can learn a new skill, some people are naturally better at it than others. This is due in part to differences in brain structure and function, differences that are influenced by both genes and environment.

Moreover, the impact of neuroplasticity on human potential is not uniform. It is shaped by cultural narratives, socio-economic factors, and individual experiences. For instance, a woman who has been socialized to believe that she is less capable than her male counterparts may find it more difficult to harness the power of neuroplasticity for learning or healing. Similarly, a person living in poverty may have limited access to resources and opportunities that could help them maximize their brain's plasticity.

In conclusion, while neuroplasticity offers great promise for human potential, it is not a panacea. It is a complex and nuanced process that is influenced by a multitude of factors. As we continue to explore the mysteries of the human brain, it is crucial that we consider these complexities, that we strive to understand how neuroplasticity can be harnessed for good, and how it can be used to overcome adversity and promote healing.

But I digress. The story of neuroplasticity is still unfolding, and there are many more threads to explore. Perhaps one day, we will have a more complete tapestry, a more comprehensive understanding of this remarkable ability that lies within each of us. Until then, let us continue to unravel the mysteries of the brain, to delve deeper into the complexities of neuroplasticity and its impact on human potential.

text: About 4,200 years ago, the area we now call southern Iraq was rocked by revolts. The once-independent Sumerian city states had been brought under one rule by the legendary king Sargon of Akkad. Over the course of what modern historians call the Old Akkadian period, the reign of Sargon and his successors reshaped the newly conquered cities in countless ways: old nobles were demoted and new men brought to power, old enemies were defeated and new standards of statecraft imposed. The Sumerian world grew much bigger and richer, but also more unstable. Discontent with the new empire festered, provoking a steady stream of uprisings as the cities attempted to regain their independence. One such revolt is depicted in a fascinating poem known as ‘The Exaltation of Inana’. Besides being a poetic masterpiece in its own right, ‘The Exaltation’ bears the distinction of being the first known work of literature that was attributed to an author whom we can identify in the historical record, rather than to an anonymous tradition or a fictional narrator. The narrator of the poem is Enheduana, the high priestess of the city of Ur and the daughter of Sargon. According to ‘The Exaltation’, she was cast into exile by one of the many revolts that plagued the Old Akkadian Empire. Tablets inscribed with The Exaltation of Inana in three parts. Old Babylonian period, c1750 BCE. Courtesy of the Yale Babylonian Collection We do not know for sure whether the poem was written by the historical Enheduana herself, as a literary retelling of a real event, or by a later poet writing in her name, in the ancient version of a historical fiction that was meant to celebrate the famous high priestess. What we do know is that the poem conveys a sense of what it would have been like to live through a period of profound turbulence, whether as a personal account or an echo preserved in the cultural memory of later periods. That is one reason why the works attributed to Enheduana – which, besides ‘The Exaltation’, comprise four other poems – continue to speak to us, millennia after their composition. As the world grows increasingly unstable, we have much to learn from this ancient priestess. The poems do not merely register the reality of an historic upheaval – they go one step further by turning that instability into a cosmic insight, an occasion to reflect on what the world is really like. They contain, compressed within their often-cryptic verses, the germ of an ancient philosophy of change. If the poems seek to turn radical change from a transient political phenomenon into a universal principle, they do so primarily by exalting the goddess Inana, better known by her Babylonian and Assyrian name Ishtar. Title: Overhyped, Underwhelming Juice Bar Experience

I usually stick to my greasy burgers and fries, but curiosity got the best of me on this busy weekend, so I decided to give this trendy juice bar a try. Let's just say it wasn't worth it.

First off, the noise level was deafening. With all the clinking glasses, shouting conversations, and that never-ending playlist blasting in the background, it felt like being at a rock concert instead of enjoying a peaceful drink. I had to shout my order across the counter, which was less than pleasant.

I opted for their "Super Detox" juice, hoping for something miraculous. The color was vibrant, but the taste... well, let's just say it tasted like someone blended a handful of grass and threw in some fruit for good measure. It wasn't as bad as drinking dirt, but close.

Now, I understand that outdoor seating can be a plus, but their so-called "outdoor lounge" was more like a smoker's den. The aroma of cigarettes overpowered the fruity scent of the juice, ruining any chance of enjoying my drink in peace.

As for the staff, I had a less than pleasant interaction with one of them. I asked if they could refill my water bottle and was met with a cold, dismissive glance before being told in a tone that wouldn't have been out of place in a military boot camp: "No. Get your own." Needless to say, it left a sour taste in my mouth.

All in all, I won't be rushing back here any time soon. I guess I'll stick to my greasy burgers and fries after all. If you're looking for an overpriced, noisy, and pretentious juice bar experience, this is the place for you. But if you value your taste buds and eardrums, give it a miss.

But somehow it all blended pretty well amid the sprawling outdoor space at Tunisia’s World Social Forum, an annual gathering of international civil society organisations. This was the forum’s first meeting in an Arab country — appropriately enough, the Arab country that kicked off a chain of revolutions two years ago. The choir, a group of men chained together and dressed in prison uniforms, were singing about Tunisia’s former political prisoners — more than 3,000 at recent count, incarcerated and horribly abused by the former regime; now released, but adrift and unsupported, they are struggling to re-enter society. The rappers, mostly Tunisian students, were meanwhile spitting rhymes about the economy, unity, employment, and periodically rousing the crowd to chants of: ‘Work! Freedom! Dignity!’ These were the demands of the Tunisian revolution that toppled the Western-backed dictator Zine El Abidine Ben Ali in January 2011. The choking regime might be gone, but those three demands are still unmet. text:
Dear Reader,

Ever pondered upon the profound influence of philosophical theories on our everyday behavior? Let's embark on a curious exploration of this intricate relationship.

Take, for instance, the concept of 'free will' - a cornerstone of Western philosophy since antiquity. It questions whether we are truly in control of our actions or if an external force guides us. This notion is subtly woven into our societal fabric, shaping how we perceive responsibility and accountability. Consider the modern debate on personal choices regarding lifestyle, such as diet, exercise, or relationships - do we consciously decide these paths, or are they influenced by factors beyond our control?

Next, let's delve into the realms of existentialism, a philosophy that grapples with the meaning and purpose of life. In contemporary times, we find echoes of this in the rise of 'self-help' culture and the pursuit of personal growth. We constantly seek to find our 'purpose,' striving to create meaningful lives amidst the chaos of modern existence.

Now, let's shift gears to the concept of utilitarianism - the belief that the best action is the one that maximizes overall happiness or pleasure. This theory can be seen in our societal decisions, from policy-making to personal choices. For example, when we decide to donate to charity, invest in renewable energy, or even choose a career path, are we not striving to bring about the greatest good for the greatest number?

Lastly, consider the idea of existential risk - the possibility of human extinction due to technology run amok. This concept is deeply rooted in contemporary debates on artificial intelligence, climate change, and nuclear proliferation. Fearful of these threats, we strive to mitigate risks and safeguard our future as a species.

Yet, the interplay between philosophy and human behavior is not always straightforward or easy to discern. Some ideas seep into our collective consciousness unnoticed, shaping our actions in ways we may not even realize. As we continue to evolve, it's fascinating to consider how our philosophical foundations will shape our future behavior - and perhaps, one day, our very existence.

text: Arriving at the chess board is like entering an eagerly anticipated party. All my old friends are there: the royal couple, their associates, the reassuringly straight lines of noble infantry. I adjust them, ensuring that they are optimally located in the centre of their starting squares, an anxious fidgeting and tactile caress. I know these pieces, and care about them. They are my responsibility. And I’m grateful to my opponent for obliging me to treat them well on pain of death. In many ways, I owe chess everything. Since the age of five, the game has been a source of friendship, refuge and growth, and I have been a grandmaster for 20 years. The lifelong title is the highest awarded to chess players, and it is based on achieving three qualifying norms in international events that are often peak performances, combined with an international rating reflecting a consistently high level of play – all validated by FIDE, the world chess federation. There are about 1,500 grandmasters in the world. At my peak, I was just outside the world top 100, and I feel some gentle regret at not climbing even higher, but I knew there were limits. Even in the absence of a plan A for my life, chess always felt like plan B, mostly because I couldn’t imagine surrendering myself to competitive ambition. I have not trained or played with serious professional intent for more than a decade, and while my mind remains charmed by the game, my soul feels free of it. In recent years, I have worked in academic and public policy contexts, attempting to integrate our understanding of complex societal challenges with our inner lives, while also looking after my two sons. I miss many things about not being an active player. I miss the feeling of strength, power and dignity that comes with making good decisions under pressure. I miss the clarity of purpose experienced at each moment of each game, the lucky escapes from defeat, and the thrill of the chase towards victory. But, most of all, I miss the experience of concentration. I can still concentrate, of course, but not with the same reliability and intensity that a life of professional chess affords. In fact, from a distance, chess looks to me suspiciously like a socially permissible pretext to concentrate for several hours at a time. In The Island from the Day Before (1994), Umberto Eco composes a love letter that includes the line: ‘[O]nly in your prison does [my heart] enjoy the most sublime of freedoms’ – that could be said of chess, too, and the experience of concentration is what makes it possible. I believe concentration is a defining feature of a fulfilling life, a necessary habit of mind for a viable civilisation, and that chess can teach us more about what concentration really means. text: of self-transformation began with a letter to his father in 1925. A year earlier, his parents had persuaded him to break off his engagement to a Swiss woman. She wasn’t English, she wasn’t a scientist, hence she wasn’t right. Sent off, in compensation, to the Galápagos Islands to repeat the fieldwork that Charles Darwin had done a century before, Bateson found the inhabitants far more interesting than the fauna. Pleading with his father not to be ‘terribly disappointed’, he wrote to tell him he needed to ‘break with ordinary impersonal science’. He would study people, anthropology. Ten years later, on the opening page of Naven, he observed that, while the artist might capture the whole emotional tone or ethos of a society in a few pages, anthropologists restricted themselves to the mechanics of its organisation. But he would seek to link organisation and ethos. As a scientist, he would invade the territory of art; or do science the way an artist might. Thus Bateson, responding to the conflicting attitudes of his parents, found himself at the beginning of the movement that questioned the limitations of a traditional scientific approach, in particular the notion that the scientific observer is entirely separate from the objects of his or her study. But such thinking was ingrained in Western civilisation. ‘There are times,’ he wryly remarked ‘when I catch myself believing there is something which is separate from something else.’ Naven takes its title from a series of bizarre rituals performed by the Iatmul people of New Guinea, which bewildered Bateson for some years. He had gone to the South Paci­fic in 1927 intent on exploring the relationship between individuality and culture, eager to undermine the dominant British anthropological model of the time, formu­lated by Alfred Radcliffe-Brown, that saw human societies very much as biological organisms, with the behaviour of the individual parts entirely explicable in terms of the needs of the whole. Bateson found this rigid determinism oppressive and unconvincing, in part because it didn’t explain conflict between individuals, and again because it ignored the realm of aesthetics. The opposing behaviour patterns encouraged escalating competition between the men and women However, once among the tribes of the area, he had no idea what to observe or how to proceed. Other anthropologists had developed elaborate questionnaires and aggressive methods of interrogation, but Bateson had a horror of all interference in other people’s lives, and believed that questionnaires implied that one already knew what the important questions were. In 1929, he settled on the Sepik River, alone, with the Iatmul, a people who had only recently abandoned cannibalism and continued to attribute everything that went wrong to ‘a lack of homicide’, moulding their whole culture on ‘a continual emphasis upon the spectacular’. text: Kindle hardware design team was honing in on the Platonic universal reading container. If the pile of unread books on the bedside table is a graveyard of good intentions, the list of unread books on a Kindle is a black hole of fleeting intentions But in the past two years, something unexpected happened: I lost the faith. Gradually at first and then undeniably, I stopped buying digital books. I realised this only a few months ago, when taking stock of my library, both digital and physical. Physical books – most of all, works of literary fiction – I continue to acquire voraciously. I split my time between New York and Tokyo, and know that with each New York trip I’ll pick up a dozen or more volumes from bookstores or friends. My favourite gifts, to give and to receive, are still physical books. The allure of the curated front tables at McNally Jackson or Three Lives and Company is too much to resist. The great irony, of course, is that I’ve never read more digitally in my life. Each day, I spend hours reading on my iPhone – news articles, blog posts and essays. Short to mid-length content feels indigenous to the size, resolution and use cases of smartphones, and many online publications (such as this very site) display their content with beautiful typography and layouts that render consistently on any computer, tablet or smartphone. Phones also allow us to share articles with minimal effort. The easy romance between our smartphones and short-to-mid-length articles and video is part of the reason why venture capitalists have poured hundreds of millions of dollars into New York publishing upstarts such as Vox, Vice and Buzzfeed. The smartphone coupled with the open web creates a near-perfect container for distributing journalism at a grand scale. But what of digital books? What accounts for my unconscious migration back to print? Once bought by a reader, a book moves through a routine. It is read and underlined, dog-eared and scuffed and, most importantly, reread. To read a book once is to know it in passing. To read it over and over is to become confidants. The relationship between a reader and a book is measured not in hours or minutes but, ideally, in months and years. Title: A Soothing Sojourn at Serenity Sanctuary, Yet Vegetarian Options Could Use Some Attention

I found myself in need of some serious relaxation after a long week, and Serenity Sanctuary seemed like the perfect spot to unwind. The exterior was inviting, with lush greenery and calming water features that instantly set a peaceful tone.

Upon arrival, I was warmly greeted by the receptionist who swiftly checked me in. A brief tour of the facilities followed, showcasing their array of treatment rooms, steam room, sauna, and relaxation areas. The cleanliness was commendable, but a minor gripe - the wait time for my appointment was extended due to unexpected schedule changes.

Once settled into my room, I was pleasantly surprised by the tranquil atmosphere. Soft music played in the background, dimmed lights cast a gentle glow, and aromatic candles filled the air with soothing scents. My therapist arrived promptly and proceeded to provide an excellent massage, leaving me feeling completely rejuvenated.

Now for the elephant in the room - or should I say, the lack thereof in their menu. As a vegetarian, my dining options were limited to a few items marked with a "V." While the food was decent, I couldn't help but feel disappointed that a spa catering to relaxation and wellness overlooked such a crucial aspect as diverse dietary needs.

Speaking of overlooked aspects, their bathroom could use some attention. Though clean, the vanity area felt cramped, and the stocked amenities were a bit lackluster compared to other establishments I've visited. It's the small details that can make all the difference in creating an unforgettable experience.

On a lighter note, I must commend their pool area. My sister and mother enjoyed lounging by the water on our subsequent visit, taking advantage of the comfortable seating and picturesque views. The small patio outside our room was a delightful addition, providing a peaceful spot to unwind before or after treatments.

In conclusion, Serenity Sanctuary offers a tranquil escape from the hustle and bustle of everyday life. However, for vegetarians like myself, I encourage them to expand their menu options to better accommodate diverse diets. With a few adjustments, this spa has the potential to be truly exceptional. Until then, I'll be keeping an eye out for other establishments that cater to my needs while providing top-notch relaxation services.

text:
The enduring influence of Michel Foucault on power and knowledge continues to resonate profoundly in our contemporary discourse, particularly when considering the intersections between theory and lived experience. His work, marked by a nuanced exploration of power dynamics, has become a cornerstone for understanding how structures of domination shape individual identities and societal norms.

Consider, for instance, the realm of queer and crip theory. These movements, born out of marginalization and resistance, have sought to challenge traditional notions of ability and sexuality. Yet, they paradoxically reinforce their own subversive status by adhering rigidly to it. The question I often ponder is: if these communities strive for outsider clout, are they truly using it to effect transformative change?

Take the case of disability rights activism, which emerged in the 1960s with significant legal advancements such as the Americans with Disabilities Act in 1990. Yet, since then, progress has seemed stagnant at best, with only minor strides made towards greater accessibility and inclusivity. This is reminiscent of Bone's argument in 'Trapped Behind the Glass' (2017) that while identity politics may serve as a starting point for radical change, it must not be the end goal if we wish to bring about systemic transformation.

This dilemma echoes Foucault's concept of power-knowledge: the idea that knowledge is always entangled in power dynamics, and those who wield knowledge hold power. For queer and crip activists, their marginal status grants them a unique perspective, but it also imprisons them within those very structures they seek to dismantle. Their politics of transgression, while shocking and subversive, ultimately effect minor change that requires maintenance rather than the radical upheaval demanded by systemic inequality.

I find myself reflecting on this conundrum as I scroll through social media, where smiling faces abound. The ubiquity of emojis, particularly the 'smile with tears of joy,' underscores our cultural affinity for positivity and unity. Yet, the recent pandemic has forced us to mask these smiles, forcing us to confront the insincerity hidden beneath. Could this be a metaphor for the superficial change wrought by identity politics?

As I write this, I can't help but recall an incident from my childhood, when I first learned about systemic inequalities. My parents, who immigrated to America seeking better opportunities, struggled to navigate the complexities of a new country. They faced discrimination, were overlooked for promotions, and endured countless microaggressions. Yet, they persevered, instilling in me a deep appreciation for the power dynamics at play in our society.

The ongoing discussion around Foucault's work is not merely an academic exercise but a call to action. We must critically examine how power and knowledge intersect, pushing beyond identity politics and working towards systemic transformation. Only then can we dismantle the very structures that perpetuate marginalization and bring about genuine change.

On account of the fact that you have to actually do the good thing to get the praise – and equally, you have to actually do the bad thing to get the punishment – people are more likely to follow through on their good, and not act upon their bad, intentions. This is a highly welcome feature of social existence, all things considered. The ‘irregularity’ of our sentiments encourages us to respect the sanctity of other persons Finally, and perhaps most interestingly of all, Smith thought that there was a special effect of the ‘irregularity’ of our sentiments: it predisposed us to be careful around other people. One last example: imagine you are walking along a path above a cliff, and you accidentally dislodge a boulder, which crashes down and kills the rock-climber below. You didn’t mean to do this – it was an accident! But the fact that you did do it matters enormously. You will be blamed by others, and will likely blame yourself too. (‘Why didn’t I look where I was going?!’) But the fact that we feel ourselves responsible even for the things that we didn’t mean to do is, Smith thinks, a very useful and desirable state of affairs, insofar as it encourages us to take care when we are acting in ways that could (inadvertently) harm others. Precisely because you know you’ll rightly be held responsible for the death of people below you, even if you only accidentally knock a rock onto them, you’re more inclined to take care where you tread when you go for a clifftop stroll. As Smith put it, the ‘irregularity’ of our sentiments in this regard encourages us to respect the sanctity of other persons: The happiness of every innocent man is, in the same manner, rendered holy, consecrated, and hedged round against the approach of every other man; not to be wantonly trod upon, not even to be, in any respect, ignorantly and involuntarily violated, without requiring some expiation, some atonement in proportion to the greatness of such undesigned violation. What has this got to do with the assassin’s knife, the guitar used by Hendrix, or the films that Spacey starred in? Like Smith, I cannot explain why our psychologies tend to transfer the guilt of an agent, or the history of what an object was used for, on to the past or future status of a thing itself. Title: A Frozen Brunch Disappointment - Just Don't Go! 

Let me start by saying I consider myself a grease enthusiast, having spent countless chilly mornings tucked away in diners and cafes across the country. So when I stumbled upon this popular brunch spot on a particularly frigid day, I had high hopes. Boy, was I wrong.

As we stepped inside, the quirky background music played softly, and the warm aroma of pancakes wafted through the air. It all seemed promising, but little did I know that the chilly vibe wouldn't fade away any time soon. My partner and I were seated near a drafty window, which made our frosty fingers grip our menus even tighter.

The menu boasted an array of unique dishes, catering to various dietary preferences - except mine, that is. As someone with a gluten allergy, I was disappointed to find only one option that didn't include bread or pasta. I reluctantly settled for the 'egg white scramble with spinach and feta' (a dish that couldn't have been further from comforting on a cold day).

My partner, however, went all out with their avocado toast and a bottomless coffee. I watched as they took a bite of their meal, only to see a look of disappointment cross their face. The avocado was mushy, the toast hard as a rock, and the coffee tasted burnt.

Feeling sympathetic, I dug into my scramble, hoping for some flavorful rescue from this icy disaster. Instead, I got a plate full of blandness - egg whites with spinach and feta that didn't even come close to mingling well together. And the coffee? Don't get me started on the coffee. It was as cold as the wind outside, and tasted like it had been sitting on the burner for hours.

As we left the restaurant, our fingers still numb from the freezing air, I couldn't help but think about how many other patrons had been fooled by this so-called popular brunch spot. So, if you value warmth in your food and atmosphere, skip this place and find solace elsewhere. Trust me; your taste buds will thank you!

text: Most of the time, getting a marriage licence is an administrative transaction like any other. The piece of paper, the legality of matrimony, is an errand, a bureaucratic checkmark. The real celebration comes later. The everyday realities of marriage arrive later still. But what happens when that order is reversed? When you’ve had the ceremony and the long years together but no piece of paper, because it wasn’t possible, because your society didn’t allow it? What happens when, suddenly, that sanction is overturned? On a cold, rainy night in Seattle last December, I saw such a quiet revolution in action. Several hundred couples — some of them couples who had already spent decades together, who had been ‘domestic partners’ for years, who had long since held grand but not legal weddings before their friends and families — lined up for the part of the matrimonial process that most couples do first: the marriage licence. The result, that night, was a strange mixture of beauty and bureaucracy. Couples kissed and cried as they spent hours in line and filled out paperwork. They carried flowers, tiaras, and signs declaring their years together — as well as lawn chairs, blankets, and thermos flasks. To pass the hours, they shared their stories of meeting each other and falling in love, reflecting with wonder or anger on the time when they thought this day would never come. Some could still hardly believe that, in just a few hours, at 12:01 am, Washington’s law extending the right of marriage to same-sex couples would go into effect. On one side it read, ‘20 years together.’ On the other: ‘Just married’ The law had actually passed the state legislature more than nine months earlier, but opponents tried to block it by demanding it be put to a popular vote. No state in the nation had ever directly voted in favor of gay marriage — in fact, citizens of 30 states have voted to write bans on same-sex marriage into their very constitutions. Washington’s opponents of gay marriage felt confident that the people would strike this law down, too. But Americans are rapidly changing their minds about gay marriage. In the decade prior to Washington’s law, according to the Pew Research Center, public opinion on legalising same-sex marriage moved from a majority in opposition (54 per cent) and a minority in support (35 per cent) to roughly equal numbers for and against. In 2012, support for marriage equality took a clear lead for the first time, with 48 per cent in favour, and 43 per cent against. And on November 6, voters chose to legalise same-sex marriage not only in Washington, but in Maine and Maryland as well. text: in the State of Nature. You all have an interest in engaging in what philosophers call an epistemic division of labour (after epistēmē, ancient Greek for ‘knowledge’) whereby information is pooled. This is where, according to Edward Craig’s Knowledge and the State of Nature (1990), the concept of knowledge emerges to fill a need. It serves to flag good informants. This is no mean feat, because the property of being a good informant is not immediately observable like the property of being tall or dark-haired. There’s an art to identifying good informants on a given question. The concept of knowledge therefore needs to track a variety of indicator properties – properties that are observable and that correlate well with and hence indicate the property of being a good informant on a given issue. For example, being able to offer a justification for one’s opinion might serve as such an indicator property; or having a good track record on this type of issue; or standing in the right causal relation to the state of affairs in question (by having looked at the berries earlier today, for instance). The concept of knowledge you need is one that tracks these and other properties that reliably indicate a good informant. According to Craig’s pragmatic genealogy, then, the concept of knowledge is rooted in practical needs: its salient contribution to life in the State of Nature is that it renders you sensitive to the presence of people who know something you’re trying to find out. And as the genealogical story shows, this is a need deriving from needs so basic that we today are likely to share those needs in some form, so that, for us as well, the concept of knowledge earns its keep notably by helping us identify good informants. Though abstract and seemingly idle, the concept of knowledge thus turns out to be a true game-changer because it makes knowledge social: it transforms the solitary business of information acquisition into a joint enterprise, where we don’t have to see everything with our own eyes, but mutually rely on each other to pool far more information than any one of us could ever gather by themselves. For a community to pool information, it must cultivate accuracy and sincerity in its members Having come this far in the genealogy of the concept of knowledge, we can explore the same scenario further to make sense of the idea of truth as a value. The Latin word for ‘truth’, veritas, adorns the blazons of countless institutions, and yet it can seem that, strictly speaking, truth is just a property of certain sentences, and not really the kind of thing that can carry value. As Bernard Williams remarked, when a mother finds out against all evidence to the contrary that her child survived the crash, this is of great value to her; yet this isn’t the value

The legacy of Desmond Tutu and South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission serve as poignant reminders of what can be achieved when truth, compassion, and justice unite.

The Truth and Reconciliation Commission, an extraordinary endeavor, was established to investigate human rights abuses during the apartheid era in South Africa. The Commission sought to provide a platform for victims and perpetrators alike to confront the past and embark on a journey towards healing and understanding. The significance of this process lies not only in the revelation of truth but also in the profound impact it had on mending a nation torn asunder by racial and political strife.

Desmond Tutu, Archbishop Emeritus of Cape Town, was an ardent advocate for truth, justice, and reconciliation. His unwavering commitment to these principles resonated far beyond the borders of South Africa, leaving an indelible mark on the global conscience. Tutu's legacy serves as a beacon of hope, demonstrating that even in the darkest of times, there is always room for healing and transformation.

However, as we reflect upon the lessons of South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission, we must acknowledge that the path towards reconciliation is fraught with challenges and complexities. The process requires an unflinching commitment to truth, a willingness to confront past atrocities, and a shared desire for justice and healing. It demands courage from both victims and perpetrators alike - the courage to speak out, to listen, and to forgive.

In our own lives, we are often called upon to navigate similar complexities. We may find ourselves entangled in conflicts, confronted with difficult truths, or seeking reconciliation with those who have wronged us. In these moments, the lessons of Tutu and the Commission offer a guiding light - one that reminds us of the power of truth, compassion, and justice to heal even the deepest wounds.

Yet, as we strive to apply these lessons in our own lives, we must acknowledge the limitations of our approach. The pursuit of truth, while essential, is not always straightforward or easy. It may challenge our beliefs, our values, and our sense of identity. It may require us to confront uncomfortable truths about ourselves and others. And it may demand that we engage in difficult conversations, listen actively, and extend grace where it is due.

In this way, the legacy of Desmond Tutu and South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission serve as a call to action - a challenge to each of us to embrace truth, compassion, and justice in our own lives and communities. It is a reminder that we have the power to heal, to transform, and to create a more just and equitable world. But it also serves as a warning - a caution against the easy path of denial, avoidance, and division.

The conglomerate aims to leverage AI capabilities to offer customers tailor-made recommendations based on their preferences, purchase history, and even social media activity.

The move could have far-reaching consequences for the fashion industry. If successful, other luxury brands might follow suit, leading to a wave of AI adoption in the sector. This could potentially disrupt traditional production processes, leading to job losses in certain areas but creating new opportunities in tech-related roles.

LVMH's AI venture is expected to be spearheaded by its newly formed AI research lab, LVAI (Louis Vuitton Artificial Intelligence). The lab, based in Paris, will focus on developing AI algorithms for various aspects of the fashion industry, from design and production to marketing and customer service.

While details about the timeline and scale of LVMH's AI project are scarce, Carrec hinted at ambitious plans. "We aim to be at the forefront of AI innovation in the luxury industry," he said. ExxonMobil, one of the world's largest oil and gas companies, has been grappling with declining revenue, raising questions about its operational efficiency and ethical practices. The multinational corporation reported a Q2 2021 loss of $1.1 billion, marking a stark contrast to the $3.1 billion profit it made in the same quarter last year.
