Dear Reader,

As we delve into the realm of lucid dreaming, a fascinating intersection of psychology and philosophy emerges. For centuries, philosophers have grappled with questions about reality, consciousness, and identity - concepts that seem to blur in the dream world. Lucid dreaming, where one is aware they are dreaming while still immersed within it, challenges our understanding of these fundamental ideas.

Consider, for instance, the concept of reality. If we can consciously navigate a dreamscape with an awareness that it's not 'real,' what does that mean for our perception of reality? Is there a clear-cut distinction between dreams and waking life, or is it more fluid than we think?

Then there's consciousness - the very essence of who we are. Lucid dreaming hints at the possibility of multiple layers of consciousness coexisting within us, each with its own unique sense of self. Yet, this contradicts our traditional understanding that a single, unified consciousness defines our identity.

Lastly, let's touch upon the concept of identity itself. When we lucid dream, are we truly ourselves? Or are we merely a shadow version of who we are in waking life? This question probes at the heart of what makes us unique individuals - something that has puzzled philosophers since antiquity.

Moreover, the ability to manipulate one's environment and experiences within a dream raises ethical questions. If we can choose our own reality in dreams, what does that mean for our morality? Are we bound by the same rules that govern waking life or are we free to act without consequence?

As we continue to explore these philosophical implications of lucid dreaming, it becomes increasingly clear that this phenomenon challenges many of our deeply held beliefs about reality, consciousness, and identity. It forces us to question whether our understanding of these concepts is as complete as we'd like to believe. And perhaps, more importantly, it reminds us that there is still much left to discover about the intricate workings of the human mind.

Title: A Cheesecake Factory visit that left me hungry (and not in the good way)

I've always heard great things about The Cheesecake Factory, but my experience during our recent vacation was far from satisfying. To start, let me clarify that I have a dairy allergy, which usually makes dining out a bit of a challenge. But I was hopeful that this popular chain would have some options for me.

We arrived at the outdoor seating area around 7 PM on a Friday evening. The place was bustling with families and groups of friends, which should've been a sign of things to come. Despite the crowd, we managed to find a table tucked away in a corner.

I browsed through the menu, my heart sinking as I realized there were very few dairy-free options. I settled on a simple grilled chicken salad, only to be told by our waitress that they had run out of that particular dish. After much back and forth with the kitchen staff, I finally decided on a different salad  one without cheese or dressings.

The wait time was excruciatingly long, and we watched as plates filled with gooey cheesy goodness passed us by. When my salad finally arrived, it looked lackluster at best. The chicken was overcooked, the greens wilted, and there wasn't a drop of dressing in sight  I had requested oil and vinegar on the side, but that didn't seem to register with the kitchen staff.

I ended up using the free refills of water as my makeshift dressing, which made for an interesting dining experience. The taste was bland, and the texture was disappointing  it felt like I was eating a plate of disappointment.

To top it off, the bill came to over $50 for two entrees (one being just a salad), an appetizer, and a drink each. Considering the lackluster service and food quality, I can't help but feel like we overpaid for this mediocre meal.

I'll admit, the cheesecake looked tempting as we left, but I couldn't bring myself to indulge in something that might send me to the emergency room. If you have dairy allergies or dietary restrictions, I would suggest giving The Cheesecake Factory a pass  unless you enjoy overpaying for disappointing food.

As the American Veterinary Medical Association, which opposes tail docking, states on its website: ‘The only benefit that appears to be derived from cosmetic tail docking of dogs is the owner’s impression of a pleasing appearance.’ Tail docking was banned in the UK in 2007 (with some exceptions for certain working breeds), and it is banned in Australia, parts of Canada, and much of Europe — but the practice remains common in the US. There is also a long history of cosmetic tail docking in horses, as well as tail ‘nicking’, in which a horse’s tail tendon is cut, causing the equine to carry its tail higher, in what some consider to be a more pleasing manner. text:
Why do we create art, and why does it seem to challenge our understanding of the world? As I gaze at a painting by Van Gogh, the swirling blues and yellows evoke a sense of turbulence within me - a feeling that defies logic or reason. It's as if the colors are whispering secrets only they understand, inviting me to peer into their enigmatic depths.

I remember one particular evening, not so long ago, when I found myself lost in the labyrinthine corridors of the Museum of Modern Art. I stumbled upon a contemporary installation - a series of interconnected rooms filled with mirrors, reflections, and echoes. The experience was disorienting yet exhilarating, as I tried to make sense of my surroundings while simultaneously losing myself within them.

The artist, it seemed, was inviting me to question the boundaries between reality and illusion - to confront the limitations of human understanding. And in that moment, I realized that art often serves as a mirror held up to society, reflecting our deepest fears, desires, and contradictions back at us. It forces us to reconsider our values, to challenge our assumptions, and to question the status quo.

Consider, for instance, 'The Invasion of America,' a mural by Mexican artist Orozco that depicts the brutal conquest of indigenous lands by European colonizers. Compare it with official maps produced by the US Geological Survey, which whitewash the history of dispossession and displacement. Which tells a truer story? The artist's work reminds us of the human cost of progress - the lives shattered, the cultures destroyed in pursuit of empire and profit.

In my own field of ethics, we grapple with similar questions. We ask whether there are objective moral truths, or if morality is a mere construct of our minds. Some argue that morality requires a foundation - an ultimate grounding in reason or nature. But I suspect they are off the mark, for morality seems to float above us, unanchored and transcendent. It speaks to something deeper within us - a yearning for justice, compassion, and connection.

As I stand before that painting once more, I am struck by the beauty of the colors, the power of the brushstrokes, and the haunting echoes of history they evoke. I find myself questioning my own values, my own assumptions about what it means to be human. And in that moment, I realize that art has the power to transform us - to challenge our perceptions, reshape our beliefs, and ultimately, help us understand ourselves a little better.

We are used to thinking of athletics as a levelling force in society, the ultimate meritocratic arena wherein anyone, regardless of class or wealth, can run, throw, hit and sweat their way to fame and fortune as professional athletes. Youth sport is widespread in most modern developed societies, and mandatory gym classes provide a path to physical training and development across the socioeconomic spectrum. This state of affairs is dramatically different from the ancient Greek situation. Only Greek male citizens could train and compete in sports. Within this population, informal constraints limited participation even further. Most poorer Greeks operated close to a basic subsistence level and worked on small farms, so they did not have leisure time available for long training sessions in the gym, located far off in the city centres, let alone could they take months out of their schedule to train and compete at Olympia without incurring disastrous agricultural consequences at home. This means that vast swaths of people who lived in an ancient Greek city – women, local residents who were not born in Greece, foreigners, and poor men – were largely excluded from athletics. In turn, participation in sport was strongly emblematic of a certain political and socioeconomic in-group – relatively wealthy, Greek, citizen males. The Greeks themselves had little clear idea of how and why athletic nudity emerged I now turn to the second key point, the cultural convention of athletic nudity. Athletes in ancient Greece were always expected to both train and compete naked. In fact, our modern word ‘gymnasium’ comes from the ancient Greek word gymnos, an adjective that means ‘naked’. Thus, while we don’t honour its etymology any more, the word’s original meaning was literally ‘naked place’. Why nude athletics? Answers to this question are elusive. Although many texts from the classical world survive, they have little to say about the meaning of nudity in society or art. The textual sources that do touch briefly on naked sport indicate that the Greeks themselves had little clear idea of how and why athletic nudity emerged as such a central part of their culture. Thucydides and Plato attribute the invention of athletic nudity to the Spartans and the Cretans, respectively, and indicate that (from their point of view) it set the Greeks apart from non-Greeks as especially civilised. However, it is not clear from their discussions how, when or why this state of affairs might have arisen. The cultural convention of athletic nudity probably originated so far back in time from the point of view of these authors that they had no direct knowledge of its advent. text:
The balance between law and moral fairness has been a persistent conundrum, often misunderstood in the grand tapestry of societal norms. It's like trying to decipher an unfinished symphony - you can hear the underlying rhythm, but there are notes missing, notes that might have tied everything together neatly.

Let me paint you a picture, one that's been dancing in my thoughts lately. Picture a courtroom, where justice is meted out, but what constitutes fairness remains elusive. Laws are the black-and-white rules, the solid structures that guide our actions and interactions. But what about those shades of gray? Those moments when compassion and empathy come into play, when we as a society decide to bend or even break the rules for the greater good? That's where moral fairness comes in - it's the colorful splash on an otherwise monochrome canvas.

But here's the catch. Laws are concrete, definable, measurable entities. They're like the building blocks of society, each one carefully crafted and put into place. Moral fairness, on the other hand, is a nebulous concept, shifting and changing with the wind. It's as elusive as trying to catch a rainbow mid-flight.

This dance between law and moral fairness isn't new - civilizations have grappled with this tension for millennia. Yet, we seem to be no closer to finding a resolution. Are we destined to live in a world where the two are perpetually at odds, like oil and water refusing to mix?

Hannah Arendt's groundbreaking work on totalitarianism, particularly her seminal book "The Origins of Totalitarianism," continues to resonate in our contemporary discourse. It serves as a prism through which we can examine the intricate web of power dynamics that shape societies and governments around the world.

Arendt's insights into the nature of totalitarian regimes, their manipulation of fear, and their erosion of individual freedoms are strikingly relevant today. One cannot help but draw parallels between her analysis of the German and Soviet experiences and contemporary authoritarian movements that exploit populism and nationalism to consolidate power.

Yet, it is not merely the overt manifestations of totalitarianism that echo in our times. Arendt's exploration of the banality of evil, the role of bureaucracy in facilitating oppression, and the dehumanization of individuals under such systems are themes that persist in our world today. We see this in the marginalization and persecution of minority groups, the erosion of civil liberties, and the instrumentalization of institutions for political gain.

Rhetorically speaking, one might ask: Are we not witnessing a new form of totalitarianism, one that is subtler and more insidious than its historical counterparts? The answer, I fear, is far from straightforward.

In my personal life, an encounter with a friend who was studying the rise of authoritarian tendencies in modern democracies served as a stark reminder of the relevance of Arendt's work. We discussed at length how the erosion of democratic norms and the politicization of institutions could potentially pave the way for more authoritarian regimes. The conversation left me with a sense of unease, underscoring the importance of continued vigilance and critical analysis in our times.

However, it is essential not to view Arendt's ideas as prescriptive or definitive. Instead, they should serve as a lens through which we can scrutinize contemporary political phenomena, challenging us to think deeply about the nature of power and the role of citizens in democratic societies.

As we grapple with these questions, it is crucial to remember that totalitarianism, in all its forms, represents a severe threat to human dignity and freedom. It is our responsibility as global citizens to remain vigilant, to question authority, and to strive for the preservation of democracy and human rights.

In this ongoing discussion, Hannah Arendt's work serves as an indispensable guide, reminding us of the complexities and dangers inherent in our political systems and urging us to remain steadfast in our pursuit of truth and justice.

text: in the late 20th century. One important dimension of Sellars’s view was a semantic theory that differed just enough from the then-dominant theories to enable Sellars’s followers to begin to make sense of Hegel’s logic, a major step towards resuscitating Hegelian philosophy. Brandom’s contribution to the recent ‘non-metaphysical’ interpretation of Hegel is that semantic theory is key to the revival of Hegelian thought, so let’s examine it closely. The analytic tradition relied on what seemed to be a straightforward conception of semantics. Whereas syntax concerns intralinguistic relations (wellformedness, rules for composition of legal strings of the language), semantics – the discipline concerned with specifying the content of linguistic expressions – concerns the relation between language and the world. But Sellars denies that semantics requires a basic word-world relation at all. Rather, the content of a linguistic expression – and also the content of a concept or a thought – is a matter of the proper inferential relations it bears to other expressions or thoughts. So semantics is primarily a matter of word-word relations, instead of word-world. In explicating this ‘inferentialist’ theory of meaning, Sellars extends the notion of inference to cover not just the formally valid inferences explored in formal logic – inferences that depend only on the syntactic structure of the sentences involved, and, thus, inferences in which the non-logical words are irrelevant to the validity of the inference – but also material inferences. Material inferences are inferences whose validity is not guaranteed by the syntax of the sentences alone. For example, the inference from ‘The ball is red’ to ‘The ball is coloured’ is not formally valid, but it is a perfectly good inference. In Sellars’s view, semantics is not all about intralinguistic connections, for our responses to the world around us, called language-entry transitions (or observation reports), and the way we act on our stated intentions and desires, called language-exit transitions, are also important elements in the constitution of meaning. How one interacts with the world (and with other people) is important for interpreting one’s beliefs and desires. The result is that the content of a word or sentence, and equally the content of a concept or thought, is determined by its position within a complex linguistic economy that includes observation reports, inferences, various speech acts, etc, all responsive to norms of proper usage. This is a holistic and non-foundationalistic semantics, for there are no expressions whose content is determined independently of other expressions in the system. Sellars’s younger colleague Brandom picked up this thread of Sellars’s thought and brought to the fore the fact that our concepts have histories, stories about how they came to have the connections that make them the concepts they are. Brandom then used this view of semantics to interpret Hegel’s conception of dialectic. Brandom includes not only material connections between concepts, but also material exclusions: what a concept excludes or is

In the heart of New York City, I stood before the towering Freedom Tower, a symbol of resilience and liberty. Yet, as I gazed up at its imposing structure, my thoughts were far from lofty ideals. Instead, they were tangled in the intricate web of tension between security and civil liberties - a conflict that echoes through the halls of power and the whispers of the streets.

Just like the Freedom Tower, erected as a response to an act of terror, modern society often responds with fortifications when faced with threats. We install cameras, build walls, and deploy surveillance technologies, all in the name of security. But at what cost? Are we not sacrificing some of our most cherished civil liberties - the very essence of what it means to be free?

Consider the case of a man I once knew, John Doe. A peaceful protester, he was swept up in a wave of mass arrests during an anti-war rally. His phone, confiscated by authorities for 'investigative purposes,' was ransacked, revealing sensitive information about his activist circle. This is but one example of how our digital lives can be easily compromised in the name of security.

Yet, it is not only technology that poses a threat to our liberties. In the pursuit of safety, we may unwittingly erode the very foundations upon which our democracy rests - freedom of speech, assembly, and privacy. As Orwell warned us, totalitarian regimes often emerge from well-meaning efforts to ensure security.

But to argue that all measures taken in the name of security are detrimental would be a gross oversimplification. For instance, the balance between national security and individual privacy can be struck through transparency, accountability, and oversight. We must work tirelessly to refine these tools, ensuring they serve as checks on power rather than instruments of oppression.

The tension between security and civil liberties is a dance as old as civilization itself. It requires delicate footwork, for one misstep can send us careening into the abyss of tyranny. As we grapple with these complexities, let us never lose sight of the fact that our pursuit of safety should not come at the expense of our freedom. The Freedom Tower stands as a reminder that resilience and liberty are not mutually exclusive, but rather intertwined in the very fabric of society. It is up to us to ensure that this tapestry remains whole, vibrant, and true to its roots.

text: required of Athenian citizens are essential to understanding the trial. Socrates’ own commitments to his city influenced the trial’s course, and those commitments were core parts of Athenian political culture, shaping the relationship between public speech and responsibility. Indeed, the actions of Socrates, Meletus and the jury must be understood in the context of the Athenians’ emphasis on the role of the responsible citizen in the democratic state, on their ideal of civic responsibility. Thus it is a story, in many ways, of civic engagement, in some respects far removed from the politics of recognition that characterise contemporary US debates. In his retelling of the trial, the late Roman-era writer Maximus of Tyre claimed that Socrates refused to respond to Meletus’ charges, maintaining a dignified silence throughout. Indeed, dissidents who seek to deny the validity of a given political system have periodically adopted a principled refusal to participate in a court proceeding or legal system. But, Socrates did acknowledge the authority of the court. He actively participated in his own defence – though it was, in many ways, an idiosyncratic one. Having thus accepted the Laws’ fruits, on what grounds could Socrates now turn around and deny their legitimacy? The point is that Socrates did not dispute the legitimacy of the trial, he did not take a stand outside a corrupt political system; rather, he participated as a citizen. As a citizen, his civic obligation required him to answer charges that he had acted in ways that harmed that community. Plato’s version of the defence speech makes clear that Socrates sought to vindicate himself, to show that he had not harmed the community. Indeed, Socrates represented himself as a civic benefactor, who ought to be rewarded. His record of contributions to the civic life of Athens, he believed, merited the honour of free meals at the state hearth. Plato’s many dialogues establish the commitments of Socrates the citizen, and paint a vivid portrait of Athenian political culture. In the dialogue Crito, Socrates explains that despite his conviction, it was not ethically permissible for him to escape from the prison. In Crito, Plato gives readers a ‘dialogue within a dialogue’, in which the personified Laws of Athens speak to another, counterfactual Socrates. This counterfactual Socrates intends to avoid punishment by escaping from prison and fleeing to some distant land. The Laws of Athens in Plato’s dialogue are not only the formal, written rules of the Athenian state, but also its civic norms. They ask the counterfactually disobedient Socrates what he found lacking in them, and when he had discovered that lack. The Laws point out to Socrates that it was through them (the laws concerning marriage) that he came into the world. The Laws point out that, through the norms concerning parents’ responsibility for their children, they nurtured him. Finally, through them he received his education. Socrates acknowledges all of this.

In the grand tapestry of intellectual discourse, few figures loom larger and more enigmatic than Friedrich Nietzsche. A philosophical titan who sought to challenge the very foundations of Western thought, his ideas continue to echo through the halls of modern culture like an unyielding drumbeat, driving human understanding and behavior in ways both subtle and profound.

At first glance, it may seem paradoxical that a man who championed the 'Will to Power' would find himself celebrated as a paragon of compassion and empathy. Yet, Nietzsche's ideas have wormed their way into the hearts and minds of a generation, inspiring movements that aim to shatter the chains of conformity and redefine what it means to be human.

Take, for instance, the modern phenomenon of 'self-improvement.' On the surface, it appears to be little more than a narcissistic quest for self-aggrandizement. However, delve deeper, and you'll find traces of Nietzsche's influence - his notion that one must strive ceaselessly to overcome oneself in order to attain true greatness. This pursuit of self-mastery has become a global obsession, fueling an industry worth billions as individuals seek to remold themselves into the embodiment of their ideals.

But Nietzsche's legacy is not without its darker aspects. His ideas have been coopted by those who would use them to justify the most heinous acts of brutality and oppression. The 'Ubermensch,' his concept of the superman, has become a rallying cry for those who seek to assert their dominance over others, casting aside the very values of empathy and compassion that Nietzsche himself held dear.

A simple act, yet one that carries an ocean of weight on its shoulders.

Consider the story of the whales. Their lives, so vast and mysterious to us, yet their behaviors seem alarmingly familiar. They mate at times we least expect, raising questions about their reproductive cycle that mirrors our own struggle to understand ourselves. The research into their baleen, a testament to human curiosity and perseverance, has given us glimpses into their lives  yet we still find ourselves in the dark.

This dance of knowledge and mystery is not unique to the whales but resonates with our own human existence. We too, much like these enigmatic creatures, are grappling with the intricacies of our identity in this digital age. Our actions, thoughts, and emotions are broadcasted across the globe at the click of a button, yet we find ourselves yearning for understanding  not just from others but also from ourselves.

The era of social media has blurred the lines between private and public lives. It has given us a platform to express our deepest feelings and thoughts, yet it has also thrust our vulnerabilities into the spotlight. We find ourselves torn between the desire for connection and the fear of exposure.

In this digital jungle, we face an endless parade of opinions, facts, and misinformation. It is a labyrinth where truth often gets lost in translation. Yet, amidst the chaos, we seek to carve out our own identity  not just for others but for ourselves. We strive to make sense of the world, to understand who we are and what we stand for.

But how do we navigate this complex landscape? How do we find personal responsibility in a world where our actions can have far-reaching consequences? These questions linger in my mind as I walk beneath the twinkling stars, their light mirrored in the calm waters below.

In the face of these uncertainties, one thing remains clear: Our actions matter. The ripple effect of our digital footprints can extend far beyond what we see or imagine. We must take responsibility for the words we speak and the actions we take, not just because others are watching but because it defines who we are.

As I continue my walk, I am reminded that understanding is a journey  one without a clear destination or end. It requires patience, curiosity, and humility. It demands that we look inward, to understand ourselves before we can hope to understand others. And as the whales remind us, sometimes the answers lie not in the present but in the past.

So, I step forward, guided by the light of my own thoughts, my digital footprints echoing behind me. I navigate the complexities of personal responsibility in this age of social media, knowing that the answers may never come but the journey is worthwhile. And as I walk, I am reminded that it is not just about understanding the world but understanding ourselves  our thoughts, emotions, and actions. For it is only then that we can truly take responsibility for who we are and who we aspire to be.

text: René Descartes’s dog, Monsieur Grat (‘Mister Scratch’), used to accompany the 17th-century French philosopher on his ruminative walks, and was the object of his fond attention. Yet, for the most part, Descartes did not think very highly of the inner life of nonhuman animals. ‘[T]he reason why animals do not speak as we do is not that they lack the organs but that they have no thoughts,’ Descartes wrote in a letter in 1646. Followers of Descartes have argued that consciousness is a uniquely human attribute, perhaps facilitated by language, that allows us to communicate and coordinate our memories, sensations and plans over time. On this view, versions of which persist in some quarters today, nonhuman animals are little more than clever automata with a toolkit of preprogrammed behaviours that respond to specific triggers. Insects such as bees and ants are often held up as the epitome of the robotically mechanistic approach to animal nature. Scientists have long known that these creatures must possess a large behavioural repertoire in order to construct their elaborate homes, defend against intruders, and provision their young with food. Yet many still find it plausible to look at bees and ants as little more than ‘reflex machines’, lacking an internal representation of the world, or an ability to foresee even the immediate future. In the absence of external stimuli or internal triggers such as hunger, it’s believed that the insect’s mind is dark and its brain is switched off. Insects are close to ‘philosophical zombies’: hypothetical beings that rely entirely on routines and reflexes, without any awareness. But perhaps the problem is not that insects lack an inner life, but that they don’t have a way to communicate it in terms we can understand. It is hard for us to prise open a window into their minds. So maybe we misdiagnose animal brains as having machine-like properties simply because we understand how machines work – whereas, to date, we have only a fragmentary and imperfect insight into how even the simplest brains process, store and retrieve information. However, there are now many signs that consciousness-like phenomena might exist not just among humans or even great apes – but that insects might have them, too. Not all of these lines of evidence are from experiments specifically designed to explore consciousness; in fact, some have lain buried in the literature for decades, even centuries, without anyone recognising their hidden significance. Title: A Delightful Happy Hour Experience at Culver's! 

Oh boy, let me tell you about my recent visit to Culver's during their happy hour! I've been meaning to check this place out for a while now, and I must say, it did not disappoint.

First things first, the ambiance was super cozy. The staff were friendly and welcoming, making me feel right at home as soon as I walked in. My friend and I grabbed a table by the window to enjoy the beautiful evening.

Absolutely divine! They had just the right amount of crispness on the outside and gooey goodness on the inside.

Being a health-conscious eater, I was pleased to find several options on their menu that fit my lifestyle. For instance, they have a variety of salads and grilled chicken sandwiches. However, during happy hour, even the more indulgent items felt like a treat worth savoring.

Now, let's talk about the restroom situation. I know it might seem a bit random, but cleanliness is important to me, and Culver's nailed it! The bathroom was spotless, with fresh towels and soap dispensers that were well-stocked. It's the little things like this that show a restaurant truly cares about its customers' experience.

Before wrapping up, I have to mention the fantastic service we received. Our server was attentive, friendly, and always made sure our drinks never went empty. And when there was a minor mix-up with my order (they ran out of tuna for my nigiri - I know, I know, first-world problems!), they were more than accommodating and quickly resolved the issue without any fuss.

In conclusion, if you're looking for a fantastic place to unwind during happy hour while still maintaining your health-conscious lifestyle, Culver's is definitely worth a visit. I can't wait to go back and try more of their delicious offerings!

Camus, a prominent figure in this philosophical movement, postulates that life is inherently meaningless, but we can create our own purpose through rebellion and revolt against the absurdity of it all. This perspective challenges the very fabric of our societal norms, where conformity and stability are often prized above individuality and upheaval. Are we, in our obedience to societal expectations, silencing the rebel within?

Lastly, consider the Buddhist teachings on suffering and the pursuit of enlightenment. According to this philosophy, our pain arises from our desiresthe wanting-to-have part that Schopenhauer discussed. In our capitalistic society, where material possessions are often equated with success and happiness, are we doomed to a life of constant suffering because of our insatiable desires?

These philosophical theories, while centuries old, still resonate within us today. They force us to confront uncomfortable truths about ourselves and challenge the status quo. Yet, they also offer a beacon of hope: a path towards a more balanced, meaningful life, one where we find harmony between reason and emotion, virtue and pleasure, rebellion and conformity, and suffering and enlightenment.

But the journey is not an easy one. It demands introspection, self-reflection, and a willingness to question our beliefs, values, and actions. As I continue to explore these ideas, I can't help but wonder: Am I ready to embark on this transformative journey? And if so, where will it lead me? The answers are still elusive, but the quest remains tantalizingly within reach.

text: echoes Finck’s of a century ago: we can have it all. Pleasure and health. Authentic, yummy, fresh food. Sustainability and economic renewal. Baked together into a pure and golden loaf studded with self-congratulatory nuggets of goodness. ‘Eat real food,’ Michael Pollan counsels, and pleasure and wellness will follow. We are warned against flavours that are delicious without being ‘good’. Today, food technology is singled out for designing food that is literally irresistible. As Michael Moss described in Salt, Sugar, Fat (2013), ‘big food’ has organised itself around the pursuit of the ‘hyperpalatable’, products aiming for the sensory ‘bliss point’, delivering compulsive flavour while withholding nutritional value. The eaters of these foods, the ones who prefer them, are disdained for their tastes even as their choices are to be pitied, bearing on their bodies the stigma. The stakes of good taste seem higher than ever, separating those whose virtuous choices reinforce health and life from those whose bad appetites lead them towards disease and death. But promising health and wellbeing as the outcomes of individual good taste has implications that we might pause to consider. The food that gets counted as ‘good’ (‘real food’) is less evidently technological, more ‘natural’, less ‘processed’ – and often more expensive. As the historian Rachel Laudan has noted, eating less processed food means processing more of your own food – a redistribution of labour towards the private household, and often women. Unlike Finck’s democratising vision of flavour and health, good food is often associated with the resources, the access and the time to procure, prepare and enjoy it. Good taste – and good health – remains elite, a mark of distinction, and chiefly a privilege of the fortunate or the wealthy. More importantly, food habits are portrayed primarily as matters of individual choice (reflections of good or bad taste) and personal responsibility, rather than the results of complex social and technological systems. Flavour is necessary, but it is not health.

If the lightning flash is present, then rain fell in the past, and thunder will growl in the future. McTaggart’s argument against time has two steps. On the B-series, nothing changes, for every event will always occupy the same position in time: the lightning will always flash after the rain. text:
Dear Reader,

In the grand tapestry of our collective consciousness, the hero's journey has been a constant thread, woven through the tales we tell ourselves and one another. It is a tale as old as time, a narrative of trials, tribulations, and triumphs that mirrors our own personal struggles. Yet, in the modern era, this archetypal story has evolved to reflect the complexities and ambiguities of our times.

The hero's journey, once a straightforward quest for glory or redemption, now often presents us with ethical dilemmas that challenge our very notions of right and wrong. In these stories, the heroes are no longer solely the paragons of virtue we once admired; they are fallible, flawed individuals grappling with the consequences of their choices. This evolution reveals the limits of language itself, for it is difficult to encapsulate the nuances of moral complexity in a neatly packaged narrative arc.

Consider, if you will, the modern anti-hero - a character who embodies the paradox of being both heroic and morally ambiguous. These characters force us to question our assumptions about what it means to be a hero, pushing us to confront our own ethical dilemmas. Are we willing to compromise our values in pursuit of a greater good? Can a hero exist without flaws or contradictions?

Moreover, this evolution highlights the fragility of our moral compasses, for they are as subjective as the stories we tell. In the hands of different authors, the same story can be told from vastly different perspectives, painting very different pictures of the same hero's journey. This reminds us that our understanding of morality is not absolute but rather a product of our own experiences and biases.

The ethical dilemmas presented in modern literature serve as mirrors reflecting our deepest fears and desires. They challenge us to confront our own moral limitations, urging us to strive for greater understanding and empathy. But they also remind us of the inherent ambiguity of our world, a world where there are no easy answers or clear-cut heroes.

As we continue to tell and retell these stories, we must ask ourselves: what role do we want the hero's journey to play in our lives? Do we seek simplicity, or are we willing to embrace complexity and ambiguity? For it is in these questions that we find the true power of storytelling - the power to shape our understanding of ourselves and our world.

In the ever-evolving dance of human civilization, one notion that has left us spinning, tripping, and finding our footing again throughout centuries is individualism. A concept as old as humanity itself, it has morphed and twisted in ways that echo the contours of societies and cultures across time. Yet, it remains a term often misunderstood, its true essence veiled behind layers of misconception.

Take, for instance, the notion of secrets. We keep them, we share them, yet their role in defining our selves is a matter of ongoing debate. Are they a reflection of our true identities or mere fragments, fragments that, if revealed, might crack open the faade we've so carefully constructed? Is it not intriguing how confessing a secret to another feels like unveiling a piece of our soul? In Nineteen Eighty-Four, Winston's exposure of his deepest secret led him to lose not only his freedom but also his very self. This is the power secrets wield over us, their influence on human behavior an intricate tapestry that continues to unfurl.

Yet, individualism has transcended the boundaries of our personal lives and seeped into the fabric of society. In the 20th century, Japan's ghost stories served as a mirror reflecting the unease of modernity. The sinister figure of Sadako climbing from her well in Ringu was no exception; she was a manifestation of our collective fear, a testament to the fact that even as we sought to master the world around us, we were reminded of our own mortality and vulnerability.

And yet, are we truly masters of our own destiny? The ghosts of Japan may be disappearing into the abyss of consumption and indifference, but they continue to ask a question that resonates with each one of us: How real are we? The world we create, the world we inhabit, serves to strengthen the myth of human mastery, yet it is the mysteries that lie beneath the surface, the shadows cast by our actions, that keep us questioning.

Is it possible that Yanagita's fears for the people of Tokyo and Osaka were misplaced? Perhaps we require a balance between mastery and mystery, a delicate dance that keeps us tethered to both reality and the unknown. The allure of J-horror lies in this tension, this pull between the curiosity that drives us to explore the darker recesses of human life and the fear that keeps us from venturing too far into the abyss.

As we look back at Japan's traumas, a subtle shift may be discernible, a hint that the balance is being recalibrated. In the face of adversity, it seems we are reminded of our fundamental nature, of the hidden world that lurks beneath the surface. The question remains: will we heed this reminder, or continue to dance along the razor's edge, teetering between mastery and mystery? Only time will tell.

In the modern age, we stand on the precipice of a new era, where our daily lives are intertwined with the digital realm. The once distant and ominous figures of ghosts, mythical apparitions that haunted our ancient lore, now seem to have taken a more tangible form - they've morphed into the intangible specters of social media.

I recall a chilling encounter with one such phantom, lurking in the shadows of my Facebook timeline. A friend, once close and vibrant, had transformed into an eerie echo of their former self. Their posts, once filled with laughter and light, now dripped with venom and resentment. The digital mirror they gazed into reflected a twisted image that bore little resemblance to the person I once knew. It was then that I realized, perhaps more than ever before, that our actions online have real-world consequences.

The ghosts of social media are not merely figments of our imagination; they are the remnants of our digital footprints, the echoes of past interactions that haunt us long after we've moved on. We create them with each swipe, like, and comment, contributing to a vast network of memories that may outlive us. The question then arises: How real are we in this virtual world?

As I scroll through my feed, I am confronted by the paradox of our modern existence. On one hand, we have harnessed the power of technology to connect with people across the globe, to share our thoughts and experiences in an instant. Yet, on the other, we are haunted by our own creations, by the ghosts of our past actions that linger long after we've moved on.

The ghosts of social media demand accountability, not just for our words and actions, but for the ripple effects they create. They serve as a constant reminder of the power we wield in this digital age, the power to shape narratives, to influence opinions, and to mold perceptions. It is essential that we recognize the impact of our actions, both positive and negative, and strive to leave behind a legacy worthy of respect.

As I navigate this digital landscape, I cannot help but wonder if we are losing touch with our humanity in our quest for virtual mastery. Are we becoming increasingly detached from our own responsibilities as we hide behind screens and keyboards? Or is this newfound power simply another facet of our innate desire to connect, to communicate, and to create?

These questions linger in the air, unanswered, as I continue my journey through the labyrinthine corridors of the digital realm. The ghosts of social media may be ever-present, but they offer us a unique opportunity to reflect on our actions, to learn from our mistakes, and to grow as individuals. It is up to us to decide whether we will embrace this opportunity or allow ourselves to be consumed by the shadows of our own creations.

No doubt one of the most enduringly controversial of modern translators is Ezra Pound, praised for the exquisite poetry of his renderings, damned for the outrageous licence he would often take. Pound’s anthology of classical Chinese poetry, Cathay (1915), is a go-to case study of a translation based on no particular knowledge of the source language (Pound cribbed from literal renditions by the scholar Ernest Fenollosa), which at the same time manages to be more convincing than versions produced by fluent readers of Chinese – to the point where some scholars feel that Pound’s intuitive phrasings are actually more accurate. This was no isolated case, for Pound took similar liberties with texts he could read in the original, such as Arthur Rimbaud’s ‘Au Cabaret-Vert’ (1870). Here’s a stanza of the poem in Wallace Fowlie’s studiously correct English: For a week my boots had been tornBy the pebbles on the roads. text:
Dear Reader,

Howard Zinn's 'A People's History' was a transformative tome that shook the foundations of American historical narratives. It challenged us to question our understanding of freedom and belonging, revealing the intricate tapestry of tension between these two fundamental human desires.

Imagine, if you will, standing on the precipice of a grand canyon, gazing at the vast expanse below. On one side, the towering cliffs symbolize freedom - the boundless potential for growth and exploration. Yet, on the other, the abyss represents belonging - the comforting embrace of community and tradition. Zinn's work invites us to peer over this chasm, to consider the complex interplay between these two forces that have shaped our nation's history.

In my own life, I was reminded of this tension when I moved to a new city several years ago. I yearned for the freedom to explore, to shape my own destiny, yet longed for the comfort and familiarity of belonging to a community. The struggle between these two desires was palpable, and I found myself asking: Is it possible to have both? Or are we forever caught in this dance between freedom and belonging?

Zinn's work suggests that the answer lies not in finding the perfect balance but in recognizing and embracing the tension. For it is within this struggle that our humanity flourishes, as we navigate the complexities of our history and strive to build a more equitable future.

As we grapple with these questions, however, I am left wondering: What does it mean for us today, in our increasingly interconnected world? Are we becoming more free or more enmeshed in the tapestry of belonging? And perhaps most importantly, how do we ensure that we are not lost in the labyrinth of history, but rather, actively shaping its narrative?

These questions linger in the air like echoes from the past, beckoning us to continue our exploration and seek answers. For it is through this ongoing dialogue that we can truly understand the tapestry of our history and strive for a more just future.

text: Rawls’s evil, bad and unjust men. ‘What moves the evil man is the love of injustice,’ Rawls wrote in A Theory of Justice (1971), ‘he delights in the impotence and humiliation of those subject to him and relishes being recognised by them as the wilful author of their degradation.’ The sun was setting on the slaverholders’ way of life and, in their own minds, they play the leading roles as victims and heroes in turn The contemporary traffickers and slaveholders I spoke with are not motivated by a love of injustice. They are instead driven by cultural inertia, a desire for profit or, more frequently, a need for basic sustenance. Instead of evil villains, we find husbands, fathers, mothers and neighbours working with the cultural materials available to them, surviving as best they can. The terms used here – slavery and slaveholder – never crossed the lips, nor perhaps even the minds, of the men I spoke with. As we broke bread, drank tea, explored plantations and silk-production houses, swam in deep wells, climbed coconut trees and talked late into countless evenings, the term slavery never came up. What came up, time after time, was respect, honour and dignity. The sun was setting on their way of life and, in their own minds, they play the leading roles as victims and heroes in turn. In so many ways, we all find ourselves at this juncture: in the interregnum between generations, eras and epochs. Tremendous changes have provided new opportunities for marginalised people, while the formerly powerful find themselves suddenly in possession of a form of power – caste and land – that lacks much exchange value in the new world. For the recently powerful, this transition can be devastating and demoralising. What psychologists call loss aversion is real; humans don’t want to lose what we have, no matter what. This is true whether the loss is occurring in Birmingham, Alabama – or Bihar, India. The real question is how we respond to loss – by lashing out, by learning, by opening up, or by closing down. Surely Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn was right when he wrote in the The Gulag Archipelago (1973): ‘If only it were all so simple! If only there were evil people somewhere insidiously committing evil deeds, and it were necessary only to separate them from the rest of us and destroy them. But the line dividing good and evil cuts through the heart of every human being. And who is willing to destroy a piece of his own heart?’

Title: A Heavenly Hideaway During the Heatwave 

Hey Yelpers!

Got a real gem to share with y'all today - The Sunflower Bakehouse, tucked away in this little nook of our town. I've been eagerly waiting for summer to try this place out, and boy, was it worth the wait!

text:
In the grand tapestry of our existence, art has always held a special place. It's a mirror reflecting our society's hopes, fears, dreams, and nightmares. Like the rhythm in 'Grandpa (Tell Me 'bout The Good Ol' Days)', it often serves as a balm for collective wounds, a soothing whisper of nostalgia amidst chaos.

But art isn't just a comfort blanket; it can be a fierce critic too. It challenges us, provokes thought, and sparks dialogue. Take, for instance, Shakespeare's 'Richard III'. A play that delves into the darkest recesses of human nature, it forces us to confront our own flaws and biases.

Art, in many ways, is a testament to our resilience and adaptability as a species. It's a beacon of hope amidst harmful mutations and overly harsh selection in the grand game of evolution. Each gene, like each artist, contributes to the symphony of life, yet none can claim sole credit for any particular melody.

However, this doesn't mean that art is devoid of consequence or power. Far from it. Art shapes our perceptions, influences our thoughts, and molds our actions. It can unite us, divide us, or push us to question the status quo. The problem lies in understanding its role and impact.

Consider nationalist nostalgia, for instance. Research suggests a correlation between this collective yearning for 'the way it was' and opposition to diversity. This isn't to say that all art that evokes nostalgia leads to prejudice, but it does highlight the potential power of art in shaping societal attitudes.

Yet, art isn't deterministic. It doesn't dictate our thoughts or actions; rather, it provides a platform for exploration and expression. Art allows us to grapple with complex issues, to find meaning in our lives, and to connect with each other.

So, where does this leave us? In the midst of a fascinating dance between art and society, a dance that's been going on since the dawn of time. A dance that's complex, nuanced, and far from over. A dance that's as much about us as it is about them, a dance that invites us to ask questions, to explore, to grow.

And so, we continue to create, to consume, to interpret. We continue to use art as a lens through which to view ourselves and our world, a mirror that reflects not just who we are, but who we aspire to be. As Shakespeare might have put it, 'What's past is prologue.' But what comes next is up to us.

text: on the pages of 1984 Winston’s rebellion is private and introverted – trying to steal moments away from the telescreens to write in his secret diary, like a teenager putting on her headphones and blasting her music behind a locked bedroom door. Until, that is, Winston begins an affair with Julia. It is only through romance that his rebellion comes to vivid, dangerous life. How completely adolescent! Only when a boyfriend or girlfriend shows up can real independence begin. 1984 is effective as a teen novel not because of its political message, but because it dramatises the internal psychic struggle of growing up. What makes 1984 immortal is that it ends with a real insight into the futility of teenage rebellion. Every teen becomes an adult, joins the machine, and comes to love Big Brother. Teens hate this fact – many of them hate the ending – but it stays with them, because they know it’s true. In 1984’s sibling, Animal Farm (1945), the animals with whom we identify see their ultimate betrayal: the pigs standing like their former human masters in their former masters’ house. But in 1984 it is Winston himself who gives in at the end. He does love Big Brother, because Big Brother, also known as adult society, has manipulated language, power and thought to perpetuate its system. By being born into it, we have already lost. Depressing, perhaps cynical – but teens see their very lives scribbled on the pages of 1984. It’s more fun to read a dystopian novel with a happy ending. But the novel that you pass on to your children is the one that first taught you the truth. When a child asks for the same book three hundred times, she is telling her parents what she needs to learn, what she needs to come to terms with. Adults do the same thing. Books are psychologists, using imagination therapy to elicit secrets that their readers did not know they kept. A child does not realise what he reveals when he names this doll ‘mummy’, that one ‘daddy’, and then has them shout at one another. Nor do we tend to realise what we are revealing about ourselves when we push a book into the hands of three friends. Maybe the bestseller list, stripped of the fly-by-night entries and dopamine drips, is a snapshot of the national psyche. It might be telling us what we need to learn, what we are coming to terms with. More certainly, I know one thing: I hope my daughter doesn’t love The Carrot Seed as much as I did.

text:
In the vast expanse of human thought, intellectual traditions have always played a pivotal role in shaping our modern ways of thinking. They serve as mirrors reflecting the cultural values and beliefs of societies across time and space, yet they also act as prisms through which we perceive and interpret the world around us.

Take, for instance, the tradition of African ethnophilosophy. It's a concept that has been both celebrated and criticized, yet its influence on contemporary philosophy is undeniable. Paulin Hountondji, an influential African philosopher, dismissed ethnophilosophy as substantive African philosophy, but he overlooked its value as a rich wellspring of African ideas. Scholars like Thaddeus Metz, who delved into the study of African philosophy, found invaluable resources in ethnophilosophical texts and traditional worldviews.

One might ask, what is the significance of this fusion? Well, it provided me with the building blocks for a unique philosophical perspective I call consolation philosophy. From the monistic worldview of the Idoma people, I borrowed the idea of mood as the essence of things. From Senghor, I adopted the concept of the melancholy being. And from African philosophers like Placide Tempels, Asouzu, Ramose, and Chimakonam, I gleaned the idea of an interdependent universe of missing links. Consolation philosophy is, in essence, a blend of these ideas woven into a tapestry of human existence.

Rhetorically speaking, one could argue that consolation philosophy is African philosophy. However, its applicability transcends geographical boundaries. I believe it resonates with the European, the American, the Indian, and the Chinese alike. Yet, this assertion remains somewhat tentative, a work in progress rather than a definitive statement.

My intellectual journey has been one of constant evolution, enriched by interactions with fellow African philosophers, Western analytic and continental philosophers, and my budding interest in Oriental philosophies. Yet, I find myself grappling with a philosophical conundrum, a struggle between tradition and modernity, between the familiar and the foreign. This is the challenge of being an African philosopher in the contemporary world - a delicate negotiation between the devil and the deep blue sea.

As I continue to navigate this intellectual landscape, I am reminded of the words of Karl Popper, a philosopher who significantly influenced the scientific mindset. He argued that science doesn't seek answers, but rather strives to disprove hypotheses. This idea resonates with me as I explore the philosophical tradition of African ethnophilosophy, constantly questioning, testing, and refining my understanding of this rich intellectual heritage.

Yet, Popperianism, as appealing as it may be, is not without its critics. Philosophers have pointed out the inherent flaw in his method of falsification - that in real-world experiments, it's impossible to isolate a single hypothetical element for disproof. And yet, despite these criticisms, Popperianism remains popular among scientists, perhaps due to its simplicity and appeal.

In my own philosophical explorations, I find myself grappling with similar questions - the nature of truth, the limits of knowledge, and the role of intellectual traditions in shaping our modern thinking. These are not questions that can be easily answered, but they are questions that demand constant reflection, constant questioning, and above all, a willingness to engage with the rich tapestry of intellectual traditions that have shaped human thought throughout history.

Dear reader, have you ever pondered upon how societies reinterpret the past? It's a fascinating topic, isn't it? Much like how our memories of events can be influenced by context and circumstance, so too does history undergo this process of reinvention over time.

Take, for instance, personal memories. We often recall events with a certain level of clarity, but the peripheral details can become hazy or distorted. This is not due to our faculties failing us, but rather, they're engaged in high-stakes multitasking. The identity of the perpetrator becomes reinforced after the fact by survival instincts, as we need to remember whom to avoid.

Similarly, history can be influenced by survival instincts too. In some societies, certain events or figures might be glorified or vilified depending on the needs of the present. The narrative can change to fit the requirements of the current context. This is not necessarily a bad thing; it's just a reflection of how we use history to guide our actions and make sense of our world.

Now, consider a more extreme example. In some cases, societies might rewrite their past to erase certain events or figures entirely. This can be seen in the way some countries have denied or downplayed their role in atrocities committed during wartime. This doesn't mean that these events never happened; rather, it suggests a desire to forget or move on from them.

This process of reinterpreting the past is not always conscious or malicious. Sometimes, it can simply be a matter of perspective. For example, consider the way a child might interpret a fairy tale compared to an adult. The same story can hold different meanings at different stages of life.

Yet, this doesn't make the process any less complex or intriguing. How do we ensure that our interpretation of history is as accurate as possible? How do we balance the need for narrative and understanding with the importance of truth and honesty? These are questions that I find myself pondering often.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on this, reader. Do you think societies should strive for objective history, or is it inevitable that our interpretation of the past will be shaped by our present circumstances? And if so, how can we ensure that our reinterpretations of history are not merely self-serving or manipulative? Let's continue this discussion another time. Until then, I hope these thoughts provoke some interesting reflections for you.

text:
John Rawls' theory of justice has left an indelible mark on modern political philosophy, challenging us to reconsider our fundamental assumptions about fairness and equality. At the heart of his work lies the idea of a social contract, where individuals agree to live together in society based on principles that guarantee the greatest amount of liberty and opportunity for the least advantaged among them.

This notion has sparked intense debate about how societies should be organized and governed. For instance, some argue that it reinforces the traditional liberal model of government, while others contend that it lends itself to more radical forms of egalitarianism. Yet, regardless of where one stands on the political spectrum, there's no denying that Rawls' work has forced us to confront the contradictions in human nature.

One such contradiction lies at the intersection of self-interest and altruism. On one hand, we are driven by our own desires and ambitions, often prioritizing ourselves over others. On the other, we possess a capacity for empathy and compassion that compels us to act in the best interests of those who are less fortunate. It is this tension between self-interest and altruism that Rawls' veil of ignorance seeks to address, by forcing us to imagine ourselves in the shoes of the least advantaged members of society and make decisions accordingly.

However, it is not clear whether this thought experiment truly allows us to set aside our own interests and biases. After all, even if we attempt to imagine ourselves in a hypothetical situation, we are still influenced by our unique perspectives, experiences, and emotions. Furthermore, the very act of imagining oneself as less fortunate may serve to reinforce existing power structures rather than challenge them.

Another contradiction that Rawls' theory highlights is the tension between individualism and collectivism. On one hand, we value the freedom and autonomy that come with being an individual, while on the other, we recognize that our well-being is deeply intertwined with that of others in our community. This dilemma is particularly evident when it comes to issues like health care, education, and wealth distribution. How do we strike a balance between prioritizing the needs of the individual and ensuring the common good?

Rawls' theory offers some answers to these questions, but it also raises new ones. For instance, what exactly constitutes the "least advantaged" members of society? How do we account for differences in abilities, talents, and ambitions when designing a just society? And perhaps most importantly, how can we ensure that our principles of justice are actually implemented in practice, given the many obstacles and contradictions that arise along the way?

As we continue to grapple with these questions, Rawls' work will undoubtedly remain a crucial touchstone for those seeking to build a more just and equitable world. His thoughtful exploration of human nature and the principles that underlie a fair society has sparked important conversations and challenged us to think more deeply about our values and priorities.

Yet, there is much more work to be done. Rawls' theory serves as a starting point for further discussion, rather than a definitive solution. It forces us to confront the contradictions within ourselves and in society, and invites us to engage in the messy, complex process of building a more just world. As we continue this journey, let us remember that progress is never easy or straightforward - it requires patience, perseverance, and a willingness to challenge our assumptions and biases.

**SEOUL: Financial Sector Braces for Activist Investor Surge**

South Korean conglomerates are bracing for an increase in activist investors, following recent trends in global finance. These investors, known for pushing for corporate changes to boost shareholder value, have been on the rise, particularly in developed markets like the US and Europe.

Last week, a foreign fund, Greenfield Capital Partners, publicly criticized Samsung C&T's management over its perceived lack of transparency and underperforming shares. The move has sparked fears among local conglomerates about potential activist intervention.

"We need to be prepared for the possibility of increased activism," said a senior executive at Hyundai Heavy Industries, one of South Korea's largest conglomerates. "It's not a matter of if, but when."

Local firms are taking note. POSCO, another conglomerate, has appointed a US-based corporate governance expert to its board, aiming to mitigate potential activist pressure.

Activist investors typically target underperforming companies with high levels of debt or weak governance structures. In South Korea, where family-controlled conglomerates dominate the market and often prioritize group interests over shareholder returns, these factors are not uncommon.

However, local firms are showing signs of adapting. Hyundai Motor, for instance, has been actively addressing governance issues, including increasing transparency and streamlining its board structure.

Looking ahead, the rise of activist investors could lead to more shareholder-friendly policies in South Korea's corporate world. This shift could potentially benefit minority shareholders but may also trigger conflicts between activists and management teams. It's a trend worth watching as the financial landscape continues to evolve.

Dear Reader,

Have you ever found yourself pondering the enigma of utopian thinking? A dream, a vision, a yearning for a perfect society - something we humans have grappled with since the dawn of civilization. It's fascinating to observe how these ideals have risen and fallen, challenging our traditional assumptions along the way.

Take Plato's Republic, for instance. He proposed a utopian society governed by philosopher-kings, where non-believers were subjected to extreme thought control. Fast forward to the 20th century, and we witness the dreadful regimes of Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia - stark reminders that forcing uniform beliefs on everyone causes great harm.

Yet, the debate persists. Is belief in God essential for a harmonious society? Or does non-belief lead us down a darker path? Social scientists offer scant help in this matter, with surveys suggesting that religious people are less prone to gossiping and more likely to give to charity. However, less religious countries often boast higher levels of wellbeing.

The question remains: Would we be better off if everyone believed in God or if no one did? There is no easy answer; we must allow individuals the freedom to make their own choices, despite the tension it may cause.

Dawkins' provocative claim that teaching religion is a form of child abuse resonates with me. If you have weighed the evidence and come out believing, then I would say, morally, you should believe. But weighing the evidence means taking science seriously - something many religious believers are hesitant to do.

Yet, I find myself drawn to faith not for its propositions or rules, but for the atmosphere it creates. A faith that changes how we perceive and experience the world. This is what struck me while gazing upon Angelico's paintings in San Marco. It made me wonder: Is it possible to maintain a sense of the sacred, even in the absence of belief?

These thoughts linger, unresolved, as I continue my exploration of utopian thinking and its challenges to our traditional assumptions. I am left with more questions than answers, but isn't that the essence of human curiosity? To wonder, to question, to seek, to learn - to strive for a better understanding of this intricate tapestry we call life.

And its attraction seems unlikely to dwindle at least while the origin of life — the unsolved puzzle that drives Nagel’s thinking — is still mysterious. There is still no convincing answer to the question of how, as the physicist Vlatko Vedral put it in his essay ‘What life wants’ for this magazine, one might reconcile biology and quantum physics in order to explain the ‘purposefulness’ of living things. In Mind and Cosmos, Nagel’s suggested teleology does not involve a creator; it is merely a law-like tendency in the universe that somehow loads the dice in favour of the appearance of consciousness. In this conception, Nagel writes, ‘things happen because they are on a path that leads to certain outcomes’. (It is important that other laws of nature be non-deterministic, as quantum physics arguably implies they are, in order for the teleological law to have some purchase over events.) As Nagel puts it, it might be that the universe exhibits ‘a bias toward the marvellous’. If so, it would not be surprising that consciousness had appeared, because we live in a universe whose very purpose, aim, or telos, is the production of consciousness. If anything, this sounds quite comfortingly mystical so far. However, what particularly infuriated critics was Nagel’s attitude towards evolutionary biology. It is his doubts about evolutionary theory which motivate the teleological speculations. It is true that Nagel is far too polite to the creationists of the ‘Intelligent Design’ movement, who still cleave to a supernaturalist version of teleology that requires a god-creator; their arguments, he writes, ‘are of great interest’. On the contrary, ID’s arguments have been comprehensively and repeatedly demolished — for instance, during the humiliation at the 2005 Dover Area School District trial of one of the ID theorists whom Nagel admires, Michael Behe. Perhaps this aspect of Nagel’s book might be excused as a sin of misplaced intellectual charity. More troubling is his fondness for the argument from personal incredulity. ‘It is prima facie highly implausible,’ he intuits, ‘that life as we know it is the result of a sequence of physical accidents together with the mechanism of natural selection’. text: Many academic fields can be said to ‘study morality’. Of these, the philosophical sub-discipline of normative ethics studies morality in what is arguably the least alienated way. Rather than focusing on how people and societies think and talk about morality, normative ethicists try to figure out which things are, simply, morally good or bad, and why. The philosophical sub-field of meta-ethics adopts, naturally, a ‘meta-’ perspective on the kinds of enquiry that normative ethicists engage in. It asks whether there are objectively correct answers to these questions about good or bad, or whether ethics is, rather, a realm of illusion or mere opinion. Most of my work in the past decade has been in meta-ethics. I believe that there are truths about what’s morally right and wrong. I believe that some of these truths are objective or, as they say in the literature, ‘stance-independent’. That is to say, it’s not my or our disapproval that makes torture morally wrong; torture is wrong because, to put it simply, it hurts people a lot. I believe that these objective moral truths are knowable, and that some people are better than others are at coming to know them. You can even call them ‘moral experts’ if you wish. Of course, not everyone agrees with all of that. Some are simply confused; they conflate ‘objective’ with ‘culturally universal’ or ‘innate’ or ‘subsumable under a few exceptionless principles’ or some such. But many people’s misgivings about moral objectivity are more clear headed and deeper. In particular, I find that some demur because they think that, for there to be moral truths, let alone objective, knowable ones, morality would have to have a kind of ‘foundation’ that, in their view, is nowhere to be found. Others, anxious to help, try to show that there’s a firm foundation or ultimate ground for morality after all. It’s my view that both sides of this conflict are off on the wrong foot. Morality is objective, but it neither requires nor admits of a foundation. It just kind of floats there, along with the evaluative realm more generally, unsupported by anything else. Parts of it can be explained by other parts, but the entirety of the web or network of good and evil is brute. Maybe you think that’s weird and even worthy of outright dismissal. I once thought the same thing. The purpose of this essay, which is based on my book Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System (2022), is to encourage you to start seeing this aspect of the world as I now see it. The first question we should ask is: what exactly is a ‘foundation’? We can get clearer on what a foundation is by querying whether a moral theory like utilitarianism might count as one. Utilitarianism says that actions are right to the extent, and only to the extent, that they promote overall wellbeing. So, is

As I sit here, sipping my morning coffee and watching the sun rise over the city, my mind wanders to a topic that has long intrigued me - the role of empathy in ethical decision-making. It's an early, quiet moment, the kind that invites deep thought, much like the octopus in its underwater world, moving gracefully and contemplatively among the coral reefs.

I recall a time when I was faced with a difficult decision at work, one that required me to balance the needs of my team against the demands of our clients. In that moment, I remember feeling overwhelmed by the weight of responsibility, the pressure to make the right choice. And it was empathy, more than anything else, that guided me.

Empathy, as I see it, is not just about feeling for another person, but understanding their situation, their needs, their pain. It's about stepping into their shoes and seeing the world through their eyes, if only for a moment. And in that moment of empathy, we can begin to make sense of the chaos, to find clarity amidst the confusion.

But there are limits to language, as I have come to understand. We can't always put our thoughts into words, can't always express the complexity of our emotions. And yet, it is through empathy that we can begin to bridge those gaps, to find common ground with others, even when words fail us.

In today's society, empathy is more important than ever. We live in a world where diversity is celebrated, but also where differences can create divisions. It's easy to slip into the trap of thinking only about ourselves, of forgetting that every person we encounter has their own story, their own struggles, their own dreams.

But when we make an effort to understand others, when we take the time to listen and learn, we open ourselves up to a world of possibility. We find that our own problems are not so insurmountable, that our own pain is not unique. And in that shared understanding, we can begin to build bridges, to forge connections that transcend the superficial differences between us.

Empathy is not always easy, of course. It requires effort, patience, and a willingness to be vulnerable. But it is a muscle that can be strengthened, a skill that can be honed over time. And as we grow more adept at empathy, we become better equipped to navigate the complex ethical dilemmas that life throws our way.

I think of the octopus again, moving through its world with grace and purpose, adapting to its surroundings in a way that humans can only dream of. And I wonder what metaphors for time it might use, how it might structure its representation of past, present, and future. If we were to adopt octopus-inspired metaphors for time, our relationship to the past might become more meaningful, allowing us to see the patterns that connect our lives, to understand the causal links between events.

But perhaps that is a topic for another day, another moment of contemplation. For now, I will return to my coffee and my thoughts, grateful for the gift of empathy, and the role it plays in shaping not just my decisions, but my very understanding of the world around me.

text:
The quest for understanding  whether it's about politics, morality, or the very nature of consciousness  has been a human preoccupation since time immemorial. Yet, we often find ourselves at the edge of the abyss when contemplating if machines can truly grasp what it means to understand.

I remember a conversation I had with my daughter about artificial intelligence, as she was growing up fascinated by it. She asked me, "Dad, will robots ever really think and feel like us?" And I found myself struggling to answer, not because I didn't know the technical aspects of AI, but because the question delved into the depths of human cognition that we barely understand ourselves.

In our quest to create machines that mimic human intelligence, we've made remarkable strides. Machine learning algorithms can recognize patterns in data, predict outcomes, and even generate creative content indistinguishable from human-made work. But do these machines truly understand? They can follow rules, learn from experience, and adapt to new situations  but is that understanding?

Consider a self-driving car navigating through traffic. It doesn't 'understand' the road signs or the other cars around it in the way a human does. It follows predefined algorithms that have been trained on vast amounts of data to recognize patterns and make decisions based on those patterns. Yet, when a child runs into the street, the car slams on its brakes  not because it 'understands' the danger, but because it has been programmed to react in certain ways to specific stimuli.

So, where does that leave us? Are we merely creating sophisticated machines that can simulate aspects of human intelligence without truly understanding anything? Or is there a point where these machines will cross the threshold into genuine comprehension?

These questions are not just academic musings. As our reliance on AI grows, so too does the need to understand its limitations and capabilities. If we cannot answer whether machines can truly understand, how can we ensure they act ethically, responsibly, and in ways that align with human values? And if we do manage to create a machine that understands  what does that mean for our own understanding of ourselves?

These are questions that linger in the air like unanswered riddles. As we continue to explore the boundaries of artificial intelligence, these questions become increasingly important. Perhaps one day, we will find answers that surprise even us. Until then, we remain at the edge of the abyss, peering into the darkness, wondering if machines can ever truly understand  or if understanding is a uniquely human trait.
