In the heart of the summer, I found myself standing on the edge of a precipice, gazing at the vast expanse below. On one hand, I held a glowing smartphone filled with images of melting ice caps and scorched forests - stark reminders of the climate crisis looming over us. On the other, I clutched tightly to the warm embrace of an old fossil fuel-powered car, a symbol of convenience and comfort that I was reluctant to let go. This paradox left me feeling like Sisyphus, forever pushing a boulder uphill, only for it to roll back down each time.

The ethics of climate change and our responsibility towards future generations is a labyrinthine conundrum, one that defies simple solutions. As I contemplated the dilemma, I thought about my own actions - driving a car, eating meat, consuming products packaged in plastic. Each choice seemed to lead me down a path where I was either contributing to the problem or sacrificing something I valued.

Take, for instance, my diet. A few months ago, I made a half-hearted attempt at veganism. It felt right, like aligning myself with a moral high ground. But then came the voice of doubt - the need for protein, the fear of vulnerability during a pandemic, and the lure of a well-cooked steak. So, I found myself oscillating between vegan and vegetarian, never fully committing to either, much like my commitment to the climate cause.

The choices we make are not always clear-cut. Take Ren Descartes' dog, Grat - a symbol of companionship yet a reflection of a philosopher who underestimated the inner lives of nonhuman animals. Today, we stand on the precipice of understanding animal consciousness and its implications for our actions. The question is, do we have the courage to challenge our preconceived notions about the world and our role in it?

The idea that consciousness is a uniquely human attribute persists in some circles, despite evidence suggesting otherwise. Insects, long considered mere automatons, might possess an inner life we are yet to fully comprehend. It's a humbling thought, one that forces us to question our place in the grand scheme of things.

As I stood there, I felt a sense of resignation - the weight of responsibility was heavy on my shoulders. But then, a spark of hope ignited within me. For if we can open ourselves up to the possibility that the world is more complex than we once believed, perhaps we can also embrace the idea that our actions matter, no matter how small they seem.

And so, I find myself at a crossroads - contemplating my role in this great cosmic dance, wondering what steps I should take next. The path ahead is unclear, but I know that it starts with understanding, compassion, and the willingness to change. As I walk along this path, I hope to meet others who share my journey, for together we can push that boulder uphill, one step at a time.

Were they not active participants in their own histories, shaping their destinies within the constraints imposed upon them?

The Iron Curtain was no impenetrable barrier, and western radio stations like Radio Free Europe served as conduits for ideas and information. These stations, funded by ordinary US citizens and the CIA, presented themselves as beacons of truth in a sea of propaganda. Yet, were they not biased by their anti-Communist ideology? Their broadcasts may not have been fake news, but were they not colored by their perspective?

As we ponder these questions, we are confronted with the paradoxical nature of human behavior. We seek to understand and explain it through philosophical theories, only to realize that our understanding is limited, our explanations incomplete. Perhaps the true influence of philosophical theories lies not in shaping human behavior but in prompting us to question, to challenge, to seek deeper meaning.

And so, the questions remain: To what extent are our actions determined by philosophical theories? Are they merely echoes or genuine influences? text: impossible. The results in the economic realm are all too obvious. While other countries have succeeded to a degree in bucking the trend toward financial oligopoly, the US has given it free reign. The system continues tottering forward because no one is able to come up with a viable alternative. The US is thus a strange combination of might and weakness; a country able to project military firepower to the far corners of the globe yet incapable of addressing its own basic structural weaknesses. It insists that its 18th century governing system is flawless and loudly thumps its chest at anyone who dares say otherwise. Yet anyone who has bothered to look into the matter knows that it just isn’t so. Although the US claims to have written the book on democracy, the fact is that ‘we the people’ have never had an opportunity to vote on the complicated checks and balances created in their name. To be sure, Americans elected delegates to special state ratification conventions that eventually gave the document their assent. But as the historian Forrest McDonald pointed out half a century ago, women, blacks, native Americans, and non-property owners were all barred from voting while three-fourths of those eligible did not even bother to take part. This hardly qualifies as a ringing democratic endorsement. Rather than a sign of strength, endless hosannas to American exceptionalism amount to so much whistling past the graveyard. Romney might tell the world that he ‘reject[s] the view that America must decline’ and proclaim his faith in American exceptionalism instead. But what can he do exactly to make the process stop? When the US columnist Ramesh Ponnuru says that decadent Europeans ‘are just waiting for someone to turn out the last light in the last gallery of the Louvre’ whereas ‘Americans are not prepared to go gentle into that good night’, he misses the point. What makes Dylan Thomas’ lines so poignant is that, no matter how much one rages against the dying of the light, it will flicker out regardless. If the phrase ‘American exceptionalism’ seems to be popping up more and more frequently, it is because many in the US are in denial about something that is by now glaringly apparent. Nothing could be sillier than the notion of strolling into the 21st century with a pre-modern plan of government. It’s like sending an 18th century man-of-war into battle against a guided-missile destroyer. The US political system’s age, in other words, is showing.

Title: A Disappointing Breakfast on a Special Day

I've always cherished the mornings when I could wake up early and enjoy a leisurely breakfast out. This weekend, my husband and I decided to celebrate our anniversary with a special brunch spot that was buzzing with activity - it seemed like everyone in town wanted to start their day here.

However, what arrived was a limp and soggy tortilla filled with tasteless scrambled eggs and an undercooked sausage link. The avocado, which should have been the highlight, was overripe and mushy. My husband's French toast was even worse - dry and hard, more like cardboard than a comforting breakfast treat.

The service didn't improve much either. Our waters weren't refilled, and when we asked for some orange juice, our server seemed annoyed rather than accommodating. We were in no rush to leave, but the lackluster meal and unfriendly staff made us feel unwelcome.

On a brighter note, the weather outside was beautiful, and seeing people enjoying their mornings added to my disappointment because I couldn't help but think about how much better our experience could have been if only this place lived up to its reputation. This anniversary breakfast will be one I won't forget, but for all the wrong reasons. If you're looking for a good meal on a special occasion, I'd suggest skipping this spot and finding somewhere else instead.

It turns out these considerations around cost, signal-to-noise ratio and compressibility are universal criteria that almost all communication systems must meet. But the firefly system, with a traceable mode of communication and the ability to track the signals from all individuals in the swarm, offers unparalleled advantages over other systems that display what scientists call ‘mate-search communication’. Fireflies’ dazzling light displays are, in fact, courtship rituals: flying males announce their presence as suitable mates to the females on the ground. Their light signal is composed of a species-specific on/off light pattern repeated periodically. A good example is Photinus carolinus, a synchronous firefly species documented in the southeast region of the US. A male would fly about a metre off the ground. Every 15 seconds or so, he makes several consecutive flashes, one second apart. The female P carolinus stays closer to the ground in low vegetation. If the female is interested, she waits two seconds before making a half-second flash of her own at the third second. Flash production is a voluntary action, resulting from the well-timed release of the neurotransmitter octopamine that then triggers a chemical reaction in the insect’s lantern. The current, state-of-the-art hypothesis is that fireflies control their flashing by regulating how much oxygen goes to their light-producing organs, though whether that’s accurate is still unclear. What’s certain is that, unlike a light bulb, a firefly’s light is ‘cold light’, with only minimal energy lost as heat. It is the ‘eccentrics’, he suggests, who keep the world running through their generous supply of bold perspectives, fresh insights and new ideas. ‘Precisely because the tyranny of opinion is such as to make eccentricity a reproach, it is desirable, in order to break through that tyranny, that people should be eccentric,’ he writes. The more eccentrics there are, the better the moral and intellectual state of the world: ‘Eccentricity has always abounded when and where strength of character has abounded; and the amount of eccentricity in a society has generally been proportional to the amount of genius, mental vigour, and moral courage which it contained.’ It is this redeeming ‘eccentricity’ that contrarians possess in abundance. The novelty and sharpness of their thinking come in large part from their determination to stay outside the circle that any group, explicitly or tacitly, draws in the sand to define itself. Left out as they are, contrarians are not only in a good position to observe how herding, marginalisation and exclusion work, but they no longer have anything to lose by articulating and broadcasting their heretical views. They are what ‘public intellectuals’ should ideally be – uncompromising ‘critics of society’ – and what, in practice, very few of them are. It’s the vigour of their dissent, the force of their language, and the seriousness of their commitment – their ‘quality of opposition’, in Gide’s words – that turn them into such formidable figures. That, incidentally, is also what distinguishes genuine contrarians from mere provocateurs, for whom challenging the establishment is not a matter of intellectual duty and inner conviction, but above all a form of attention-seeking and a histrionic compulsion to entertain. The peculiar cut of the contrarians’ minds, their innate distrust of anything authoritative or established, their iconoclasm and radical separation from the society into which they were born, all conspires to give them access to a higher truth than their society can afford to hear. Contrarians don’t care for fads and fashions, authorities and hierarchies, and have little patience for the rituals of the establishment. Since they have cut off their ties with their tribe, nothing prevents them from seeing things as they are. Their dissent doesn’t only free them, it gives them new eyes. In the labyrinthine tapestry of American history, a single thread has emerged, subtly altering the narrative's very fabric. This thread, woven by the hand of Howard Zinn in 'A People's History', has challenged long-held notions and cast an unflinching light on the hidden corners of our collective past.

Raising questions about power, oppression, and injustice, Zinn's work has stirred a storm of debate among scholars, historians, and the general public alike. What if we were to question not just the events that shaped our history, but also the lens through which we perceive them?

Consider, for instance, the 'Manifest Destiny', a term that once reverberated with the ring of righteousness, a divine mandate bestowed upon a chosen people. But what if this was not a tale of exceptionalism and destiny, but one of conquest, exploitation, and violence? How would this change our understanding of America's expansion westward?

Or consider the 'American Dream', that beacon of hope, a promise of opportunity and prosperity for all. Yet, what if this dream was more a mirage than a reality, accessible only to a select few, while the masses were left to grapple with the harsh realities of life? Would we still cling to this myth as fervently as before?

These are not rhetorical questions, but invitations to delve deeper, to peer beneath the surface, and to question the narratives that have shaped our understanding of America. Zinn's work is a call to arms, urging us to challenge the status quo and to reconsider our past with fresh eyes.

But is this not also a dangerous endeavor? To question the heroes of our past, to cast doubt on the stories that have bound us together, could we not risk tearing apart the very fabric of our nation? These are questions that linger, unanswered, in the back of my mind as I grapple with Zinn's provocative arguments.

In the grand tapestry of history, Zinn's thread is but one among many. Yet, its impact is undeniable, a reminder that our past is not set in stone, but a living, breathing entity, open to reinterpretation and revision. It is a call to arms, a challenge to question, to probe, and to reconsider. And in doing so, we may find ourselves not only understanding our past better, but also shaping our future.

Title: A Disappointing Brunch at BuzzBrunch on a Busy Weekend 

I've heard great things about BuzzBrunch, so when my friends suggested we try it out for brunch, I was excited. But let me tell you, the experience left much to be desired.

We arrived at around 11 AM on a bustling Saturday morning. The place was packed, and the background music was loud enough to make conversation difficult. Not a great start, but I thought, "It's just noise, it won't affect the food." Boy, was I wrong!

Firstly, let me address the elephant in the room - their famous waffles. I ordered the Blueberry Lemon Waffle with fresh whipped cream and a side of bacon. The waffle itself was overcooked and hard, not at all fluffy as promised. And oh, the bacon! It was greasy, limp, and had an unpleasant taste that lingered long after I'd finished eating.

My friend opted for the Avocado Toast with poached eggs. Now, avocado toast isn't usually my thing, but she loves it, so I tried a bite. The avocado was mushy and had an off-putting taste that made me wonder if it was actually ripe. The eggs were overcooked, and the bread was dry.

But here's the strange thing - we both loved their Mimosa! The fresh orange juice and bubbly champagne were a delightful combination. So at least there's one positive note from our brunch experience.

I'd been looking forward to this outing, but unfortunately, it turned into a disappointing meal. I won't be rushing back anytime soon, that's for sure. If you're in the area and need a drink, their Mimosa is worth trying, but if you're after good food, look elsewhere.

In the realm of ethical decision-making, empathy emerges as a crucial yet challenging factor. It's like a delicate dance between two individuals, each trying to navigate their own desires and needs while acknowledging the other's presence. Empathy, in its essence, is about understanding another person's feelings, thoughts, or attitudes from their perspective. This seemingly simple concept can shake the foundations of traditional assumptions, for it compels us to consider not just our own interests but those of others as well.

Consider Alice, a manager in a bustling tech company. Traditional decision-making might dictate that she prioritize projects with the highest profit margins, disregarding the impact on her team's morale or work-life balance. However, empathy encourages her to consider her team's wellbeing, understanding their struggles and concerns. This shift in perspective challenges the assumption that profit alone should dictate decision-making, suggesting instead that ethics and empathy are intertwined.

Yet, this dance of empathy is not without its complexities. Empathy can sometimes blur the lines between one's own feelings and those of others, leading to decisions based on misinterpreted or exaggerated emotions. This is where doubt creeps in. How do we ensure that our empathy is genuine and beneficial, rather than misguided or detrimental?

As I grapple with these questions, I am reminded of a quote from the philosopher Immanuel Kant: "It is impossible to think otherwise than rationally if only you are determined to think." Perhaps, then, the answer lies in striking a balance between empathy and reason. By using our cognitive abilities to discern the truth behind others' feelings, we can make decisions that are both ethical and compassionate.

The dance of empathy continues, its steps shifting and evolving with each encounter. It challenges traditional assumptions, forcing us to reconsider our approach to decision-making. As I delve deeper into this topic, I am left with more questions than answers, but I am certain of one thing: the role of empathy in ethical decision-making is a dance worth learning.

Proponents of levelling up can’t simply assume the answer is yes, when that is the very question at issue. Here is one reason to think levelling down is appropriate. While the US criminal justice system treats Black and brown people worse than it treats whites, it seems plausible that the treatment of white people adheres more closely to our society’s views of justice, since white people are thought to represent the ‘normal’ or the default. People of colour, then, are subject to a surplus penalty rather than white people being let off easy. If that is so, reducing the penalties Black people pay rather than increasing those of whites would bring us closer to justice. You cannot reasonably believe that someone should spend their whole life in prison unless you believe they will always present a significant threat to public safety, or that they deserve such a sentence. I have already argued that the overwhelming majority of the more than 200,000 people currently serving life sentences in the US do not present a lifelong threat to public safety. Whether they ought to spend their whole lives in prison depends, then, solely on whether they deserve it. They can deserve it only if they are fully responsible for their criminal acts. In the law, responsibility is best understood by what it excludes. One who is not responsible (liable) for their criminal act has a partial or complete excuse. A complete excuse, making one wholly nonculpable, has required, since at least the 19th century, that one not know what one is doing, not know it is wrong, or not be able to control one’s actions (be under the influence of an irresistible impulse). Under these definitions, most people serving life sentences are probably responsible for their acts. Growing up in environments of deprivation increases the likelihood that a person will go on to commit crimes But a broader understanding of responsibility is essential. For one thing, the insanity defence is a complete excuse that renders a person legally innocent (which doesn’t mean they walk free; they are likely to be institutionalised in a psychiatric facility). But there are many more whose responsibility is diminished, even though not entirely absent. The Death Penalty Information Center in its 2021 report found that: [A]ll but one of the 11 people executed in 2021 had one or more significant impairments, including: evidence of mental illness; brain injury, developmental brain damage, or an IQ in the intellectually disabled range; or chronic serious childhood trauma, neglect, and/or abuse. There is every reason to believe that those sentenced to life also have extremely high rates of impairments. Many have experienced abuse, neglect, and trauma. By

In the quiet corner of a bustling coffee shop, nestled between the hum of chattering patrons and clinking cutlery, I find myself lost in thought. The steam from my cup swirls gently before me, mirroring the complexities that occupy my mind. Today, I wish to explore the profound impact of neuroplasticity on human potential - an idea as vast and intricate as the universe itself.

Neuroplasticity, the brain's extraordinary ability to reorganize connections among its cells, has long captivated scientists, philosophers, and everyday individuals alike. But what does it mean for us, in the grand scheme of things? How does this elusive concept shape our lives, mold our personalities, and define our destinies?

Consider a man named John. A passionate musician with dreams of composing symphonies that would echo through the ages, he spent countless hours practicing his craft. Day in and day out, he worked tirelessly to perfect his skills, each note etching itself into the very fabric of his being. Through these repetitive actions, John's brain was reshaping itself - adapting, rewiring, growing stronger in the areas dedicated to musical expertise. In this way, neuroplasticity played a pivotal role in John's journey towards mastery, shaping him into the accomplished musician he would eventually become.

But let us not forget that our brains are also shaped by experiences far more mundane - and often, far less pleasant. Take, for instance, an encounter with fear. Imagine yourself standing at the edge of a dark forest, the air thick with tension and uncertainty. A sudden rustle in the bushes sends a shiver down your spine, and your heart pounds in your chest as adrenaline courses through your veins. Your brain, in that moment, is hard at work, laying down new connections that will forever alter the way you perceive and respond to danger. In this way, neuroplasticity plays a role in shaping our fears, limiting our potential, and defining our boundaries.

These examples serve as mere glimpses into the vast and complex landscape of human potential shaped by neuroplasticity. As we delve deeper into this fascinating topic, we find ourselves confronted with questions that challenge our very understanding of what it means to be human. How does our brain adapt when faced with trauma or adversity? How can we harness its power to overcome obstacles and unlock new possibilities? And perhaps most importantly, how can we use this knowledge to shape a brighter future for ourselves and generations to come?

In contemplating these questions, I am reminded of a conversation I had with a dear friend - a neuroscientist who had dedicated her life to unraveling the mysteries of the human brain. We spoke at length about the potential applications of neuroplasticity research, discussing everything from the treatment of mental illnesses to the enhancement of cognitive abilities. It was during this discussion that I realized the true power of neuroplasticity lies not only in its ability to shape our past but also in its capacity to transform our future.

As we continue to explore the vast and complex landscape of human potential shaped by neuroplasticity, let us remember that each of us holds within ourselves an infinite reservoir of untapped potential - a reservoir waiting to be tapped, reshaped, and transformed through the power of neuroplasticity. It is up to us, then, to choose how we will harness this power - whether we will use it to overcome adversity, conquer our fears, or reach for the stars. The possibilities are as limitless as the cosmos themselves.

In closing, I am reminded of a quote by the poet Rumi: "Let the beauty we love be what we do." As we continue to explore the mysteries of neuroplasticity and its impact on human potential, let us strive to be agents of change - using our newfound understanding to shape a brighter, more fulfilling future for ourselves and those around us. For it is in this pursuit that we find not only the keys to unlocking our own potential but also the keys to shaping a world in which each individual has the opportunity to thrive.

text: KARACHI: Lending to private sector contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the current fiscal year of 2014/15, despite the central banks efforts to get credit flowing through thrice cut in policy rates, data showed on Tuesday. Analyst said banks hefty invested into the government papers and low borrowing costs have made little success in investment and spending by the businesses and consumers. The flow of loans to different firms fell to Rs178.9 billion between July 1, 2014 and April 3, 2015 against Rs281.9 billion during the same period of last fiscal year, the central bank said. Analysts said that bank heavy investment in the government treasury reduced the supply of lendable funds for the private sector despite falling borrowing costs. Since the start of 2014, banks have been actively placing funds in long bills and 8 in funds in PIBs on high returns. Since November 2014, the cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points following low inflation and positive outlook on external sector. The government is not firmly agreed with the analysts view that banks are not interested in extending private sector loans and parking their whole money into the government papers. The government in its latest letter of intent to the IMF said, Private sector credit continued its healthy expansion (10.2 percent so far in FY15) driven mainly by businesses, and in particular manufacturing.Some analysts foresee bank lending to start expanding in coming quarters. Within different financing segments, a moderate pickup is expected in consumer loans. The State Bank, with the help of World Bank experts, has developed a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS) to implement financial sector reforms to meet their financing needs. The central bank is likely to launch the NFIS by end ups, complex legal, taxation and border trade requirements, and impaired access to finance.

text: Copper Star is a great example of adaptive use in the heart of the Melrose curve. Who would have thought that a building initially designed to provide fuel to cars would so work so well serving fuel to humans? Their coffee is locally roasted and, while not my favorite, is well above average in Phoenix. Their drive-thru is a great feature for those mornings when I'm running late. For the coffee adverse, Copper Star serves chai, tea, smoothies, Italian sodas, and some unique drinks including a black cherry limeade. Foodwise, there is a wide selection of baked goods,including some of the best bagels. They also have decent lunch offerings, such as sandwiches and a tasty hummus platter. But where they shine is their 'home baked' cupcakes. (I've seen Mayor Gordon here on several occasions getting his cupcake fix!) Despite being in a converted gas station/garage, the atmosphere is quite a bit more cozy than most of the other coffee shops in central Phoenix. While the sandblasted block walls and exposed ceiling let that building's industrial past peak through, this is offset by rotating art work and selection of comfortable (if a bit dingy) couches and easy chairs as well as small bistro tables and long 'work' tables to choose from. The staff is friendly and helpful, and the owner, Bill, is a great neighborhood resource. He can tell you anything and everything going on along 7th Ave. All in all a good place to grab a coffee and cupcake, meet a friend or get some old fashioned reading done. I can't recommend it for serious work requiring internet access, however, as the wifi access remains intermittent at best.

text:
As I ponder the current state of our world, I can't help but feel a sense of urgency, a weight upon my shoulders. We find ourselves at a crossroots, teetering on the edge of an abyss. Climate change, biodiversity loss, pandemics - these aren't just buzzwords anymore; they're stark realities that threaten our very existence.

It seems we've stumbled upon some sort of cosmic game, where the stakes are nothing less than our survival. Yet, this isn't a new story. Throughout history, humanity has always been on the brink, always teetering on the edge of extinction. What makes this moment different? Is it the sheer magnitude of the threats we face? Or is it something deeper, something inherent within us?

I've been thinking about empathy and altruism, these curious human traits that seem to defy logic and reason. They're not just the product of social conditioning or religious indoctrination. No, they're rooted deep within our biology, buried in our DNA, whispering tales of survival, of unity.

Empathy, the ability to understand and share the feelings of another, is a powerful tool that allows us to connect, to unite. It's not just about feeling compassion for a suffering friend or family member; it's about recognizing the interconnectedness of all life, the delicate balance that keeps our world spinning. When we empathize with the suffering of the Earth, when we feel the pain of the forests burning, the oceans choking, the animals dying - that's when we truly understand the gravity of our situation.

Altruism, the selfless concern for the well-being of others, is another trait that puzzles many. Why would a species driven by survival instincts put the needs of others before their own? Yet, it's this very trait that has allowed us to build societies, to work together, to create a world beyond what any one individual could achieve alone. It's the glue that holds us together, the thread that stitches our collective tapestry.

But we can't just rely on these traits to save us now. We must dig deeper, explore further. We must understand not only why we care, but how we can act upon that care. It's no longer enough to simply empathize with the suffering of the world; we must take action. And it's no longer enough to simply feel compassion for our fellow humans; we must extend that compassion to the rest of the natural world as well.

As I continue to explore these ideas, I can't help but wonder: if empathy and altruism are indeed rooted in our biology, then what does that mean for our future? Are we destined to self-destruct, or are we capable of rising above our baser instincts, of creating a world that truly embodies these traits?

In our modern world, we're grappling with an issue that transcends generations - climate change. It's a complex web, intricately woven into the very fabric of life on Earth. Yet, it's often treated like a distant concern, a problem for future generations to solve. But what if I told you that our actions today are shaping not just their tomorrow, but ours as well?

Consider this: The climate is a delicate balance, much like the fragile interplay between biology and quantum physics. We're still trying to figure out how these seemingly disparate fields can coexist, let alone explain the 'purposefulness' of our planet. Some suggest that the universe has a bias towards the marvellous, nudging us towards consciousness. If so, it's hard not to see climate change as a stark reminder of our role in this grand cosmic scheme.

Take evolutionary biology, for instance. We often speak of traits 'evolving in order to' allow organisms to adapt and survive. But is that merely anthropomorphizing the natural world? The truth lies somewhere in between. Evolution isn't about purpose; it's about survival. Yet, the consequences of evolutionary changes have profound implications for our planet.

Consider the information that defines life. It's not some mystical force; it's a delicate balance, a dance between genetics and environment. This information is what separates the living from the non-living. But unlike Louis Pasteur's vital force, this one isn't static. It evolves, adapts, over time and space. To understand climate change, we must grasp how this information - the very essence of life - adapts to a changing environment.

Information, in the Shannon sense, is about reducing uncertainty. A weather report telling you it will rain reduces your uncertainty about whether it will or not. In our planet's case, climate change is an uncertain future, a question mark hanging over our heads. And yet, we keep flipping coins, making choices that increase the odds of a catastrophic outcome.

The challenge lies in putting meaning back into information, in understanding how the reduction of uncertainty can shape the very fabric of life on Earth. Climate change isn't just about melting ice caps or rising sea levels; it's about the survival of our species, the future of our planet. It forces us to reconsider our values, to prioritize sustainability over short-term gains.

As we grapple with climate change, we're confronted with a stark reality: Our actions today will define the world tomorrow. We can no longer afford to be passive observers; we must be active participants in shaping our planet's future. Climate change isn't just about the future; it's about us, right now. It's about reconsidering our values, redefining our purpose, and recognizing that the universe's 'bias towards the marvellous' might just be our call to action.

text: the classroom, I learned to put away my body. Outside of the academy, however, specifically through fanfiction, I was learning to read with it. I discovered fanfiction in 2001, when I was 16, unhappy at school, and frequently mistaken for a boy in women’s bathrooms. I’d joined a number of web mailing listservs, desperate to make friends, and I remember vividly the first time one of the fanfiction writers I followed posted a story she’d written about a character from The Lord of the Rings. I was staggered. It was like nothing I’d ever read before. Suddenly, I had a word for a genre that I had known intimately for years. I’d always fantasised about stepping into books and having adventures with my favourite characters. Now I wrote them down and shared them. Strangers left positive comments on each chapter. I discovered that I could write. Later, I learned that there was a name for this kind of story: ‘Mary Sue’. The name comes from the short satire ‘A Trekkie’s Tale’ (1973), written by the mathematician Paula Smith and first published in a Star Trek fanzine to parody the flood of (in her opinion) terrible fanfiction stories in which all-American teenagers with names such as Mary Sue stride into the world of the Starship: Enterprise and win Captain Kirk’s fickle heart. Smith’s parody is embarrassingly similar to my first story, and thousands like it online, written by young women experimenting with fanfiction. But as I read more widely, I discovered a rich vein of sophisticated and brilliantly written fanfiction, offering insights into characters I knew and loved. While bingeing on Harry Potter fanfiction, I was taking courses at high school, then university, in classical and medieval literature. I learned Latin, and read Virgil, Cicero and St Augustine in the original language. I wrote cold, sober, critical essays, but stayed up late into the night with fanfiction, my guilty pleasure, seemingly as far removed from the study of literature as it was possible to be. I moved to Canada to go to graduate school. By then I knew that fantasising about meeting Geoffrey Chaucer, or imagining what Queen Eleanor of Aquitaine might do if she met Sir Lancelot, was not the way serious readers did it. But I struggled with cognitive dissonance, since it was obvious to me that fanfiction had made me good at reading. Fanfiction makes its source texts richer for its loving readers. It amplifies allusions and hidden currents, pulls out notes of characterisation and subtleties of plot, and spends time with them. After reading fanfiction, I return to texts I love with a new eye – sometimes a more critical one. For example, I read hundreds of stories embroidering the relationship between the Harry Potter characters Remus Lupin and Sirius Black, which – fanfiction writers suggested – was the real reason Sirius’s family had thrown him out.

text:
Neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to reorganize and adapt throughout one's lifespan, is a fascinating and complex topic. It's like a puzzle piece that keeps fitting into new places, revealing unexpected connections. But, how does this intricate process influence human potential? Let me try to unravel some threads here, though I must admit, it feels like chasing shadows in a dark room.

Take the example of a child with dyslexia. Traditionally, we'd see their reading difficulties as a deficit. But neuroplasticity suggests that with the right training and stimulation, their brains can rewire themselves to process language more effectively. This is not just about overcoming a weakness; it's about tapping into untapped potential - the child learning to 'read' their world differently.

On the flip side, consider an athlete who's spent years perfecting their craft. Their brains have been sculpted by countless hours of practice, forming efficient neural pathways for motor skills. But what happens when they suffer an injury? Can their brain reorganize itself to compensate for the loss, or will it be stuck in its old grooves, unable to adapt?

These examples raise intriguing questions about human potential - not just what we can do, but how we can change and grow. But here's where things get tricky: Neuroplasticity is still a relatively new field, and we don't fully understand all its nuances. There are so many variables at play - genetics, environment, experience, to name a few. It feels like trying to catch water in a net.

Moreover, when we talk about neuroplasticity as a tool for improving human potential, it's essential to consider ethical implications. For instance, if we can manipulate brain structure to enhance cognitive abilities, are we playing God? And who gets access to these enhancements - the wealthy elite or everyone equally?

As I grapple with these questions, I'm left with more questions than answers. But that's part of the allure of neuroplasticity - it challenges us to rethink our understanding of human potential and pushes us to explore new territories. It's a puzzle worth solving, even if the pieces keep shifting under our fingers.

There’s neither reason in history nor some kind of Hegelian teleology or theodicy. History itself is without meaning or direction. Only historical analysis, theoretically informed, might begin to discern the pattern of its ‘absurdity’ and ‘abysmalness’. The analytical grid that Koselleck suggested as the baseline of his theory of history – before and after, below and above, inner and outer – also conditions historical writing itself. It’s crucial for the viewpoint of historians, whether they are a contemporary or born later, and are thus an eyewitness or a retrospective narrator of events, respectively; whether they are situated higher or lower in social or political terms, for example, whether they are on the side of the winners or losers of the conflict they are describing; and, finally, whether they belong to the political, religious, social or economic community whose history they are writing about, identifying themselves more or less critically with it, or whether they are looking on from outside. For Koselleck, the decisive distinction between his theory of history and hermeneutics (or Michel Foucault’s ‘discourse analysis’) is his insistence that not all experiences can be captured by language. As he explained in a lecture delivered in honour of his teacher Hans-Georg Gadamer in 1985: When the flexible distinction between inside and outside is intensified into an impassioned dichotomy between friend and enemy, when the relation between above and below leads to slavery and irrevocable humiliation or to exploitation and class struggle, or when the tension between the sexes leads to degradation – this is when events emerge: chains of events, cascades of events that withhold themselves from language, to which every word, every sentence, every speech can only react. It’s precisely these cascades of events that we’re struggling to put into words that interested Koselleck as a historian and theorist. Some of his most compelling essays explore the limits of language. What brings about a human? Aristotle knew that animals with two sexes create new animals from the ‘union of male and female’. So, like his predecessors, he acknowledges that males and females each contribute something to conception. But what? Aristotle assumes that male ejaculate, semen, contains seed. Closely studying semen, he argues it must be a ‘residue’ of a ‘growth nutriment’. On Aristotelian biology, nutriments contribute to a body’s natural, healthy growth. If a body does not use up nutriments, the leftover becomes a residue. Aristotle argues his semen = residue theory explains various ‘facts’. For example, healthy males produce more semen, because sick ones use up more nutriment. Children do not produce semen because they are still growing, using up their nutriment without leftovers. Because nutriment helps bodies gain weight, people and goats who are ‘getting rather too fat’ do not have nutriment leftover – explaining why they produce ‘less semen and are less desirous of sexual intercourse’. For Aristotle, blood is the most important nutriment, travelling around the body bringing life, growth. He reasons that semen must be a residue of blood. But blood is runny and red whereas (he notes) semen is ‘thick and white’. Why doesn’t semen look more like blood? Aristotle’s answer is that males ‘concoct’ blood residue, thickening and purifying it, turning it into semen. Female bodies do not concoct semen, which is why they regularly lose leftover blood – a nifty explanation for menstruation. Why do only males concoct semen? Because concoction requires heat. And, as on the Table of Opposites, Aristotle connects males-hot, females-cold. Aristotelian pregnancy is all nautical: wave froth, whirling currents, magically forming fishnets This brings us to the heart of Aristotle’s theory. He claims that male semen contributes ‘the form and the efficient cause’, while females contribute ‘the material’. Male semen forms the material provided by the female. How does he arrive at this? One reason to think females provide the material is that, after conception, the new creature grows inside its mother. As Aristotle notes, further material ‘must constantly be added’ to an embryo ‘that it may increase in size’. New creatures grow regardless of what their fathers are doing, so this material must come from their mothers. And if you think females provide the material cause of a new creature, then you had better deny they also provide the efficient cause. In the grand tapestry of human history and societal evolution, the legacy of Desmond Tutu and South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) stands as a beacon, a testament to the resilience and complexity of the human spirit. Yet, it raises questions that continue to challenge our understanding of ourselves, of justice, and of reconciliation.

Were we better off after the TRC? Or did it merely scratch the surface, leaving deep-seated wounds unattended? These are questions that linger in the air, echoing in the corridors of academia, courtyards of justice, and the quiet corners of our minds.

The TRC was a bold experiment, an attempt to heal a nation by acknowledging its past atrocities and fostering forgiveness. But does such a process truly bring reconciliation? Or does it merely serve as a bandage over gaping wounds, allowing us to ignore the festering rot beneath? These are questions that I pondered during a visit to Robben Island, where Tutu and many other freedom fighters were imprisoned. The sun was setting, casting long shadows over the barren landscape, and I couldn't help but feel a sense of unease, a disquiet that echoed the turmoil within our souls.

The TRC was about truth, yes, but it was also about forgiveness, reconciliation, and healing. Yet, these are abstract concepts, elusive as shadows in the moonlight. How do we measure them? How do we know if they have truly taken root? These are questions that plague us, niggling at the edges of our consciousness, refusing to be silenced.

In the annals of history, the TRC stands as a testament to human resilience and the capacity for forgiveness. Yet, it also serves as a reminder of the depths to which we can sink, the darkness that lies within us all. It is a beacon, yes, but one that casts long shadows, reminding us of our capacity for both great good and great evil.

As I stand on the precipice, gazing into the abyss of history, I am left with more questions than answers. Yet, it is these questions that propel us forward, driving us to seek truth, to strive for reconciliation, to heal the wounds of our past. And perhaps, in the end, it is this quest, this journey, that truly defines us as humans.

In the realm of the intellectually tangled and ethereally boundless, we find ourselves at an impasse. A conundrum that has plagued the minds of many a scholar: can machines truly understand? And in our ceaseless pursuit of knowledge, does this question not shed light on the limits of human understanding itself?

Consider the humble calculator, a marvel of modernity. It can perform complex arithmetic faster than any human could dream, yet it lacks the capacity for empathy or introspection. A machine that understands not joy nor sorrow, only cold calculation. But is this not similar to our understanding of the physical world? We analyze, we measure, we quantify, but can we truly comprehend the depth and complexity of emotions, the beauty of a sunset, or the pain of heartbreak?

The question of machine understanding leads us down a labyrinthine path. To create a machine capable of understanding would require the very essence of human understanding, which is not yet fully understood by mankind. We are left with questions that feel like riddles, the answers to which lie hidden in shadows yet unexplored.

In our pursuit of artificial intelligence, we have unleashed ethical dilemmas aplenty. If a self-driving car must choose between hitting a pedestrian or swerving into oncoming traffic, who does it choose? The machine makes the choice based on algorithms, but whose ethics are those it follows? Whose moral compass is programmed within its circuits?

These questions echo in our minds like an endless echo chamber. Is it not ironic that as we strive to create machines that can understand, we are forced to confront the limitations of our own understanding? We grapple with the ethics of programming morality into machines, yet struggle with ethical dilemmas within our own societies.

As we continue this ongoing debate, let us not forget the power and potential of machines. They can process vast amounts of data at speeds unimaginable to humans, aiding us in solving complex problems that might otherwise remain unsolved. But as we embrace the digital revolution, let us also remember to question, to explore, and to ponder the deeper implications of our creations.

And so, the dance between man and machine continues, a delicate waltz of creation and discovery. As we tread this path, we are reminded that the line between understanding and misunderstanding is finer than a silicon thread, and that the answers to our questions may be as elusive as they are essential.

text:
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, the Nigerian author who burst onto the global literary scene with her critically acclaimed novels like 'Half of a Yellow Sun' and 'Americanah', has undeniably left an indelible mark on contemporary African literature. Yet, understanding her role requires navigating complex layers that extend beyond the pages of her books.

Adichie's work is a testament to the resilience and diversity of African narratives, often challenging stereotypes and offering nuanced perspectives on issues like identity, feminism, and post-colonialism. Her characters are richly drawn, with their flaws, strengths, and complexities reflecting the intricacies of the human condition. This depth and authenticity have earned her a prominent place in African literature, giving voice to stories that were long overlooked or marginalized.

However, Adichie's influence extends beyond her prose. Her powerful TED talk, 'We Should All Be Feminists,' has sparked conversations about gender equality across continents, transcending literary boundaries. Through her speeches and essays, she champions the need for a more inclusive and equitable world, emphasizing the importance of empathy, understanding, and dialogue in bridging cultural divides.

But, as with any influential figure, Adichie's role is not without controversy. Critics argue that her focus on individual narratives can overshadow systemic issues and the collective struggles of African communities. They contend that by centering personal stories, she risks reinforcing a narrative that prioritizes Western perspectives and neglects broader societal challenges.

This debate underscores the complexity of Adichie's role in contemporary African literature. On one hand, her work offers a vital platform for African voices, contributing to a global conversation about identity, culture, and power. On the other, her success within Western literary circles raises questions about the commodification of African narratives and the potential erasure of collective struggles.

As I grapple with these ideas, I find myself asking: What does it mean for an African writer to achieve international acclaim? Is it a triumph or a betrayal of the continent's literary traditions? And how can we ensure that such success is harnessed for the benefit of all African writers and readers?

These questions are far from resolved, and I find myself drawn into a labyrinth of thoughts. Adichie's role in contemporary African literature is multifaceted, challenging, and vital. Yet, understanding it requires an honest examination of both its triumphs and its pitfalls, a task that demands patience, open-mindedness, and the willingness to engage with complexities rather than oversimplifications.

In 2010, Cliodynamics, the flagship journal for this new discipline, appeared, with its very first article (by the American sociologist Randall Collins) focusing on modelling victory and defeat in battle in relation to material resources and organisational morale. In a move that paralleled Comte’s earlier argument regarding the successive stages of scientific complexity (from physics, through chemistry and biology, to sociology), Turchin passionately rejected the idea that complexity made human societies unsuitable for quantitative analysis, arguing that it was precisely that complexity which made mathematics essential. Weather predictions were once considered unreliable because of the sheer complexity of managing the necessary data. But improvements in technology (satellites, computers) mean that it’s now possible to describe mathematically, and therefore to model, interactions between the system’s various parts – and therefore to know when it’s wise to carry an umbrella. With equal force, Turchin insisted that the cliodynamic approach was not deterministic. It would not predict the future, but instead lay out for governments and political leaders the likely consequences of competing policy choices. Crucially, and again on the back of the abundantly available and cheap computer power, cliodynamics benefited from the surge in interest in the digital humanities. Existing archives were being digitised, uploaded and made searchable: every day, it seemed, more data were being presented in a format that encouraged quantification and enabled mathematical analysis – including the Old Bailey’s online database, of which Wolf had fallen foul. At the same time, cliodynamicists were repositioning themselves. Four years after its initial launch, the subtitle of their flagship journal was renamed, from The Journal of Theoretical and Mathematical History to The Journal of Quantitative History and Cultural Evolution. As Turchin’s editorial stated, this move was intended to position cliodynamics within a broader evolutionary analysis; paraphrasing the Russian-American geneticist Theodosius Dobzhansky, he claimed that ‘nothing in human history makes sense except in the light of cultural evolution’. Given Turchin’s ecological background, this evolutionary approach to history is unsurprising. But given the historical outcomes of making politics biological, it is potentially worrying. More startling perhaps is that this explicit evolutionary turn was mirrored in a broader shift towards the natural sciences on the part of (some members of) the humanities. As noted, the experiences of the Second World War, and of imperialism more generally, made Western scholars deeply wary of using biology and evolution to explain culture and society: the links to xenophobic and sexist politics seemed far too close for comfort. Attempts in the 1970s to establish biosocial rules that could apply to human and nonhuman society likewise resulted in vicious intellectual schisms that split disciplines such as anthropology down the middle. As I sat there enjoying my meal and watching the rain pour down outside, I couldn't help but smile at how much my life has changed lately. Ramen Bar Tokyo was the perfect place to unwind and reflect on all the good things happening in my life.

All in all, I can confidently say that Ramen Bar Tokyo is a must-visit for any ramen or Japanese food lover out there! The atmosphere, staff, and most importantly, the delicious food will leave you wanting to come back again and again. So next time it's pouring outside, don't hesitate - grab your umbrella and head over to Ramen Bar Tokyo!

Nonetheless, both remain iconic because Nietzsche and Popper were each on to something resonant about the relationship between myth and philosophy, and the curious symbolic role of Plato in our inherited understandings of that relationship. To some extent, both their critiques were right, in the fractured way everyone is a little right in the parable about the blind men and the elephant. Plato continues to be celebrated as a figure who set philosophy on a rational foundation by isolating a distinctly rigorous, critical mode of reasoning from the clutches of myth and received wisdom. And yet, his dialogues are also run through with myths of his own invention: the Myth of Metals, the Symposium’s account of the origin of love, and the stories about the afterlife which close the Phaedo, Gorgias and Republic. Squaring both these aspects of Plato’s thought is difficult because we remain wedded to two distinct, but related, ideas in our intellectual heritage. The first is the idea that philosophy is fundamentally opposed to myth. And the second is that it was Plato who first pointed this out. Concerns about myth are often bound up with concerns about misinformation. But these anxieties go beyond a worry about factual inaccuracies that require correction. Rather, when scholars and commentators designate something as a myth, they are usually grappling with a more existential threat: a symbolically powerful narrative that seems immune to correction altogether, but that, nonetheless, has the power to captivate our imaginations. The language of myth, for instance, has recently been used to talk about frontier imagery in US immigration policy – ‘the myth of the border wall’ – as well as nostalgic appeals to a somehow more authentic version of a nation’s people – from the ‘myth of a “real” America’, to the ‘myths of Englishness’ that made rounds during the Brexit campaign. One reason myths make us uneasy is that we expect them not to have a place at the heart of the assumptions and beliefs that found modern culture. That is, there is an expectation that we, as a society, ought to have outgrown myths. This expectation stems from an evolutionary account of culture that goes back at least to ancient Greece, when Presocratic thinkers adopted rhetoric that distinguished their methods of investigation from the oral tradition associated with ancient myths about the Olympian deities, by focusing on the unverifiable nature of such stories. After a long, grueling day at work, I found myself in desperate need of a little self-care. A quick Google search led me to this gem hidden away on the outskirts of town  Sunny Nails & Spa. I'll admit, I was skeptical at first, especially given my penchant for all things greasy. But, I decided to give it a shot, and boy, am I glad I did!

As I stepped inside, the atmosphere was nothing short of serene. The gentle hum of chatter from other patrons intermingled with soft instrumental music, creating a soothing symphony that instantly calmed my frazzled nerves. The interior was tastefully decorated  clean lines, muted colors, and comfortable seating that made me feel like I was lounging in a plush, cozy cloud.

The price point was more than reasonable, especially considering the quality of service they offered. For just $35, I opted for the Deluxe Manicure & Pedicure package  and it was worth every penny. The speed at which my technician, a petite woman with nimble fingers, worked was nothing short of amazing. She expertly trimmed, buffed, and painted each nail, all while chatting amiably about our shared love for Korean dramas.

However, there was one minor contradiction that I found intriguing. While some reviews complained about the messiness of applying polish, I actually found it therapeutic  a sensory experience that allowed me to unwind and let go of the stresses of the day. Yes, my hands were covered in nail polish remnants by the time she was done, but the satisfaction I felt as each layer was applied was well worth the mess.

To top it all off, they even included a warm cup of tea and a refreshing cucumber slice for my eyes  a thoughtful touch that really made me feel pampered and cared for. As I left Sunny Nails & Spa with freshly polished nails and a skip in my step, I knew I'd be back again soon. This little oasis of tranquility was the perfect antidote to my hectic worklife  definitely worth five stars!

With that unsettled feeling, day after day, I suspected I was more vulnerable to pathogens than usual. I expected to catch COVID-19 at some stage (as I did, a month or so later), and wanted to be physically well-equipped to fight it off. I realise that this was a very short experiment. But the moments of transition between the various diets posed some choices in a clear way. Suppose one decides that a wholly plant-based or near-vegan diet is not going to work, and something must be added. If one is looking for one step away, thinking primarily about animal welfare questions, then three options appear that have completely different kinds of justification: humanely farmed meat (especially beef)wild-caught fishdairy products (conventionally farmed)These aren’t really the only three options (I will look at others below), but they are some obvious and available ones, within a developed-world urban or suburban setting. Let’s say, initially, that the goal is to choose one of these as the step away. Which should it be? I said above that they have different kinds of justifications and, when one looks closely, something like an incommensurability appears in the situation. This term from philosophy means that you can’t measure or compare alternatives using a common standard that is fair to all of them. No suitable ‘common currency’ or measuring stick is available. These three possible ways of going shopping bring on board quite different ways of looking at the moral issues. Let’s clarify each option before looking at the ethical side. When I talk about humanely farmed beef, in option 1, I have in mind beef produced so humanely that it makes sense to think that the cows have a good life overall, and a life that is better, most likely, than the life that nearly all nonhuman mammals might have. Title: A Refreshing Experience at Juice Joint! 

Let me tell you about my recent visit to this fabulous juice bar - a hidden gem in the heart of our city. I've been meaning to check it out for quite some time, and finally got the chance during a delightful first date (but more on that later).

The ambiance was on point! The place was bustling with energy, yet not too noisy to disrupt a conversation or two. The staff were friendly, attentive, and full of positive vibes. Now, let's talk about the star of the show: the drinks! I opted for their signature Detox Delight, while my date chose the Tropical Twist. Both were nothing short of divine! The blend of fruits was so refreshing and the sheer vibrancy of the juices spoke volumes about their freshness.

As for value for money, I must say that Juice Joint offers a fantastic deal. For the quality of ingredients used and the taste experience, it's an absolute steal! Plus, they have various options catering to different dietary preferences - vegan, gluten-free, you name it!

Now, onto the story behind my visit. I've been under the weather lately, and my friend suggested a healthy juice cleanse to boost my immunity. So, here I was on a first date, sipping away at this wonder drink, hoping to make a good impression (I guess it worked!).

If you're looking for a place that serves up refreshing, nutrient-packed drinks with great service and reasonable prices, Juice Joint is the place to be. My only caveat would be to expect a bit of a wait during peak hours as the popularity of this joint is well-deserved.

text:
How do altered states shape our perception and sense of self? This question has tantalized philosophers, psychologists, and artists for centuries. While we often associate these states with extreme experiences like psychedelics or near-death encounters, they can also be found in more subtle forms such as meditation, sleep, or even daydreaming.

The impact of altered states on perception is undeniable. They often blur the boundaries between self and world, merging inner and outer realities in ways that challenge our conventional understanding. For instance, during a psychedelic trip, one might experience objects morphing and changing colors, or even perceive connections between seemingly unrelated things. These shifts can be disorienting, yet they also offer a glimpse into possible alternate realities.

Yet, the question of identity becomes murkier in these states. Who are we when our familiar sense of self dissolves? Some argue that this dissolution is merely an illusion, a temporary loss of the ego's grip on our consciousness. Others claim it reveals a deeper, truer self hidden beneath the layers of conditioning and social norms.

Take meditation, for example. Many practitioners report moments of profound clarity and self-realization during deep meditation. They speak of transcending their usual selves, only to return with renewed insight and understanding. Is this a temporary escape from our everyday identities, or a glimpse into who we truly are?

It's important to note that not everyone experiences these states in the same way. Some people might feel lost or disoriented during altered states, while others find them profoundly enlightening. This suggests that our individual histories, personalities, and beliefs play a significant role in shaping these experiences.

So, where does this leave us? Are altered states mere hallucinations, or gateways to deeper truths about ourselves and the universe? The answer may lie somewhere in between, a complex interplay of individual psychology, brain chemistry, and cultural conditioning. Yet, these states continue to fascinate us, offering tantalizing glimpses into realities beyond our everyday experiences.

As I delve deeper into this topic, I find myself questioning my own beliefs about identity and perception. Am I merely a product of my environment and upbringing, or something more? These are questions that may never have clear answers, yet they continue to shape our understanding of what it means to be human.

Lawrence is too turbulent, too meteoric for that. Instead, Lawrence was both stimulus and provocation. There was an emancipatory quality in reading him, because he never thought about what was expected of him, but examined his own thoughts by a bright, exacting light and took seriously what it might mean to think the opposite. Through all this was his deep immersion in the lives of others: his novelistic ability to observe everyone, from children to animals, closely and vividly, and to see the world as they might. Lawrence’s philosophy of parenthood was based on just-about-benign neglect. He thought we’d become too self-conscious, and that we inflicted self-consciousness on our children by loving them too much. In the essay ‘Education of the People’, written in 1918 and published posthumously, he advised that ‘babies should invariably be taken away from their modern mothers and given … to rather stupid fat women who can’t be bothered with them.’ He’s not saying we shouldn’t actually love our children, more that we shouldn’t inflict our love on them It’s tempting to dismiss Lawrence’s ideas about child-rearing, just as we dismiss his ideas about democracy (he hated it) and race (in his 1925 essay ‘Reflections on the Death of a Porcupine’, he signed up to an idea of racial hierarchy). I don’t think there can be any contemporary readers who admire everything that Lawrence wrote, and I find that it’s often necessary to separate the complex wisdom of the novels from the more glibly single-minded pushiness of the essays. So often, Lawrence used his essays to drive thoughts to their comic extreme, but I think there was value in this. He was a writer who allowed himself to play with ideas, to hold two thoughts in his head at once, to permit every intellectual position to coexist with its opposite. I’ve found this helpful in our own divided age, where it feels increasingly less possible to admit to ambivalence and contradiction. When it comes to parental love, I think there’s enough truth in the thought that we need to avoid smothering our children with love that it feels worthwhile for him to overstate it. Courtesy WikimediaThere was more to Ocean’s iconography than vases and tile floors; there were also the maps, on which Ocean appeared in his physical, non-personified form. Thanks to the historian Herodotus, we know much about how these early maps were drawn, though the drawings themselves have not survived. Surprisingly, their arrangement drew from the normally sober historian a rare moment of comic levity. ‘I laugh,’ Herodotus wrote dismissively, ‘when I see the many who have drawn maps of the Earth in a nonsensical way; they make the Earth rounder than one would draw it with a compass, and put Ocean all around the outside; and they make Europe and Asia equal in size.’ The scheme he describes could be illustrated like this: Why did Herodotus find this so funny? Indeed, I puzzled over his laughter for many months as I hacked away at what became my first published scholarly article, some three decades ago. The map has good Homeric authority behind it, since Homer had similarly placed Ocean around the rim of Achilles’s shield, described at length in Book 18 of the Iliad – a shield that, in some respects, represents a pre-cartographic image of the world. Did Herodotus really have the temerity to scorn such a time-worn mythic tradition, enshrined in the Greek world’s most revered literary text? One clue to Herodotus’s mirth, it seems to me, lies in the satiric phrase ‘rounder than one would draw it with a compass’. There’s too much perfection in this geometry. Actual features of the Earth, Herodotus seems to imply, do not form perfect circles. Achilles’s shield might have formed a circle, but the fact that Ocean stood at that shield’s perimeter has no cartographic significance. Similarly, the exact symmetry of ‘Asia’ and ‘Europe’ – taking ‘Asia’ (as many Greeks did) to mean the southern half of the world, including Africa, and ‘Europe’ the northern half – seems to Herodotus all too simple. A second problem Herodotus has with Ocean stems from the historian’s concern with the reliability of his information. No one, he asserts in a separate discussion, has ever seen the waters of Ocean lapping at northern Europe, or eastern Asia. So these two regions, for all he knows, might well be connected to some further landmasses that make the Atlantic and the Pacific – Herodotus knew of the first name, but not the second – into two disconnected bodies of water. Indeed in another passage, a discussion of the river Nile, Herodotus goes so far as to call Ocean an out-and-out lie: ‘I don’t know that any river Ocean exists, and I think that Homer or some other of the early poets invented the name and introduced it into his poetry’. text:
In our modern world, we often find ourselves romanticizing historical figures, casting them in an idealized light that glosses over their flaws and complexities. Yet, this romanticization can obscure the truth and lead us down a dangerous path of distortion and misrepresentation. Allow me to elucidate with three examples, starting with one that may surprise you.

Take Claude Monet, the Impressionist painter whose work has fetched millions at auction. We tend to think of him as a starving artist, struggling to make ends meet during his lifetime. However, little do we know that Monet was far from destitute for most of his career. He dined on exquisite cuisine and owned several automobiles, living comfortably amidst his famous gardens. In truth, he bore more resemblance to contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons than the penniless Vincent van Gogh.

The second example lies in the realm of technology and surveillance. The New Aesthetic, a movement that emerged from the proliferation of drones and surveillance cameras, has often been dismissed as apolitical. But to think so is to overlook the power dynamics at play. These technologies serve to reinforce existing power structures, making those in positions of authority seemingly omnipotent while rendering the surveilled powerless. In this way, the New Aesthetic exposes our inherent vulnerability and serves as a reminder that the ability to see and be seen is fraught with political implications.

My final example delves into the realm of history and memory. The romanticization of figures like Napoleon Bonaparte obscures the fact that he was a complex individual, capable of great cruelty and violence. By focusing solely on his military triumphs and charisma, we risk distorting historical truth and ignoring the suffering that his actions caused.

These examples serve as reminders that romanticizing historical figures can be dangerous. It obscures their flaws, minimizes their complexities, and strips them of their humanity. We must resist this urge to idealize, for it is only by acknowledging the full spectrum of human experience that we can truly understand our past and shape our future.

This is not an argument for cynicism or pessimism, but rather a call to approach historical figures with criticality and nuance. We must strive to see them as they were - fallible, complex, and deeply human. Only then can we truly learn from the past and create a more just future.

As I continue this exploration, I am left wondering: How can we balance our admiration for historical figures with an honest assessment of their flaws? What role do emotions play in our interpretation of history, and how might they cloud our judgment? These questions and more remain unresolved, inviting further discussion and inquiry.

text:
In the intricate dance of societal progress, we often grapple with the delicate tension between security and civil liberties. This tension is not a new phenomenon; it echoes through the ages, resonating in the hearts and minds of those who yearn for freedom while recognizing the necessity of order. A poignant example can be found in the ancient dialogues of Plato, where Socrates stood trial for his beliefs, ultimately embracing death rather than compromising his principles.

Today, this struggle manifests itself in various contexts, one of which is the debate over the prostitution trade and the question of whether it should be viewed as work or abuse. This is a debate riddled with contentious issues - power dynamics, financial interests, and the pursuit of equality all play significant roles. Yet, despite differing perspectives, the call for human rights and dignity remains a unifying force.

One proposed solution is full decriminalization, advocated by institutions like UNAIDS and Amnesty International, among others. This approach suggests that prostitution should be regarded as any other form of work, with minimal regulation. However, this perspective is not without controversy. Critics argue that such a policy might inadvertently legitimize brothels and increase exploitation, particularly for vulnerable individuals.

New Zealand serves as an example often cited by proponents of decriminalization, with its 2003 legislation considered a model for other countries to follow. Yet, it's important to note that even in New Zealand, there are still limitations on prostitution advertising and where brothels can be located. This raises questions about the true nature of full decriminalization and whether those advocating for such a policy are fully aware of its implications.

As I ponder this issue, I am reminded of Socrates' question to the people of Athens: 'Are you not ashamed of your eagerness to possess as much wealth, reputation and honors as possible, while you do not care for nor give thought to wisdom or truth, or the best possible state of your soul?'. His words echo in my mind, urging me to consider not just the immediate benefits or drawbacks of any policy, but also its long-term effects on the moral and ethical fabric of our societies.

This is an ongoing conversation, one that demands careful consideration and thoughtful reflection. We must strive to find a balance between security and civil liberties, ensuring that our actions promote human dignity while safeguarding the wellbeing of all members of our society. It's a challenging task, but as history has shown us time and again, it is one worth pursuing.

Industry insiders predict that the new regulations could have a significant impact on the luxury goods sector, particularly as more consumers turn to online shopping amid the ongoing pandemic. I decided to go all out and indulge in their Signature Brunch Platter, which included eggs benedict, avocado toast, and a side of crispy hash browns. The avocado toast was a work of art - thick slices of avocado nestled on top of crunchy, toasted bread, drizzled with a zesty lime dressing that added an unexpected but delightful twist.

The Smoked Salmon Benedict was equally as impressive - the salmon was smoky and rich, pairing beautifully with the velvety hollandaise sauce. The potato cakes were crispy on the outside and soft on the inside, providing a satisfying contrast to the tender smoked salmon. We couldn't help but share bites of each other's dishes, marveling at the exquisite flavors that danced across our taste buds.

Throughout our meal, Amelia checked in on us just the right amount - never too often, but always there when we needed her. She even shared a few insider tips about the cafe's history and future plans, which made our dining experience all the more special.

In terms of negative feedback, I would say that the prices are slightly steep for a brunch spot. The only other slight inconvenience was that the menu didn't indicate which dishes were spicy - a minor oversight that left my mouth ablaze when I took a bite of the house-made sriracha.

All in all, I wholeheartedly recommend The Bountiful Brunch Cafe to anyone seeking an exceptional brunch experience! I've been pondering lately about intuition and logical thinking, two seemingly contrasting yet interconnected forces that guide us in our daily lives. They're like the yin and yang of decision-making, each with its own merits and drawbacks.

Take, for instance, the way we perceive drugs. In the early 20th century, opium, morphine, and cocaine were deemed 'evil' and 'contagious'. But look at us now, sipping our morning coffee or chewing coca leaves, hardly giving a second thought to the psychoactive substances we casually consume. It's fascinating how societal norms can shape our perceptions, isn't it?

I remember my grandmother telling me about her school days in the 50s when smoking was considered cool and rebellious. Fast forward to today, and it's a social taboo, especially among young people like myself. Yet, I find myself drawn to the stories of Moroccan hashish smokers or the LSD experiences in Switzerland during the post-war era. It makes me wonder about the role of intuition in shaping our attitudes towards substances and how it intertwines with societal norms and logic.

In a more academic context, I've been reading about cliodynamics - a field that applies mathematics to history to predict social upheavals. It's fascinating how data and quantification can uncover patterns in human behavior, but it also raises concerns about determinism and the potential misuse of such knowledge for political ends.

This shift towards the natural sciences in the humanities is intriguing, especially given our historical cautions against using biology to explain culture and society. The links between science and xenophobic or sexist politics are undeniable, but it's also important to note that scientific progress shouldn't be halted due to potential misuses.

As I delve deeper into these ideas, I can't help but feel a sense of awe at the complexity of human thought and behavior. It's a humbling reminder of our capacity for both great wisdom and great folly. And it leaves me wondering: What role will intuition and logic play in shaping our future? Will they guide us towards enlightenment or lead us further down the path of chaos? Only time will tell, I suppose.
