In the intricate dance between memory and historical truth, we find ourselves grappling with a paradox. The mind, a vessel of individual experience and perception, serves as our personal historian, recording moments, events, and stories that form the tapestry of our lives. Yet, this subjective lens can often cloud our understanding of objective reality, raising questions about the veracity of our memories and their role in shaping the narrative of history.

Consider Johann Gottlieb Fichte, a philosopher whose influence reverberates through the annals of continental thought, but whose name remains obscure to many beyond the narrow confines of academic circles. Born in a small German town, his life was transformed by two fortuitous events - an intellectual encounter and a loan from Immanuel Kant - that propelled him from the humble world of artisanship into the realm of scholarship. But what if these events were not as they seemed? What if Fichte's account of these events, his memory of them, was skewed by the passage of time or the lens through which he viewed them?

Similarly, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, the philosopher who dared to question the very foundations of society and individuality, struggled with a mind restless under constraint. His education was marked by rebellion and flight, his adult life by fits and starts of creative brilliance and paralyzing self-doubt. He found solace in the wilderness, composing in his head as he wandered amidst the rocks and woods. Yet, we must question the reliability of Rousseau's memories, of his own account of his life in Confessions, for they were shaped by his own narrative, his own truth.

These examples serve as a reminder that memory is a fallible tool, subject to distortion and manipulation. It is a filter through which we view the past, shaping our understanding of historical events. But it is also a vital component in the construction of history itself. We remember what we deem important, what resonates with us, what we choose to preserve and pass on. In this way, memory plays an integral role in the creation of historical truth, for it is through our collective memories that we construct narratives about the past.

However, this does not mean that memory should be dismissed as an unreliable source of historical truth. Rather, it should be treated with caution and skepticism, interrogated and cross-examined to uncover the underlying truths beneath the layers of personal perception and interpretation. By doing so, we can arrive at a more nuanced understanding of history, one that acknowledges the subjective nature of our memories while striving for objectivity.

In this dance between memory and historical truth, we are left with a challenge: to remember faithfully, critically, and creatively. For it is through this delicate balance that we can hope to construct an accurate and meaningful account of our past - one that reflects the complexity and richness of human experience.

Authenticity was already an influential cultural ideal during Williams’s lifetime (he was born in 1929 and died in 2003) but it has become only more so since. What is more familiar and compelling than the injunction to be true to oneself, to keep it real? Williams explored the force and appeal of this ideal, and his work still helps us makes sense of it. But, as he was also keenly aware, being true to yourself can be dangerous. In his essay ‘Moral Luck’ (1976), Williams discusses Paul Gauguin’s decision to leave Paris in order to move to Tahiti where he hoped he could become a great painter. Gauguin left behind – basically abandoned – his wife and children. This was on the face of it a very selfish thing to do, and you might think that Gauguin’s action was morally indefensible. Williams, however, thinks that Gauguin’s eventual success as a painter constitutes a form of moral luck, in that his artistic achievement justifies what he did. It provides a justification that not everyone will accept, but one that can make sense to Gauguin himself, and perhaps to others. ‘Look,’ we can imagine Gauguin saying to himself, ‘I was right … I knew I had it in me.’ Williams imagines Gauguin to be conflicted. He also freely admits, however, that his ‘Gauguin’ is not necessarily true in all details to the historical French artist. Williams introduces Gauguin as a useful prop in a thought experiment designed to explore the role that authenticity, achievement and luck play in justification. Williams also just assumes, for the purposes of argument, that Gauguin did in fact succeed, which is to say that Gauguin did create valuable art, and that this art was a great expression of his gifts as a painter. The story of moral harm in pursuit of art, with the overall endeavour somehow justified by the art, is a familiar one both in fact and fiction. The fiction tends to be written by men, and their protagonists tend to be men. Life imitates art and vice versa, and in reality those who justify their selfishness (and worse) in the name of art tend to be men too. Title: A Heatwave Bite at Food Truck Park - 3 stars 

Oh boy, I was hoping for a refreshing lunch break during this sweltering heatwave, but my visit to the food truck park left me with mixed feelings. The glaring sun didn't exactly help the ambiance, but let's dive in.

I've got a gluten intolerance and was craving some veggie tacos from one of those vibrant trucks. After standing in line for what felt like an eternity under the scorching sun (seriously, where were the fans?), I finally placed my order - veggie taco with no gluten, please!

The truck's crew seemed a bit frazzled, juggling orders left and right. My tacos came out quicker than expected, though the presentation could've been better (a little more creativity would've gone a long way). The first bite was... well, decent. The veggies were fresh enough, but I wish there had been more seasoning to really make it pop.

On the plus side, the cleanliness of the truck and its surroundings were top-notch. It was nice to see that attention was paid to maintaining a sanitary environment amidst the chaos. I also appreciated the friendly chats with the crew members, despite their hectic day.

Now, if only they had been as speedy in replenishing ice and drinks! I found myself sipping on tepid water, which wasn't exactly thirst-quenching during this heatwave.

All things considered, I'd say the experience was just okay. There's room for improvement, but I'll probably give it another shot when the weather cools down and my cravings kick in again. Here's hoping for a tasty turnaround!

In the quiet corners of my university library, I found myself lost in the labyrinthine pages of a seminal treatise on international law. The words danced before me, each one a testament to the complexities of global justice, yet they seemed strangely alien - distant echoes of a world far removed from the one I inhabited. As I sat there, surrounded by the hushed whispers of other students engrossed in their own studies, I couldn't help but wonder about the effectiveness of international courts and tribunals, and how they, in their quest for justice, might be limiting our understanding of humanity itself.

A few years ago, I found myself in a small village nestled deep within the heart of Africa. The villagers spoke of a fearsome group that lived on the outskirts, a group called the LRA. To them, these rebels were not human beings but rather wild animals - dangerous predators who prowled the bush, wreaking havoc and violence upon their communities. Yet to the rebels themselves, they saw the bush as a sacred space, a place where they lived and fought in organised military units, transcending the boundaries of what it meant to be human.

This dichotomy between perception and reality struck me as I delved deeper into my studies of international justice. In our quest to bring those who commit atrocities to account, are we not guilty of reducing them to less than human? By labelling their actions as 'crimes against humanity,' are we not stripping them of their individuality, their experiences, and their stories?

I think back to my days as a teenager, sitting in front of my computer screen, typing away at fanfiction stories. At the time, I was struggling with my identity, feeling lost and misunderstood in the halls of my high school. But through fanfiction, I found solace and connection. I discovered that by stepping into the worlds of my favourite characters, I could better understand their struggles, their triumphs, and their humanity.

In many ways, international courts and tribunals are akin to fanfiction - they offer us a limited, often one-dimensional view of those we seek to hold accountable. We reduce them to archetypes, to caricatures, rather than recognizing the complexities of their experiences and the nuances of their stories. In our quest for justice, are we not guilty of perpetuating the very violence that we seek to combat?

As I continue my journey through the world of international law, I am left with more questions than answers. How do we balance the need for accountability with the desire for understanding? How do we hold those who commit atrocities responsible while still recognizing their humanity? These are questions that I grapple with daily, as I navigate the complex landscape of international justice.

I find myself returning to the stories of the LRA rebels and the villagers who feared them, wondering about the human experiences that lay hidden beneath the labels of 'criminal' and 'victim.' In our quest for justice, let us not forget the complexity of humanity, the rich tapestry of stories and experiences that make up the fabric of our world. Let us strive to understand, rather than judge, in our pursuit of a more just world.

Have you ever found yourself in a situation where logic just doesn't seem to cut it? When the rational mind stumbles, and you find yourself reaching for something deeper, something intuitive? I've had moments like that, many times, actually. Like the time I was hiking in the mountains of Colorado, lost among towering pines and snow-capped peaks, when my GPS died and I had to rely on an uncanny sense of direction to find my way back.

I remember standing there, staring at a maze of forest trails, feeling a twinge of fear mixed with a strange certainty that I'd eventually stumble upon the right path. In that moment, logic took a backseat. I wasn't thinking about compass bearings or triangulation, but listening to a voice within, a voice that told me which way to turn.

And yet, there are other times when intuition seems like little more than guesswork, a gamble at best. When you're faced with a tough decision, say, choosing between two job offers or deciding whether to end a relationship, logic can feel like the safer bet. You weigh the pros and cons, gather data, make lists, and yet, despite all that analysis, there remains a nagging doubt, a gut feeling that something isn't quite right.

So, what role do these two faculties play in our lives? Are they enemies or allies? Can we trust intuition as much as logic, or does it lead us astray more often than not?

I find myself returning to this question time and again, particularly when I'm grappling with a complex issue. And each time, I come to a slightly different conclusion. Sometimes, intuition feels like an unerring compass, guiding me through the murky waters of uncertainty. Other times, it seems like nothing more than a fickle, unreliable guide, leading me down blind alleys and dead ends.

Take, for example, the petroglyphs I stumbled upon in western Sweden last fall. These ancient carvings, dating back thousands of years, seem to defy logic. They depict a world that has long since vanished, a world of bowmen and longships, of stags and giants. And yet, they speak to us across the ages, their silent messages echoing down the corridors of time.

How did these people navigate the currents of time and matter to communicate with us? Was it their intentional signs and symbols that left the most lasting marks, or their unintentional traces? We'll never know for sure, but I can't help but wonder if there was a certain intuition at play here, a sense of the future that guided these ancient artists.

In our own lives, we grapple with similar questions. How do we leave a mark on the world, a message that will echo down through the ages? Is it in the grand gestures and monumental achievements, or in the small, everyday acts of kindness and compassion?

I don't have any easy answers to these questions. Like those ancient petroglyphs, I'm left with more questions than answers, a tangled web of thoughts and feelings that defy logical analysis. But I do know this: whether we trust our intuition or rely on logic, we are all part of the same great tapestry, woven from the threads of time and matter, connected by the currents of love and loss, hope and fear.

And so, I continue to navigate these currents, my compass set to a mix of logic and intuition, doubt and certainty. I can't say where this journey will take me, but I know that it's one I must undertake. Because in the end, it's not about arriving at a destination, but about the journey itself, the connections we make along the way, and the stories we tell as we go. And sometimes, in the quiet moments between the noise of logic and the whispers of intuition, I hear those stories echoing down through the ages, a testament to the power of the human spirit, a reminder that we are all part of something much larger than ourselves.

It’s an emptiness that has become palpable and insistent, like a black hole that sucks everything into itself. As a phenomenologist, I would say that the abyss is an experience of space unhinged from the world and from the sense of an ‘elsewhere’ or ‘otherwise’. It is an experience of space without horizon, without the basic coordinates of ‘here’ and ‘there’ by which we orient ourselves in everyday life. The prisoner who bashes his own body against the walls of a rec yard is both refusing and confirming the abyss of solitary confinement. The self-battering body makes a statement of sorts: these walls might confine me absolutely, but I absolutely refuse to be confined! There is a world out there – a ‘there’ to which my ‘here’ is correlated – and I will find it, even if I have to hurl myself against it, or destroy myself in the process. This kind of resistance might be self-defeating, but it remains an eloquent expression of the depth of emotional and ontological harm that prolonged solitary confinement can inflict on a person. Some prisoners find other ways of coping with the exhaustion of spatial horizons. Robert King, who was convicted of robbery in 1969 and spent 29 years in solitary confinement in Louisiana, writes: Some days I would pace up and down and from left to right for hours, counting to myself. I learned to know every inch of the cell. Maybe I looked crazy walking back and forth like some trapped animal, but I had no choice – I needed to feel in control of my space. Like the act of bashing one’s body into walls, the habit of pacing both resists and reinscribes the limits of extreme isolation. But pacing develops a more sustainable coping mechanism, a way of feeling in control of your space even if you don’t have the power to change places. The prisoner who paces both refuses to sit still within his allotted space, and refuses to destroy his bodily integrity by bashing against the walls. He affirms himself as a living, moving being, even though the world has been diminished to the point where he is no longer able to live and move freely. if our ‘here’ is intertwined with their ‘there’, it cannot help but affect our own capacities to see, hear, and make sense of our lives Pacing does not express an acceptance of limits, but rather a nervous retracing of them, a habit formed around the impossibility of habituating oneself to what is an intolerable situation. In the realm of international politics, one cannot ignore the complexities surrounding the effectiveness of international courts and tribunals. As a student of political science, I've always been intrigued by how these institutions challenge our traditional assumptions about justice, power, and sovereignty.

Consider the International Criminal Court (ICC), established to bring perpetrators of war crimes, genocide, and crimes against humanity to justice. Yet, its effectiveness is questionable when we look at the number of states that have yet to ratify its Rome Statute or refuse to cooperate with it altogether. The ICC, in theory, stands as a beacon of international justice, but in practice, it faces significant challenges, particularly when it comes to enforcing its rulings against powerful nations.

One cannot discuss the effectiveness of international courts without mentioning the International Court of Justice (ICJ), the primary judicial organ of the United Nations. The ICJ was created with the noble intention of resolving disputes between states peacefully. However, its ability to do so is often hampered by the fact that only states can bring cases before it, and even then, compliance with its rulings is not always guaranteed. For instance, in the 2018 case concerning Myanmar's treatment of the Rohingya, the ICJ issued a ruling for provisional measures to protect the Rohingya, but as of now, there has been little evidence of meaningful change on the ground.

These examples highlight the complexities surrounding the effectiveness of international courts and tribunals. They exist as symbols of global justice, but their ability to enforce rulings is often limited by the very states they aim to serve. This raises questions about the nature of power in the international system and whether these institutions can truly challenge it.

One might argue that the limitations of these courts stem from the inherent tension between sovereignty and international law. States are reluctant to cede too much power to international institutions, fearing a loss of control over their domestic affairs. Yet, this very reluctance undermines the effectiveness of these courts, making them little more than symbolic gestures towards global justice.

It is also worth considering the role of political will in the functioning of these courts. Without the support and cooperation of powerful nations, international courts are rendered impotent, unable to enforce their rulings or bring perpetrators to justice. This raises questions about whether these institutions can truly serve as a check on power when those with the most power have little incentive to comply.

In conclusion, while international courts and tribunals offer promising solutions to global problems, their effectiveness remains questionable. The challenges they face in enforcing rulings against powerful nations and the inherent tension between sovereignty and international law are significant obstacles that must be addressed if these institutions are to truly serve as instruments of justice. It is a complex issue that demands further exploration and critical reflection.

Herodotus didn’t follow his own argument through to its logical conclusion – that the voyage to the underworld in Book 11 of the Odyssey, and perhaps all of Odysseus’ wanderings, must also be fictions. But a later geographer named Eratosthenes pressed Herodotus’s argument home in exactly this way. The entire tale of Odysseus’ wanderings, Eratosthenes claimed – including such memorable episodes as the Cyclops, the six-headed monster Scylla, and the Lotus-eaters with their intoxicating fruit that takes away all desire for effort or action – was nothing more than an exokeanismos, an ‘Oceaning-out’ of the narrative line. Readers might derive pleasure from it, but they couldn’t learn about the geography of the Earth from it. For Eratosthenes, Ocean therefore became associated not only with fiction, but with the kind of wholesale inventions we today group under the rubrics ‘science fiction’ and ‘fantasy’. It formed an alternative universe, complete with its own exotic peoples and monstrous life forms – the kind of realm we moderns, almost invariably, locate in outer space. What ancient mariner would dare to sail upon this alien element, a place that, depending on what literary sources one relied on, might be either infinite, or unimaginably old, or not there at all? Only a few ancient explorers, to our knowledge, ignored the warning signs at the Straits of Gibraltar – the so-called Pillars of Hercules, set there to tell humankind they must go no farther – and ventured out into the unknown Atlantic. This duality reflects the complexities of human nature and serves as a reminder that we are not always what we seem - a lesson that remains relevant today.

Now, let's take a leap across continents to Africa, where the tales of Anansi the spider abound. Anansi is a trickster figure who uses his cunning and wit to outsmart others. text:
In the labyrinthine tapestry of post-conflict societies, justice and forgiveness often occupy center stage. Yet, these concepts, long revered as pillars of societal recovery, seem to blur the lines between righteousness and misconception, leaving us grappling with an incomplete understanding.

Consider a war-torn nation attempting to heal its wounds, where justice is meted out through trials and tribunals. The victims seek retribution, while the perpetrators plead for mercy. However, this simplistic binary of victim versus villain rarely captures the nuances of human behavior in conflict zones.

The truth often lies in the intricate network of relationships and alliances that bind individuals together, transcending the black-and-white labels of guilt and innocence. A leader might be seen as a savior by his followers, while the international community brands him a war criminal. This dichotomy raises questions about whose justice we are striving for - the individual or the collective?

Forgiveness, too, is not as straightforward as it seems. In many cases, forgiveness is demanded from victims who have lost everything, while perpetrators are expected to show remorse without experiencing the consequences of their actions. This asymmetry in the call for forgiveness creates a dissonance that undermines the very essence of healing and reconciliation.

Moreover, forgiveness often overlooks the systemic issues that fueled the conflict in the first place. A rapist may be pardoned, but what about the socio-economic conditions that led him to commit such a heinous act? Forgiving an individual without addressing the underlying problems only serves to perpetuate the cycle of violence and injustice.

The challenge lies in navigating this complex maze of justice and forgiveness, where every turn presents a new conundrum. We must strive for a more nuanced understanding that acknowledges the intricacies of human behavior, the systemic issues at play, and the need for collective healing rather than individual punishment or pardon.

In the grand scheme of things, justice and forgiveness in post-conflict societies are not just about retribution or mercy, but about fostering a society where individuals can coexist peacefully, learning from their past while building a future together. Yet, our current understanding seems to fall short, leaving us with more questions than answers.

As I delve deeper into this topic, I find myself questioning the very foundations of justice and forgiveness. Are they universal concepts that apply equally to all societies, or are they cultural constructs that vary from one society to another? And if they do vary, what does this mean for our attempts at reconciliation in post-conflict societies?

These questions leave me with a sense of unease, a feeling that I am navigating uncharted territory. Yet, it is precisely this unease that propels me forward, pushing me to explore the intricacies of justice and forgiveness further. For in the end, it is only by understanding these complexities that we can hope to build a better future for ourselves and generations to come.

In my journey so far, I have encountered scholars who argue that justice and forgiveness are not mutually exclusive, but rather two sides of the same coin. Others contend that they are inherently contradictory, with one demanding retribution while the other promotes mercy. Yet, others propose a third way - restorative justice - which focuses on healing the wounds inflicted by conflict rather than punishing the perpetrator or pardoning the offender.

Each of these perspectives offers valuable insights, yet they also leave me with more questions. How can we reconcile these seemingly opposing views? And more importantly, how can we apply them in practice to bring about lasting peace and reconciliation in post-conflict societies?

These are the questions that keep me awake at night, fueling my desire to delve deeper into this complex and fascinating topic. As I continue my exploration, I am reminded of the intricate network of relationships and alliances that bind individuals together, even in the most adversarial of circumstances.

In many ways, our understanding of justice and forgiveness mirrors this network, with each perspective offering a different vantage point from which to view the larger picture. It is only by considering these diverse perspectives that we can hope to build a more comprehensive and nuanced understanding, one that acknowledges the complexities of human behavior and the need for collective healing.

Yet, this journey is far from over. There are still many unanswered questions, many mysteries to be unraveled. But it is precisely this sense of wonder, this thirst for knowledge, that drives me forward. For in seeking answers, we not only expand our own understanding but also contribute to the greater good, helping to build a more peaceful and just world for all.

text: To display Félix González-Torres’s ‘Untitled’ (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991), the museum must acquire a supply of the appropriate wrapped hard candies and display approximately 175 lbs’ worth in a pile on the gallery floor, available for audience members to take. To display Drifting Continents (2009) by El Anatsui, a massive wall-hung work made of liquor bottle tops connected with copper wire, the museum must come into possession of the panels painstakingly fabricated in Anatsui’s studio, but the installers have the latitude to make decisions about how to fold, drape, and orient them to best suit the space and their own taste. To display Jana Sterbak’s Vanitas: Flesh Dress for an Albino Anorectic (1987), the museum must fabricate a new dress out of salted flank steak and allow it to desiccate over the course of the exhibition. Rules for display govern both what to display – specific objects created by the artist, a type of object the artist has designated, or a physical array newly constituted for the occasion – and how to display it: whether in a very specific physical configuration, or in one of a range of ways defined by the artist’s aesthetic sensibility or by some other set of criteria. Untitled (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) by Félix González-Torres. Courtesy the Art Institute of Chicago/WikipediaThe second group are rules for conservation. These have to do with which elements of a work must be preserved for the work to maintain its material integrity. Rules for conservation are important in shaping how the material components of a work will appear to future viewers, but also in identifying which elements are essential to the work’s ongoing existence. When Murakami created abstract paintings whose paint is designed to flake away over time, he embraced within the works a process of change that is normally treated as damage to be prevented and repaired. Whereas paintings have traditionally been intended to have a relatively stable surface and be conserved with the goal of maintaining it, Murakami redeploys painting as an essentially time-based medium. Finally, we have rules for participation. These specify what, if anything, we as audience members may or must do to experience the work. The works in Jill Sigman’s Hut Project (2009-) are designed not just to be appreciated for their visual aesthetic, but to set up social situations: we are invited to participate in various ways, from contributing a small note or drawing, to coming in for tea. Paul Ramírez Jonas’s The Commons (2011), a monumental sculpture of a riderless horse atop a pedestal, offers a cork surface pierced with thumbtacks as an invitation for viewers to contribute whatever items or messages they like, making the work into a site of collective communication that papers over the object the artist supplied. The Hut Project (2009-) by Jill SigmanThe Commons (2011) by Paul Ramírez Jonas. Materials: cork, pushpins, notes

Let me tell you about an absolute gem I stumbled upon during a recent heatwave while celebrating a special occasion with my partner. This unassuming street food spot, tucked away in the city's bustling heart, was like a breath of fresh air amidst the sweltering summer heat.

As we stepped inside, the vibrant aroma of exotic spices and zesty herbs enveloped us, instantly transporting us to the vibrant streets of Bangkok. The interior was tastefully done, with an eclectic mix of wooden tables and chairs, adorned with colorful Thai textiles.

We were greeted warmly by the friendly staff who quickly handed us menus that looked like they'd been flown straight from Thailand. We decided to share a few dishes, given the generous portion sizes this place is known for. The Pad Thai was a must-try, and it did not disappoint! The noodles were cooked to perfection, with just the right balance of sweet, sour, and spicy flavors. The portion was more than enough to satisfy our hunger pangs.

The Gaeng Keow Wan Gai (Green Curry Chicken) was another standout dish. Creamy and rich, yet not overly spicy, it was a comforting treat on a sweltering day. And let's not forget the Som Tum, a spicy green papaya salad that was bursting with flavor. Each bite was a symphony of tart, sweet, and spicy notes that left us craving more.

The staff were attentive yet unobtrusive, ensuring our glasses never went empty and checking in regularly to make sure we were enjoying our meal. They even offered suggestions on which dishes to try based on our preferences.

Parking wasn't a hassle either, with a large lot just a block away. The only downside was that the heatwave had caused a few power outages, but the resourceful staff quickly improvised and used portable generators to keep the kitchen running smoothly.

In conclusion, if you're looking for an authentic Thai street food experience in the city, this hidden gem is definitely worth a visit. The flavors, the ambiance, and the friendly service make it a must-try, especially during those sweltering summer days when a spicy bowl of Pad Thai hits the spot! They cut through the thin, cold air in supersonic aeroshells and parachutes, making planetfall in bouncing airbag cocoons and bursts of braking retrorockets, bristling with cameras and spectrometers, antennas and manipulator arms. Some stay where they land; others rove for years on end. Sniffing the air, sifting through soil, wheeling across a cratered landscape, the robots doggedly seek any wisp of Martian moisture, like lost desert travellers dying of thirst. They are looking for life, following a strategy that NASA officials describe as ‘following the water’. This strategy pays heed to water’s eerie harmony with life: wherever one is found on Earth, the other isn’t far behind. There are good reasons to believe that water is the ideal cornerstone of biology, not only on Earth, but elsewhere in the Universe. All you need to make it is hydrogen and oxygen, respectively the first and third most abundant elements in the cosmos. In liquid form, water is unparalleled in its attunement to life’s needs. It facilitates the transmission of biochemical energy, nutrients, and waste. It shapes the structures and interactions of proteins and other macromolecules. It shields against hostile cosmic radiation, and it has a remarkably robust capacity for retaining warmth. Most strangely, unlike almost every other substance in the Universe, when water freezes, it expands instead of contracting, forming a protective, insulating shell of ice at its surface. This helps oceans, lakes, and other liquid reservoirs avoid freezing solid when exposed to prolonged cold. For researchers who study Mars, following the water has paid off handsomely. Every mission sent to Mars seeking water has found it and, as a result, we now know that our neighbouring world used to be a warmer, wetter, more habitable place. Billions of years ago, that all changed, as the planet cooled and lost most of its air and water, and settled into a quiet senescence. But present-day Mars still harbours a slumbering aquasphere, locked in the ground as ice, which may stir every so often, erupting to the surface in evanescent briny flows. Officially, the next stage of NASA’s Mars exploration programme is to ‘seek signs of life’, a phase that supposedly began in the summer of 2012 with the successful landing of the car-sized Curiosity rover. Dear Reader,

Navigating difficult moral dilemmas can often feel like a labyrinth, with twists and turns that challenge our beliefs and values. Yet, throughout history, we've seen countless individuals who have bravely faced these conundrums, finding ways to make sense of them in their unique contexts.

Let me share a personal story as an example. Last week, I found myself at a crossroads - a choice between my professional ambition and the well-being of someone dear to me. The weight of this decision pressed heavily upon my shoulders, and for days, I paced back and forth, wrestling with the implications of each possible path. In the end, I chose to prioritize the other person's needs, sacrificing a chance at personal success in the process. It wasn't easy, but it felt right - it was the moral choice.

This experience reminded me of the Jena Set, a group of brilliant minds who came together during the late 18th century. They too faced difficult moral dilemmas, pushing boundaries and questioning the status quo. In an age of absolutism, they sought freedom for their thoughts and ideas - a quest that often landed them in trouble with authorities. Yet, they persisted, drawing inspiration from one another's radical ideas.

The Jena Set offers us a powerful lesson about the importance of community and collaboration when navigating moral dilemmas. By sharing their struggles and successes, they created an environment where new ideas could flourish - an ecosystem that fostered growth, innovation, and ultimately, change. In times of uncertainty, we too can find solace in the support of our peers and mentors - individuals who challenge us to think critically and act compassionately.

But what happens when there is no community to turn to? When faced with a moral dilemma, we may feel isolated and lost. It is during these moments that we must look within ourselves for answers - leaning on our innate sense of empathy, morality, and justice. By examining our personal values and beliefs, we can discern the right path forward and find the courage to act upon it.

Yet, we must also remember that there is no one-size-fits-all solution when navigating moral dilemmas. What may be the right choice for one person may not be for another - our circumstances, beliefs, and values all play a role in shaping our decisions. It is up to each of us to listen to our inner voices, consult with trusted advisors, and ultimately make the choice that feels true to ourselves.

As we continue to encounter difficult moral dilemmas, let us remember the stories of the Jena Set - individuals who stood up for their beliefs and made a lasting impact on history. May we too find the courage to challenge ourselves, engage in open dialogue with others, and ultimately, make choices that reflect our deepest values and aspirations.

In this ongoing discussion about moral dilemmas, I am curious: How have you faced difficult moral decisions in your own life? What strategies or resources did you use to navigate these challenges? Please share your stories with me - together, we can learn from one another's experiences and continue to grow as individuals and a community.

Yours sincerely,
A fellow explorer of complex ideas

The lawsuit, led by the California State Teachers' Retirement System (CalSTRS), accuses Shell and several other companies, including BP and ExxonMobil, of conspiring to artificially inflate prices during the specified period.

This alleged manipulation affected crude oil options traded on the New York Mercantile Exchange, according to CalSTRS.

If successful, this lawsuit could result in significant financial penalties for the companies involved. The exact amount of damages being sought by CalSTRS is not yet clear, but given the length and scope of the alleged price manipulation, potential fines could reach billions of dollars.

This lawsuit adds to a growing list of legal challenges faced by oil companies in recent years over their trading practices. In 2019, BP reached a $425 million settlement with the US Department of Justice over similar allegations of price manipulation. ExxonMobil is also currently under investigation for its role in the same practice.

Shell, BP, and ExxonMobil have all denied the accusations and are expected to vigorously defend themselves in court. However, the sheer number of legal challenges facing these companies raises questions about the transparency and fairness of the global oil trading market.

text:
In the realm of psychology and neuroscience, we've long been grappling with understanding the nature of conscious experience. Traditional assumptions have placed it on a pedestal, an untouchable sanctum of individuality and agency. But what if this isn't quite accurate? What if our consciousness isn't ours alone but is more like a playground where external forces shape our thoughts and emotions?

Take, for instance, the case of addiction - not just to substances but to experiences. Schll's research on slot-machine players sheds light on an intriguing phenomenon. These individuals aren't driven by focus or concentration; instead, they are alienated, disconnected from the world around them. They're not so much engaged as they are being played. The thrill, the allure isn't in winning but in losing - a paradox that makes it hard to distinguish between reality and simulacrum.

Gambling isn't about calculated risk or probability modeling; it's about merging with the experience, letting it consume you. For some, it becomes an insidious virus that affects their thoughts, feelings, and actions. It's not a simulation of life but a replacement - a substitute reality.

And herein lies the tragedy. My father, a victim of this substitution, lost more than just money; he lost his grip on reality. He became infantilized, stuck in an endless loop of play. His story mirrors that of Schll's slot-machine junkies - people who reduced their interaction with the world to a single, manageable exchange: bet or not. Play or not.

But what about me? What drew me into the world of blackjack? The adventure, the thrill, the edgy allure - yes, but there was more. There was a sense of control, of mastery over an unpredictable world. In the game, I found a semblance of order amidst chaos, a way to tame the beast that is life.

And yet, this exploration raises more questions than answers. If our conscious experience isn't entirely ours, if it's shaped by external forces, then who are we? Are we just pawns in a grand cosmic game, played and manipulated at will? Or is there a way to reclaim our agency, to assert our individuality amidst the sea of influences?

These are questions that haunt me as I navigate this fascinating labyrinth of human consciousness. They echo Derrida's musings on confession, secrets, and selfhood. In his work, he delves into the intricate relationship between our secrets and our selves, suggesting that revealing our secrets isn't just about exposing a part of ourselves but merging with others, becoming part of a larger community.

This notion of confession resonates with my own experiences. When I reveal my secrets, I expose a part of myself, inviting others to do the same. In this act, we create bonds, forge connections that transcend individuality. Yet, as Derrida reminds us, confessions aren't always voluntary; they can be forced, coerced, or manipulated.

This idea of compulsory confession is strikingly similar to the massacre of Jews in Spain and Portugal during the Middle Ages. The attackers didn't just target the Jewish community; they targeted their secrets, their unique identity, their hidden selfhood. In doing so, they sought to erase them, to obliterate their individuality.

As I continue my journey through this maze of consciousness, I can't help but wonder: Are we more than our secrets? Or are our secrets the essence of who we are? And if they are, then what happens when those secrets are exposed, when our individuality is stripped away? These questions haunt me, gnaw at my thoughts, shaping my exploration of human consciousness.

This isn't a conclusion, just a pause in the journey. A moment to reflect on where we've been and where we might be going. The answers aren't clear yet, but the questions are worth asking, the exploration worth undertaking. After all, understanding the nature of conscious experience could reshape our society, redefine our relationships, and reveal truths about ourselves that we never knew existed. It's a journey worth taking, even if the destination remains elusive.

The best description we can make of ourselves is as storytellers of a sort.’ However, the Harrisons’ emphasis on dialogue and storytelling closely paralleled one of the characteristic traits of the fringe survivalist movement. This community’s literature abounds with stories about how society collapsed, and provided a necessary prelude to detailed narratives about how prepared individuals kept themselves and their loved ones alive. As the sociologist Richard G Mitchell noted after his participant-observation studies of survivalists and preppers, the heart of their endeavours is ‘constructing “what if” scenarios in which survival preparations will at once become necessary and sufficient’. Creative and artful in their own grim way, these ‘survival scenarios’ are, Mitchell wrote in Dancing at Armageddon (2001), ‘akin to contemporary legends told in future tense’. In a sense, their projections of the present into the future are morality tales about who will survive (and how) a coming catastrophe caused by nuclear war, economic collapse, communism or natural disaster. Sketch for Survival Piece V: Portable Orchard (1972), at California State University, Fullerton. Courtesy the Whitney Museum of American Art, New YorkThe Harrisons deliberately aligned their art-making with a similar focus on preparedness. By 1974, they had made seven installations in the Survival Pieces series. Works such as Portable Fish Farm and Portable Orchard (1972) were created, the Harrisons said, to offer people ‘a self-sufficient survival resource’. Their blend of research, art-making and storytelling was predicated on fears about the future, aimed ‘to teach lessons about survival in an ecologically blighted world’. But they also contained seeds of hope for averting undesirable futures. After the Survival Pieces series, the Harrisons grounded their artworks even more deeply in narrative and storytelling. The best example of this is their monumental installation The Lagoon Cycle. Conceived and created over a decade (1974-84), this seven-part epic – it is 360 ft long and 8 ft tall – is organised as a dialogue between two characters, ‘the Lagoon Maker’ and ‘the Witness’. The former, a devotee of technological solutions infused with hubris, proposes increasingly elaborate ways to carry out sustainable food production – what if the Salton Sea in southern California could be reclaimed, its pollution sent to the Pacific Ocean, and used as a large-scale fish farm? The Witness, in turn, reacts to these schemes, encouraging their partner to stop thinking like a technocrat and consider the real-world ecological effects of their schemes. The cycle’s final part, titled The Seventh Lagoon – The Ring of Fire the Ring of Water (1984), ends with a map of the world, its coastlines now underwater. The Witness asks of their partner: and in this new beginningthis continuously rebeginningwill you feed me when my lands canno longer produce?and will I house youwhen your lands are covered withwater?and togetherwe will withdrawas the waters riseFrom The Lagoon Cycle, The Seventh Lagoon

PARIS: Apple Inc. announced an aggressive cost-cutting plan on Monday, sending shockwaves through the tech industry and sparking mixed reactions from investors and consumers alike.

The Silicon Valley giant, known for its premium pricing strategy, aims to save billions by streamlining its supply chain, reducing costs associated with research and development, and trimming workforce expenses. The move comes as the COVID-19 pandemic continues to weigh heavily on global economies, putting pressure on businesses worldwide to minimize overheads.

Investors initially reacted negatively, sending Apple's stock price down by 3% in after-hours trading. However, some analysts argue that this could be a strategic move to maintain profitability amid economic uncertainty and prepare for potential future disruptions.

Consumer response has been more subdued, with many expressing concerns about the potential impact on product quality and innovation. Some Apple enthusiasts worry that the cost-cutting measures might lead to compromises in design or features, which could dilute the brand's appeal.

Apple's cost-cutting plan is expected to save the company approximately $4 billion annually, according to a report by Bloomberg News. The tech giant plans to achieve these savings primarily by consolidating operations and reducing workforce expenses.

The next few months will be critical in determining whether Apple's cost-cutting strategy pays off or backfires. With the launch of its highly anticipated iPhone 13 series just around the corner, the company will need to strike a delicate balance between cutting costs and maintaining its reputation for delivering top-quality products.

text:
In the realm of contemporary art, few figures captivate my imagination as deeply as Marina Abramovi. Her performance art, with its raw, intimate exploration of the human condition, pierces the veil of our everyday existence and compels us to confront the profound mysteries that lie within ourselves.

It is not just her work that intrigues me, but also the woman behind it - a woman who has bared her soul time and again in pursuit of truth. As I stand before one of her installations, my heart races, and I am both enthralled and unsettled by the vulnerability that permeates her performances.

One such work that haunts me is "The Artist Is Present," where Abramovi sat across from an empty chair for seven hours a day for three months, inviting visitors to take a seat opposite her. The experience was at once profoundly simple and painfully complex. Each interaction was unique, a delicate dance between two souls, each carrying their own burdens and secrets.

As I watched the performance unfold, I found myself drawn into the swirling vortex of human connection. I could see the tears welling up in the eyes of some participants as they grappled with their emotions, while others remained stoic, their faces betraying barely a flicker of emotion. Yet, all were touched by Abramovi's courage and vulnerability, her willingness to expose herself so completely for our benefit.

In many ways, Abramovi embodies the essence of performance art - a fearless exploration of the human experience, unfiltered and raw. But her work also raises questions that linger long after the performance has ended. What does it mean to truly connect with another person? How much of ourselves are we willing to share with the world? And, perhaps most importantly, can we find meaning in our lives by confronting our own mortality?

These questions swirl in my mind as I continue to grapple with Abramovi's work. It is not an easy path, nor one that offers simple answers. But it is a journey that I am compelled to undertake, drawn by the promise of self-discovery and understanding that lies at its heart.

As I wander through the gallery, my thoughts are interrupted by a soft whisper - a voice that seems to come from within me. It speaks of courage, vulnerability, and the human capacity for connection. It is a reminder that we are all more alike than we are different, that our fears and dreams are shared by countless others.

And as I walk away from the gallery, those words echo in my mind, guiding me through the labyrinth of life. For it is in the darkest corners of ourselves that we find our truest selves, and it is only by embracing our vulnerability that we can truly connect with one another.

Abramovi's performance art has left an indelible mark on my soul, a reminder of the beauty and pain that lie within each of us. And as I continue on my own journey through life, her work will remain a guiding star, illuminating the path before me and helping me to navigate the complexities of the human experience.

Title: A Fascinating, Yet Slightly Disappointing Experience at La Piazza during the Off-Season

Star Rating: 

Ah, La Piazza! A beacon of Italian cuisine nestled in this quiet corner of town. I've always heard the buzz about its bustling atmosphere, but on a chilly off-season evening, it was just me, my appetite, and the warm glow of the restaurant's inviting lights.

The wait was minimal, a stark contrast to the stories I'd heard. The interior was as cozy as expected, with rustic wooden furniture and soft, dim lighting that made for an intimate ambiance. The soft strumming of an acoustic guitar in the background added a soothing vibe to the evening.

I opted for their health-conscious menu, eager to indulge without guilt. My dish, a zesty chicken and vegetable medley with lentils, was visually appealing and promised a symphony of flavors - sweet, bitter, and spicy. The portion size was generous, yet manageable, making me feel like I was feasting on a home-cooked meal rather than dining out.

However, the lamb and jalapeo dish that my companion ordered seemed a tad greasy, even for Italian fare. Perhaps it was the extra kick of spice that made it so? The bread provided was a delightful surprise - a tartish crepe-like variety that complemented the dishes beautifully.

Now, let's talk service. It was prompt and attentive, making our dining experience effortless. But, as a health-conscious eater, I couldn't help but wonder about the origins of some ingredients. Was the lamb fresh, or did it hail from a frozen packet? The menu boasted local produce, but I couldn't quite tell if their signature pasta was made in-house or sourced elsewhere.

In the end, I left La Piazza with a sense of satisfaction, yet also feeling a bit let down. The flavors were bold and the setting charming, but the mystery surrounding some ingredients lingered like an unwelcome guest at dinner. Would I return? Absolutely, to sample more dishes and perhaps discover hidden culinary gems. But if you're used to frozen pizzas and fast-food joints, La Piazza might not be the quick fix you crave. Instead, it offers a slow, thoughtful Italian dining experience - one that invites you to savor each bite and appreciate the artistry behind it.

Rorty took the new analytic philosophy to have been a malignant force in American philosophy departments, an invasion that displaced pragmatism. Crucially, the displacement is said to have been achieved not by way of a critical engagement with the pragmatists’ arguments and commitments, but instead simply by declaring pragmatism soft and insufficiently rigorous. Pragmatism was, in this telling, eclipsed as philosophers in the US began taking their intellectual cues from the analytic philosophers. Having gained strongholds in nearly all the elite PhD-granting universities in the US, the analytics swiftly trained the next several generations of professional philosophers. Pragmatism, America’s homegrown philosophy, thus was driven underground, where the remaining loyalists built scholarly networks devoted to keeping the classical idiom alive. A more reliable strategy for marginalising the classical pragmatists could hardly be imagined Yet there is also a resurrection in the eclipse narrative. It goes on to say that analytic philosophy eventually proved itself too self-absorbed and socially irrelevant to be sustainable. Recovering from the analytic fad, philosophers in the US, notably Rorty, Hilary Putnam and Cornel West, rediscovered pragmatism in their landmark works of the 1970s and ’80s. Hence ‘neo-pragmatism’ came to the fore as a leading ‘post-analytic’ development in professional philosophy. The eclipse seems to have been undone. Well, not quite. The resurrection story is tinged with resentment. It is alleged that neo-pragmatism is too analytic and not closely tied to the classical texts. text:
Meditation, this ancient practice that seems to have been around since the dawn of humanity, has a profound impact on our sense of self. It's like diving into the deepest corners of your mind, exploring the hidden nooks and crannies, and maybe even discovering a treasure or two about yourself.

I remember my first meditation session, I was in college, feeling overwhelmed by exams, deadlines, and life in general. I sat down on a worn-out yoga mat, closed my eyes, and tried to follow the soothing voice of the instructor. It was chaotic inside my head, thoughts were racing like wild horses. But as time passed, I found myself becoming more aware of these thoughts, observing them without judgement, letting them pass by like clouds in the sky.

Meditation can be a journey of self-discovery, helping us to uncover layers of our personality that we didn't even know existed. It's not just about calming down or finding inner peace; it's about learning to observe ourselves objectively, understanding our habits, fears, and desires without getting entangled in them.

In a way, meditation changes the lens through which we see ourselves. Instead of looking at ourselves as a collection of thoughts, emotions, and experiences, we start seeing ourselves as observers, detached yet connected. We realize that we are not our thoughts, we are not our emotions, we are not our experiences.

This shift in perspective can be liberating. It allows us to let go of the stories we tell ourselves, the labels we've been given, and the roles we've played. We begin to see ourselves as ever-changing, as works in progress, rather than fixed entities. And this, I believe, is a crucial step towards self-acceptance and compassion.

But here's the tricky part - this newfound sense of self doesn't necessarily make us better people. It doesn't automatically erase our flaws or solve our problems. Meditation might help us understand ourselves better, but it doesn't change who we are fundamentally. It's up to us to decide how we want to use this understanding, how we want to grow, and how we want to live.

So, meditation changes our sense of self, but in a way that leaves us with more questions than answers. It invites us to explore the depths of our being, to understand ourselves better, and to take responsibility for our actions. But it doesn't provide easy solutions or quick fixes. Instead, it offers a path, a tool, a means to navigate the complexities of our own minds. And that, I think, is a journey worth taking.

Title: A Charming Birthday Bistrot Experience...On a Budget!

The ambiance was just delightful - dimly-lit candlelit tables, soft jazz playing in the background, and a welcoming staff that made me feel right at home. Plus, they have an excellent value fixed-price menu for lunch, so keep that in mind if you're looking to save a few pennies!

Overall, Le Petit Chou is a must-visit for anyone seeking a bit of Parisian charm right here in our city. I'll definitely be returning, and I can't wait to introduce my friends to this little gem. Bravo, Le Petit Chou - you've made one birthday girl very happy indeed!

Since then, the centre has hosted two film crews a month, from Japan to Britain, says Singleton. ‘And it’s bloody exhausting.’ It’s no wonder that Singleton is cynical about the orangutan’s protected status in Indonesia, a place, he says, where ‘corrupt officials pay lip service to their protection while turning a blind eye towards trafficking’. Singleton is aware that he is one in a ‘long line of white people coming in to tell Indonesians how to do conservation’, but says that’s beginning to change. In the past decade, a new generation of Indonesian environmental activists have begun to make headway on the major resource and land issues in the country by putting wildlife aside and focusing instead on the people on the ground; the humans whose land and livelihoods are also threatened by palm-oil companies. Armed with smartphones, GPS and chainsaws, they have cut back hectares of palm illegally planted in protected areas Recently, Harry Surjadi, an Indonesian environmental journalist, began recruiting and training farmers in remote corners of Sumatra’s Riau province to act as citizen journalists, sending text-message reports on palm-plantation activities to subscribers from mainstream media, government and activist organisations. It’s an expansion of a programme Surjadi launched in Borneo, where it’s been successful in helping to resolve disputes between local communities and powerful palm companies. Last year, Rudi Putra, a Sumatran biologist, won the prestigious international Goldman Environmental Prize for his work monitoring palm-oil companies. The non-profit he runs, the HAkA Foundation in Banda Aceh on Sumatra, is staffed by young, passionate local citizens who are coalescing their efforts around protecting the Leuser ecosystem, a 2.6-million hectare swath of tropical old-growth rainforest. Armed with smartphones, GPS and chainsaws, his teams have worked with local police forces and municipal governments to cut back hectares of palm that has been illegally planted in protected areas within Leuser and community forests around it. Leuser is ecologically unique – the only place where tigers, elephants, rhinos and orangutans exist together – but that’s not the message that Putra and his team focus on. Rather, they frame the issue around water: the more intact forest, the more water the region will sustain. The less logging, the fewer and less severe the floods. People get it, says Putra. When the interests of wildlife and local people are in sync – in protecting watersheds or forests, for example – then conservation becomes possible. Having an ‘animals versus humans’ approach helps no species prevail. The alignment of interests comes when you recognise that the so-called human-wildlife conflict is a convenient obfuscation for the powerful, and that its true root lies in the pressure placed on traditional small farming and other livelihoods when greedy players usurp lands both from humans and from wild species. Title: A Hidden Gem Ruined by Overhype - The Sinking Ship Called "Taste of the Bayou"

I've been a seafood lover all my life, and I pride myself on knowing good Cajun cuisine. So, when everyone was raving about Taste of the Bayou as the best Creole restaurant in town, I couldn't help but be skeptical. It wasn't until a long workday that I finally decided to give it a try.

To top it all off, when I asked for the manager to discuss my disappointing experience, he simply shrugged and walked away, leaving me feeling completely ignored and insulted.

I left Taste of the Bayou feeling cheated and disappointed. This so-called hidden gem is nothing more than a sinking ship that's lost its way. I'd advise others to steer clear and stick with tried-and-true seafood spots instead.

But Wittgenstein asks: if these ‘automata’ are feigning being in pain, for example, how do you know that it is pain that they are feigning? For Wittgenstein, these criteria do not determine the certainty of statements, but the application of the concepts employed in these statements. Our understanding of that concept, our inclination to characterise whatever they are ‘feigning’ as pain, is nothing more or less than our ability to speak the language that we do. But if all the criteria are thus manifested, you are not missing anything. There is not a piece of knowledge (a glimpse ‘inside’ them) that you lack: what is at stake is whether you acknowledge that they are a someone, in pain. As Cavell writes, ‘the slack of acknowledgement can never be taken up by knowledge.’ Analogously, your own mind, your own feelings, are not logically occluded from being grasped by another. Manchester City, led by record-signing striker Sergio Aguero, broke the English Premier League's all-time points record on Saturday with a 4-1 win over West Bromwich Albion. The Citizens surpassed Chelsea's previous mark of 95 points set in the 2004-2005 season.

City's historic achievement comes on the heels of their Champions League quarterfinal exit at the hands of Tottenham Hotspur last week. With one eye on the FA Cup final against Watford in May, City will be aiming to complete a treble in Guardiola's third season at the club.

Title: Trendy Juice Bar - A Refreshing Stop Amidst the Dinner Rush Chaos! 

Had a delightful visit to this trendy juice bar during dinner rush yesterday, and let me tell you, it was a breath of fresh air amidst the city's hustle and bustle. The place was packed, but the lively atmosphere added to the overall experience rather than detracting from it.

I opted for their signature Acai Bowl - a heavenly blend of acai, mixed berries, granola, and honey - and a Green Machine juice (kale, spinach, apple, cucumber, ginger, and lemon). Both were fresh, vibrant, and oh-so-refreshing! The perfect antidote to the evening's heatwave.

Now, let me tell you about my wonderful interaction with the server. She was a ray of sunshine, greeting me with a warm smile even as she juggled orders left and right. When I mentioned that it was quite noisy, she not only empathized but also offered to find a quieter spot for me if one was available (unfortunately, they were all taken). She even shared some fun facts about the juice bar's history - a nice touch!

One small hiccup occurred when I asked her to hold the bee pollen in my Acai Bowl. Instead, she brought out the bowl with the pollen, and it took me a moment to realize that she hadn't heard me correctly. She apologized profusely and quickly whipped up another one for me without batting an eye.

All in all, I would give this juice bar a solid 4 stars! If you're looking for a health-conscious pit stop amidst the chaos of dinner rush, this is the place to be. Just remember to speak up if you have any dietary restrictions or preferences - they'll do their best to accommodate!

text: and what is going on. Humans are not simply social beings but political ones. From playgrounds to corridors of power, they are constantly forming, modifying, ending and generally complicating alliances. Stable cues that signal alliance, such as speech traits or styles of dress, offer regularities that help people orient themselves in the social whirl. Race might be one such cue, but Cosmides, Tooby and Kurzban’s experiments from 2001 show that it does not necessarily carry the weight its salience as a social issue would suggest. People do indeed seem to categorise each other by race automatically. Even so, the experimenters were able to downgrade it as a category in a matter of minutes by using neutral colours as markers. When people were told that individuals wearing grey or yellow shirts belonged to two groups, they relied much more heavily on the shirts than the ethnic appearance of the wearers to decide which group they belonged to. Other researchers have reported that accent also trumps race. Young children choosing friends seem to prefer children of other races who speak with the same accent to children of the same race who speak with a different one. Those American findings might seem familiar to people living in Britain, where the Union flag erased visible ethnic differences between the Olympic athletes of Team GB last summer, and young Londoners of all kinds of heritage share an accent peculiar to their singularly diverse city. Although people are hazy about what Britishness is, the vast majority of them no longer think it depends on race. At the same time, many of them mistrust Muslims and consider immigrants unwelcome. The great British in-group has reinvented both itself and its others. It is a much fairer nation than it was, inclusive to a degree unimaginable a generation or two ago. But in recomposing its coalitions, it inevitably sets up the potential for new biases and unfairness. Perhaps we simply have to accept that the relationship between us and them is always a work in progress.

Take happiness as an example. For the classical utilitarian, the fact that something increases happiness is always a reason to do it. But the particularist will point out that in the real world things are more complicated: the enjoyment of the sadist, for instance, is actually a further argument against an act of cruelty, not an argument in favor of it, or even a consideration that mitigates its badness. Conversely, refusing to make someone happy is sometimes morally right, as in cases of ‘tough love’: sometimes, as the Nick Lowe song has it, one must be cruel to be kind. To see that a person who appears to be acting cruelly is actually motivated by kindness, and indeed is being genuinely kind, is to grasp the correct shape of the situation, to latch onto the accurate description. And this accurate perception, again, tells us what we need to do to respond properly: it conveys what the situation demands, for, on the particularist view, these demands are quite literally part of the situation itself. Indeed, happiness is complicated in other ways as well, many of which are beginning to be articulated by psychologists and other happiness researchers. Early utilitarians such as Bentham held a very simple view of happiness, equating it with pleasure and assuming it was a unitary substance that could be empirically and objectively measured. However, more recent investigators tend to prefer a picture in which several distinct and perhaps incommensurable factors make contributions to a person’s happiness. This fits in well with the particularists’ view that evaluation is always a holistic matter. It is worth remembering, too, that Aristotle understood eudaimonia, which is frequently translated into English as ‘happiness’, as something considerably broader and less subjective than pleasure or momentary satisfaction. Instead, it has to do with the general quality of one’s life as a whole. For particularists, then, individual perception and judgment are always necessary to decide difficult ethical questions: there is no theoretical ethical system that can do the work for us. Principles are useful, perhaps, but only as rules of thumb, practical guidelines that hold for the most part, but to which there will always be exceptions. At the foundational level, ethics is built not on a system of rules, but on individual human beings who possess character, judgment, and wisdom. Particularism re-opens the door to the idea of wisdom. text:
I've been pondering about the intricate connections between empathy and altruism, two traits that seem to be deeply rooted in our biology. It's fascinating to delve into their origins, especially when we consider how they shape our interactions with one another and the world around us.

Take, for instance, mirror neurons - these little guys are responsible for our ability to empathize with others. They fire not only when we perform an action but also when we observe someone else doing it. This biological mechanism might explain why we feel a pang of sadness when watching a heart-wrenching movie or experience joy alongside a friend's triumph.

But here's the twist: empathy doesn't always lead to altruistic actions. We can easily observe someone else's pain and yet choose not to help. So, what drives us to act selflessly? That, my friends, is still up for debate among scientists and philosophers alike.

Some theories suggest that empathy triggers an emotional response in us, which then leads to altruism. But it's important to note that not everyone reacts the same way when faced with someone else's suffering. Some people might feel compelled to help, while others might turn away. It seems our responses are influenced by a myriad of factors, from our upbringing to cultural beliefs and personal values.

I've always been intrigued by the idea that altruism might have evolved as a way for us to survive in groups. By helping each other out, we could increase the chances of collective survival. Yet, I can't help but wonder if there isn't more to it than that. Is it possible that the desire to help others stems from something deeper within us - some primal need to connect and belong?

These questions have led me down a winding path, exploring theories about our evolutionary past, our social interactions today, and even the very nature of humanity itself. There are no easy answers here, just a complex tapestry of ideas that continues to unfold as we delve deeper into the mysteries of empathy and altruism.

It's an endless journey, this quest for understanding our own hearts and minds. But isn't that what makes it so captivating? The chance to unravel the threads of our own existence and glimpse the intricate patterns that bind us all together. For now, I'll keep exploring, one thought at a time...

Now, when I am watching sexual-assault trials unfold on the news, I find myself immobilised by a flood of troublesome memories. Each new case brings up debilitating recollections I’d rather forget. Yet the scrutiny these trials elicit also motivates me to remember more: in particular, to remember what I said, when and to whom, to check whether my story is consistent. Consistency is an everyday norm of judgment. It’s a common test of credibility in the courts, as well as a staple of epistemic practice – that is, a way of arriving at knowledge. An Australian study in 2004 found that consistency was the most frequently cited criterion by which juries assessed the credibility of a victim. They looked for consistency in the statements by the accuser, and also expected consistency in the victim’s post-assault behaviour. If that is the focus of juries, attorneys and prosecutors are bound to follow suit in an attempt to attain convictions. Judges sometimes give instructions to juries about ‘explainable’ discrepancies, but such directives are unlikely to pre-empt entrenched, pre-existing cultural narratives. In 2018, Christine Blasey Ford, a psychologist at Palo Alto University in California, accused the US Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh of sexual assault when they were teenagers. In the report issued by a Republican Party-appointed attorney, the first point claimed that: ‘Dr Ford has not offered a consistent account of when the alleged assault happened.’ Point three is that ‘Dr Ford has not offered a consistent account of the alleged assault.’ During cross-examination in the Senate, the attorney asked Blasey Ford about topics apparently peripheral to the event: the distance between her home and the house where the assault took place; the names and number of all of the people present; her reported fear of flying, her history of flights, and the reasons for and destinations of those flights. These questions were designed to test Blasey Ford’s consistency in reporting certain details, even when they were irrelevant to the accusation at hand. It was the answers that Blasey Ford gave to these questions that were used to discredit her reports of what Kavanaugh had done to her at that party. So what’s wrong with consistency? It might seem like a relatively objective measure, free of the implicit bias that affects other credibility judgments. Arguably, it’s a better indicator of truth than, for example, a confident demeanour, which might indicate entitlement, and can depend on how you are socialised by race, class and gender. Looking for inconsistency appears to replace fuzzy qualitative criteria with a hard-nosed quantitative one, and so to circumvent our susceptibility to prejudice. The criterion of consistency could serve as a modest baseline for establishing a credible accusation.

Although it raises important questions, Relihan’s reading is ultimately unpersuasive. A more plausible view is that Boethius the author did in fact intend Philosophy’s arguments to be taken seriously and tried his very best to put well-constructed reasonings into her mouth. **WASHINGTON: Declining Revenue at the US Federal Reserve Sparks Market Concerns**

Stocks dipped on Wall Street today following news of declining revenue at the US Federal Reserve. Analysts say this could impact monetary policy and potentially slow economic growth.

According to sources within the Fed, a decrease in interest income from its vast portfolio of Treasury securities is causing the shortfall. The Fed's annual report projects a $62 billion decline in revenue for 2021, the largest drop since the Great Recession.

The market reacted negatively to this news. The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell by 1.5%, and the S&P 500 dropped by 1.3%. Bonds also took a hit, with yields on 10-year Treasury notes rising from 0.92% to 0.94%.

"The Fed's decline in revenue could lead to tighter monetary policy, which could slow economic growth," said John Williams, chief economist at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. "Investors are understandably concerned."

Despite these concerns, some experts remain optimistic. They believe the Fed has tools to mitigate the impact on the economy and can still provide stimulus when needed. The Fed could sell some of its Treasury holdings or adjust interest rates to generate more income.

Looking ahead, analysts will be closely watching the Fed's actions and any potential changes in monetary policy. A shift towards tighter policy could have significant implications for the stock market and broader economy.

Pondering the ripples of Alan Turing's intellectual odyssey, one cannot help but marvel at how his ideas about machine intelligence continue to percolate through the ongoing debates within the realm of artificial intelligence. Like a stone tossed into a placid pond, his work has caused waves that ebb and flow, reshaping the landscape of our understanding. Yet, as we stand on these shifting sands, we find ourselves grappling with the limits of language to fully capture the essence of what Turing envisioned.

Turing's seminal paper, "Computing Machinery and Intelligence," introduced the concept of a machine that could imitate a human minda thought experiment that remains at the heart of our quest for artificial intelligence today. However, as we strive to create machines that mimic human cognition, we encounter a puzzling paradox: how can we program a machine to think like a human when the very act of thinking defies precise articulation?

Consider the Turing Test, which poses the question: can a machine exhibit behavior indistinguishable from that of a human being? A simple, elegant concept, yet it raises questions about the nature of intelligence and consciousness. Are we truly able to measure intelligence through a series of dialogues, or is there an intangible essence that eludes our grasp?

Moreover, as we delve deeper into the intricacies of machine learning and neural networks, we are confronted with a bewildering array of algorithms and equations. Yet, despite this apparent mastery over the mechanics, we find ourselves struggling to explain why these machines behave in certain ways or what they might be "thinking" when they make decisions.

In contemplating Turing's work, one cannot help but feel a pang of doubt, a gnawing uncertainty about our ability to fully comprehend the mysteries of intelligence. Is our understanding merely a mirage, a reflection of our own cognitive processes projected onto machines? Or is there a fundamental truth waiting to be uncovered, a key that will unlock the door to true artificial intelligence?

As we continue to probe these questions, we must also acknowledge the limitations of language in capturing the essence of intelligence. For, just as Koselleck observed in his analysis of history, there are events that defy articulationcascades of events that withhold themselves from language. It is perhaps in these silences that we will find our most profound insights into the nature of machine intelligence.

In delving deeper into this enigma, we are reminded of the words of the great poet Rilke: "Have patience with everything unresolved in your heart and try to love the questions themselves as if they were locked rooms or books written in a very foreign language." As we grapple with the questions posed by Turing's work, we must have patience and embrace the uncertainty, for it is here that our greatest insights may be found.

As I continue my exploration of this fascinating topic, I am left with more questions than answersa testament to the complexity and richness of the issues at hand. The journey ahead promises to be a challenging one, but also an exhilarating one, as we seek to unlock the secrets of machine intelligence and push the boundaries of what it means to be intelligent.

Title: A Disappointing Visit to the Overcrowded Bookstore Cafe - Not Worth Another Try

I've been a regular at this bookstore cafe for quite some time now, but my latest visit during happy hour was far from enjoyable. Let me start by saying that it was insanely crowded, making it difficult to find a table and adding to the overall chaotic atmosphere.

The place was relatively clean, but the excessive clutter of merchandise made it feel cramped and disorganized. I ordered their famous lemonade, which on a good day is refreshing, but this time tasted more like watered-down lemons than the tangy, sweet drink I've come to expect.

I also tried one of their popular sandwiches, but it was lackluster at best - dry bread and forgettable fillings. The worst part? The portion size was way too large, leaving me with leftovers that I had no intention of taking home.

Now, let's talk about takeout. When I ordered a sandwich to go, the packaging didn't do justice to the food inside. It was flimsy and cheap-looking, making me worry about the state of my sandwich by the time I got home. The lemons for my lemonade were also haphazardly tossed into a paper bag without any protection, ensuring an even more watered-down drink upon arrival.

To top it all off, the service was subpar. Our waiter seemed distracted and rushed us through our meal, hardly giving us enough time to enjoy our food or each other's company. I couldn't help but feel like just another number in their sea of customers.

In conclusion, my experience at this bookstore cafe during happy hour was less than satisfactory. With better organization, improved portion sizes, more attention to packaging quality for takeout orders, and attentive service, this place could be a contender. But as it stands now, I won't be returning anytime soon - or ever again, if I can help it.
