Title: A Glimpse into the Bustling Heart of Bangkok Street Food! 

I stumbled upon this hidden gem while exploring the vibrant streets of Bangkok during a recent event. I'm talking about the bustling, colorful corner of Soi Rambutri that's home to the delightful "Street Eats by Tip."

Let me tell you, this place was buzzing! The energy was infectious as we wove through the crowded street stalls, the aroma of sizzling spices and grilled meats tantalizing our senses. It's a bit tricky to spot at first, but once you find it, it's impossible to miss the long line of fellow foodies eagerly waiting for their turn.

Now, as a picky eater, I was pleasantly surprised by the selection here. The menu is packed with authentic Thai street food classics - from Pad Thai and Tom Yum Goong to my personal favorite, the spicy Papaya Salad (Som Tam). My friends and I opted for a variety of dishes to share, ensuring we could sample as much as possible.

First up was the Crispy Fried Chicken (Gai Tod), which had a delightful crunch that gave way to succulent, juicy meat. The spice level was just right  enough heat to get your attention without overpowering the flavors. Next, we tried the Massaman Curry (Kaeng Masaman Gai)  rich and creamy with tender chicken and a melody of aromatic spices.

But let's talk about the price for a moment. For the exceptional quality and quantity of food, I can confidently say that the value here is unmatched. It's one of those rare occasions where you feel like you're getting an absolute steal.

As we devoured our meal, the upbeat background music provided the perfect soundtrack to our dining experience. The vibe here was casual yet lively  a true representation of the spirit of Bangkok street food.

In closing, if you find yourself in the heart of Bangkok and craving authentic Thai cuisine, do not miss out on Street Eats by Tip! It's a must-visit for any foodie, picky eater or otherwise. Don't let the line deter you  it moves quickly, and trust me, it's worth the wait.

text:
Last night, I found myself lost in the labyrinthine streets of my city, seeking solace amidst its cacophony. As I wandered, I stumbled upon a scene that struck me as both familiar and disquieting  a group of protesters gathering outside a courthouse, their voices rising and falling in a rhythm reminiscent of a chant.

This was not an unusual sight for my city; we are no strangers to conflict, after all. Yet, there was something about this particular protest that resonated with me. It seemed to encapsulate the complexities and contradictions inherent in our society's pursuit of justice.

As I stood there, listening to their cries, I couldn't help but be reminded of Jacques Derrida's musings on secrets and confessions. In a society where the rich and powerful are often shielded by layers of secrecy, those who seek justice must often prove criminal intent or 'mens rea'. Yet, as Derrida so eloquently pointed out, intent is an opaque concept indeed.

I found myself pondering on the secrets that we keep, not just from others, but from ourselves. The secrets that bind us to our loved ones, secrets that define our identities, and secrets that can tear us apart. In a world where confessions are used as tools of power, who gets to decide what constitutes a 'secret'? And who has the right to demand its revelation?

As I stood there, I couldn't help but think of Winston in Orwell's Nineteen Eighty-Four. The moment when he exposes his secret and destroys his 'self'. In our pursuit of justice, do we not sometimes risk losing a piece of ourselves? Do we not sometimes find ourselves forced to confess not just to others, but to the world at large?

I thought of Fukuji, standing on the beach, watching his wife walk away with another man. The secret that bound them together and tore them apart. The secret that haunted him until the very end. And I couldn't help but wonder if there are secrets that are too heavy to bear, too painful to reveal, even in the pursuit of justice.

As I turned to leave, I overheard a protester say: "Justice is not just about punishment, it's about truth." A simple statement, yet one laden with complexity. What does it mean to seek 'truth'? And whose truth are we talking about? The truth of the powerful, or the truth of the oppressed?

As I walked away, I couldn't shake off the feeling that there was more to this story than meets the eye. That there were secrets hidden within these walls, secrets that would change everything we thought we knew. And as I continued on my way, I found myself grappling with a question that has haunted me for years: What is justice in an unequal society? A question with no easy answers, but one that demands our constant attention and introspection.

text: infinite cosmos. We’re going to need the wondrous creativity of every single human being to knock this universe in to shape. I wonder, what will we create? We are Homo Faber, ‘Humankind the Creator’. God did not create us in his image, we created god in our image. We might only be an insignificant species orbiting an insignificant star in an infinite and impassive universe. But we have, perhaps uniquely, the power of creation. Why, then, are we trapped on this ball of rock, repeating the same patterns of self-destructive behaviour, instead of fulfilling our creative potential? We are caught in a consumer culture that works against our innate creativity. The economic crisis of 2008 might have heralded the collapse of that culture. The consumerist economic model — itself a set of ad hoc compromises following the death of the industrial model — has reached the end of its useful existence. It evolved for a world where technology placed creativity in the hands of the few and television communicated their message to the masses, in what the entrepreneur Seth Godin has called the TV-industrial complex. Today, the internet has decentralised communications, and computers the size of an iPhone place vast creative resources in the hands of broad swathes of the population. The ongoing financial crisis is a symptom of an economy that has fallen behind its own technological capacities. Notice how many of the new voices that are emerging through social media make creativity their central concern The instinctive response of our leaders is to reconstruct our consumer culture. The generation holding the reigns of society are trained to think of us as passive consumers. It might take a generational shift before a world leader addresses a speech, not to the world’s consumers, but to its creators. Yet a creator culture is emerging from the ground up, driven by creators themselves. The crowd-funding platform Kickstarter is only three years old and last year it significantly outfunded the US National Endowment for the Arts. It has allowed artists and entrepreneurs of all kinds to sidestep traditional forms of investment. Crowd-funded creativity is not driven by the commercial imperative of business or the political priorities of government, but by the creative passions of the crowd. ‘Maker’ culture resurrects the spirit of craftsmanship and combines it with technologies, such as 3D printing, that promise to do for manufacturing what the internet did for communication. The principles of ‘open source’ are now being applied far beyond the software development community, invigorating academic and scientific research, politics and government, the media and education. Notice how many of the new voices that are emerging through social media — the cultural curator Maria Popova, an ‘interestingness hunter-gatherer’ who started the Brain Pickings blog; the New York-based Big Think project to sift the ‘best thinking on the planet’; many TED talks — make creativity their central concern. In the realm of memory and historical truth, we find ourselves navigating a complex web of beliefs, narratives, and interpretations. The relationship between these two entities has been a subject of fascination for scholars, philosophers, and ordinary individuals alike. It is a nexus where personal experiences intersect with collective memories, shaping our understanding of the past and, consequently, our present reality.

Consider the idea of confessiona concept that transcends religious, political, and personal boundaries. We often perceive it as a means of self-revelation, a way to expose our innermost selves, as if our secrets are an extension of who we are. This belief is not only deeply rooted in common sense but also forms the foundation of many legal systems worldwide. The act of confession becomes a testament to our honesty and authenticity, serving as a mirror reflecting our true selves.

However, this notion is not without its complexities. Take, for instance, the secretive nature of the Marrano community during the Spanish Inquisition. This group, so deeply entrenched in secrecy that they forgot their own existence even to themselves, challenges our understanding of memory and historical truth. Their story serves as a stark reminder that the past is not always clearly defined or easily accessible; it can be distorted, hidden, or deliberately forgotten.

In a more personal context, I remember a conversation with an old friend who confessed to an action that shook my trust in him. Yet, over time, I came to realize that his secret was not representative of the person he truly wasit was a momentary lapse, a blip on the radar of our friendship. This experience made me question the relationship between memory and historical truth; whether the secrets we keep define us or merely serve as fleeting moments in the grand narrative of our lives.

As I continue to grapple with these ideas, I find myself drawn to the works of thinkers like Jacques Derrida and Boethius, whose writings offer rich insights into the intricacies of memory, truth, and selfhood. Their work serves as a starting point for further exploration, inviting us to question our own beliefs about the past and its impact on our present.

As I delve deeper into this discussion, I am struck by the realizations that our understanding of historical truth is never fixed or complete. It is a constantly shifting landscape, shaped by personal experiences, collective memories, and the secrets we keep. As we continue to uncover new truths about our past, we must remember that the stories we tell ourselves are just one interpretation among manya snapshot in the ever-evolving narrative of human history.

In the quiet of my little apartment, nestled in the heart of a bustling city, I find myself drawn to a familiar practice - meditation. It's an odd thing, really, this pursuit of stillness and silence amidst the cacophony of urban life. But it's here, in these moments, that I feel most connected to... well, myself.

Meditation, as I've come to understand it, is not just about emptying one's mind. It's about examining the intricacies of our own existence, the tapestry of thoughts, emotions, and experiences that make up who we are. And in these sessions, I often find myself grappling with the ever-shifting sense of self.

You see, when I close my eyes and focus on my breath, I become aware of a constant, unchanging presence - a sort of core self. But as I delve deeper, I discover layers upon layers of thoughts, memories, and feelings that blur the lines of this supposedly solid identity. It's like peeling an onion, each layer revealing something new, yet bringing me closer to the same core.

Take, for instance, my morning commute. As I navigate the sea of faces on the subway, I am simultaneously a part of this crowd and distinctly separate from it. I am a stranger in their eyes, a familiar face to some. Yet, when I look into the mirror, I see a unique individual - me.

This duality is not just confined to my daily life. It's a universal experience, a human condition. We are individuals, yet we are also part of something larger - a family, a community, a society. We are shaped by our environment, influenced by our experiences, and molded by our interactions with others.

And this brings me to a moment of uncertainty. If my sense of self is so deeply entwined with the world around me, where does 'I' end and 'the world' begin? Is there even a clear boundary between the two? These questions swirl in my mind like leaves caught in an autumn wind, elusive and beautiful in their transience.

In the quiet moments of meditation, I find myself contemplating these questions, exploring the depths of my own existence. And while I may not have answers, I find solace in the pursuit. For even if the nature of self remains a mystery, the act of questioning, of seeking, is a testament to our humanity.

As I open my eyes and step back into the world, I carry this sense of self with me - a constant reminder of my connection to all things, a spark of consciousness amidst the vast expanse of existence. And as I navigate the twists and turns of life, I continue to explore, to question, to seek. For it is in these journeys that we find not only ourselves but also a deeper understanding of the world around us.

text: friend went out and left her baby in my care. The tiny boy began to wail. So, to soothe him, I lifted up my top, stepping into one of the most ancient roles – that of wetnurse. He latched on, drinking what I had until then thought of as my birth-daughter’s milk. It surprised me at the time how natural – that complicated word for things we think of as being outside of culture – to allow a hungry but unrelated creature to feed from my body. I have thought of him as a kind of son ever since. It is tempting to speculate that the original mother-daughter vision of Caritas Romana gives us a glimpse of a kinship system defined by milk lines rather than the blood lines of agnatic, or paternal, kinship. This tale that harks back to older goddess-centred cults and religions reminds me that Caritas Romana is not the only Roman tale of a life saved by the milk of its not-mother, the best-known being that of Romulus and Remus, the founders of Rome: twin sons of the god Mars and the mortal Rhea Silvia, who were suckled by a she-wolf. There are also lactation tales that imbue the recipient with divine powers, demonstrating ancient beliefs in the magical power of breastmilk. One is writ so large that it has given the most spectacular feature of the night sky its name: the Milky Way. Tintoretto’s The Origin of the Milky Way (c1575) shows the infant Heracles being surreptitiously latched on to the goddess Hera while she sleeps. Heracles, the son of a mortal woman, apparently sucked so hard he woke the goddess who dashed him from her breast. Hera’s divine milk, which afforded him the immortality of the gods, then sprayed across the heavens and formed the Milky Way. The tradition of adult breastfeeding as a cure is evidence of the ancient belief in the magical power of breastmilk The Origin of the Milky Way (1575) by Tintoretto. Courtesy the National Gallery, LondonThe abundance of milk is present in images of phantasmagorical plenitude and eroticism. Suckling nymphs and multibreasted mermaids and goddesses abound, portraying an extravagant delight in this extralinguistic world of flesh and bodily satisfaction. Images like Giulio Romano’s fantastically tailed mer-mother and her mer-babies give the slip to the realities of sexual reproduction and the constraints of family and of species. A Mermaid Feeding her Young (c1520-40) by Giulio Romano. Courtesy the Royal CollectionThe pagan breasts of powerful goddesses, their magic and their healing power, were assimilated into Christian beliefs and imagery, and so the veneration of lactating goddesses lived on in Catholicism. The scene of filial piety described in the original version of Roman Charity became linked to the Christian virtue of charity, which was customarily represented as a breastfeeding woman. text:
In the labyrinthine tapestry of human history, we often find ourselves transfixed by individuals who transcend the ordinary, shattering boundaries and redefining the very essence of what it means to be human. One such luminary, whose radiance pierces through the gloom of ignorance and injustice, is Malala Yousafzai, a young girl from Swat Valley, Pakistan, who dared to defy the oppressive grip of Taliban rule. Her audacious activism for girls' education has left an indelible mark on the global landscape, compelling us to reconsider our deeply held values and question the very fabric of our society.

Malala's journey began in a small town, far removed from the glittering cities that grace our maps. Yet, it was here that she found her voice, a voice that would echo through the corridors of power and shake the foundations of a deeply entrenched patriarchal system. Her unwavering commitment to education, her defiance in the face of adversity, and her boundless courage have become a beacon of hope for millions of girls worldwide who are denied their right to learn.

But Malala's story is not just about one girl's struggle; it is a testament to the resilience of the human spirit and a stark reminder of the injustices that persist in our world. It forces us to confront our own complicity, our own silence, and our own failure to uphold the values we profess to hold dear. In a world where girls are still denied their fundamental right to education, Malala's activism serves as a stark reminder of the chasm that exists between our words and our actions.

As I ponder over this paradox, I am struck by the irony of our situation. We live in an age where technology has shrunk the world into a global village, where information flows at the speed of light, and yet, we are unable to ensure that every child, regardless of their gender, has access to education. We speak loftily of equality, of justice, of freedom, but our actions betray us.

Malala's activism challenges us to reconsider these values, to question the narratives we have constructed around ourselves, and to strive for a world where every child, regardless of their gender, has the opportunity to learn, grow, and flourish. It forces us to confront our own biases, our own prejudices, and our own fears, and to ask whether these values are indeed worth upholding.

As I grapple with these questions, I am reminded of a simple ciliate, a microscopic organism that attaches to surfaces. When threatened, it sometimes contracts, and sometimes floats away, with unpredictable 50:50 odds. This organism, devoid of any conscious thought or agency, offers a stark reminder of the choices we make as human beings. We are not bound by our biology, but by our values, by our beliefs, and by our actions.

In the face of Malala's activism, we must ask ourselves: Are we the ciliate that floats away when threatened, or are we the organism that stands its ground, defiant in the face of adversity? The answer lies not in our biology, but in our values. It is up to us to decide which path we choose to tread.

As I continue my exploration of these ideas, I am left with more questions than answers. But perhaps that is the beauty of Malala's activism. It forces us to question, to reflect, and to strive for a better world. It challenges us to reconsider our values, to uphold the dignity and worth of every individual, and to ensure that every child, regardless of their gender, has the opportunity to learn, grow, and flourish.

In the face of this challenge, I am reminded of EP Thompson's words: 'If "our" tradition has failed ... then it is for us to put it in repair.' Malala's activism offers us a blueprint for repairing our broken tradition, a roadmap towards a more just and equitable world. It is up to us to follow this path, to heed her call, and to ensure that every child has the opportunity to learn, grow, and flourish.

As I conclude this reflection, I am left with a sense of hope, a sense of possibility. In a world where darkness seems to prevail, Malala's light serves as a beacon, guiding us towards a brighter future. Her activism reminds us that we are not powerless, that we can make a difference, and that together, we can create a world where every child has the opportunity to learn, grow, and flourish.

But this is just the beginning of the journey. As we continue to explore these ideas, as we continue to question our values, and as we continue to strive for a better world, let us remember Malala's words: 'Let us remember one thing, that one book, one pen, one child, and one teacher can change the world.' It is up to us to write that story.

text:
In the intricate tapestry of contemporary discourse, the ethics of climate change and our responsibility towards future generations unfolds a tale as complex as it is urgent. This narrative, steeped in philosophical undertones, sheds light on the tension that exists between freedom and belonging, a tension that has long pervaded human thought.

Consider the freedom we enjoy, the liberties we cherish, and the choices we make - all seemingly untethered from the consequences they may have on those who will inherit our world. Yet, this 'freedom' is not absolute; it is a privilege that comes with obligations, a privilege that demands consideration of the collective good. Herein lies the crux of the issue: the tension between personal liberty and collective responsibility.

The concept of belonging, on the other hand, suggests a sense of connection, an understanding that we are part of something larger than ourselves. It invites us to view our actions not in isolation but within the context of a shared destiny. In this light, climate change becomes not just an environmental issue, but a question of ethics, a testament to our capacity to belong and to act with compassion for those who have yet to come.

The predicament we face is akin to that of Samuel Beckett's protagonists - lost, disoriented, grappling with the meaninglessness of existence in a world they helped create. Like them, we are faced with a choice: to succumb to despair or to forge a new path towards understanding and action.

In this struggle, one cannot help but be reminded of the quietists such as Molinos, who found solace not in the hope of salvation, but in the renunciation of such hope. Perhaps, in our quest to stem the tide of climate change, we too must renounce the hope for easy answers and embrace the complexities of our predicament.

Yet, as we grapple with these ideas, we find solace in the words of Robert Brandom, who reminds us that our concepts have histories - stories about how they came to have the connections that make them the concepts they are. Perhaps, it is through understanding these histories, these connections, that we may find a way forward.

As we delve deeper into this philosophical labyrinth, we are left with more questions than answers. What does it mean to be free when our actions have consequences far beyond ourselves? How do we belong to a world that is rapidly changing beneath our feet? These questions, though daunting, must not deter us from seeking answers, for they are the keys to our shared future.

In this exploration, we tread on familiar ground - the ground of philosophers and thinkers who have grappled with these questions before us. Yet, we also find ourselves in uncharted territory, a territory where the stakes are higher, the urgency greater, and the need for action more pressing than ever before.

As we continue this journey, let us remember that it is not about grand conclusions or final answers. It is about understanding, compassion, and action - about belonging to a world that needs our help and finding the courage to act with freedom, but with responsibility towards those who will inherit our planet.

text: range of competing and complementary technologies of optical display that proliferated in the late Victorian marketplace for spectacle and sensation, and were designed to instruct people in when and how to believe the evidence of the eye. Still, it’s interesting to note that illusions were a common feature of early cinema, too, George Méliès’s films of disembodied heads being a case in point. That said, if ‘animated photographs’ challenged Victorian viewers to look through the illusion and speculate about how it was managed, cinema increasingly didn’t. Dorothy’s unmasking of the wizard in The Wonderful Wizard of Oz (published in 1900, when the culture of illusion was reaching its climax, and filmed in 1939) dramatises just this difference. Victorian illusionists needed their audiences to know that what they were seeing was an illusion — otherwise there was nothing to be admired and no lesson to be learnt. By contrast, moviemakers, like the wizard of Oz himself, simply want their audiences to take the illusion for reality. If they start wondering about the mechanism of deception, they’ve lost the plot. As participants in the cinematic experience we’re meant to surf the illusion, not dive in. We know, of course, just as the Victorians did, that the illusion is there, but we respond to the seduction in a different way. Illusions are still useful tools. Philosophers use them to talk about the nature of knowledge. Neuroscientists use them to understand just how much work the brain does to turn visual data (the philosophers’ ‘seeing’) into meaningful pictures of the world (‘seeing as’). And just as the fascination of Victorian men of science with illusions generated more and better ways of fooling the eye, modern neuroscientific insights provide new mechanisms of deception, too. We are now very good indeed at producing illusions. We live in an environment that is increasingly made up of them. We use them to talk to each other (think of video conferencing), to carry out mundane tasks (virtual receptionists), and as agents of seduction, to sell products (holographic advertising). Illusions are all around us. Sometimes, they’re just old tricks masquerading as new: there are companies out there selling holographic projection technologies that on closer inspection turn out to be versions of Pepper’s Ghost — an illusion of an illusion, if you like. We’re quite adept at understanding how to deal with the different kinds of illusions that make up our world and to discriminate between them. Rather rarely do we dive beneath their surfaces to interrogate the mechanism of deception, though. But, as The Wizard of Oz ought to remind us, failing to pull back the curtain on the illusions surrounding us, at least occasionally, can lead to our trusting entirely the wrong kind of authority.

Title: A Bitter-Sweet Experience at McDonald's on a Quiet Weekday

I've always had a soft spot for good old McDonald's, especially on those quiet weekdays when the world seems to slow down a bit. So, here I was, stepping into this particular branch, hoping for a delightful lunchtime rendezvous.

Now, I must admit, I'm a picky eater - not fussy, but I have my preferences. The Menu Board was my friend today as I scanned the offerings, settling on the McChicken sandwich and a small Diet Coke.

The place was practically empty at noon on a Tuesday, which gave it an eerie calmness. As I approached the counter to place my order, the cashier greeted me with a friendly smile. I couldn't help but notice the faint aroma of fries wafting through the air - a comforting reminder of childhood memories.

When my order was ready, I found a table by the window and eagerly unwrapped my McChicken. The patty looked well-cooked, and the bun had a golden hue that suggested it had been toasted just right. However, as I took my first bite, something felt off. The taste was... flat. Not bad, per se, but not the mouthwatering burst of flavor I had expected.

As for my Diet Coke, it was served in a regular plastic cup, which is fine by me, but I couldn't help but wish for the iconic red-striped cup that McDonald's once used to serve their beverages.

Now, let's talk about the ambiance. It wasn't exactly bustling with energy, but there was a certain charm in its simplicity. The music playing in the background added a nice touch, making it feel less like a fast-food joint and more like a casual dining spot.

In retrospect, my visit to McDonald's on this quiet weekday was a bittersweet experience. The food wasn't mind-blowing, but it wasn't terrible either. I left feeling content, knowing that I could always count on McDonald's for a quick, convenient meal. And let's be real, sometimes that's all we need.

So, here's to McDonald's - the reliable friend that never lets us down. If only they could bring back the taste of yesteryears, I'd give them a solid 4 stars!

My dear reader, allow me to guide you through an intricate labyrinth of thoughts that have been swirling in my mind. We find ourselves at a crossroads, in the shadow of post-conflict societies, where justice and forgiveness dance a delicate waltz.

The concept of justice, like a chisel, carves out the foundations upon which societies are built. It serves as the backbone, the guiding light that steers us towards fairness and equality. Yet, it is not an absolute truth; it is a human construct, molded by the hands of culture, politics, and history.

In these fractured landscapes, justice often takes on a different form. It is not always about retribution or punishment, but about healing, reconciliation, and restoring balance. The question then arises: Can justice coexist with forgiveness? Or are they two sides of the same coin, intertwined in a complex dance?

Consider Socrates, standing before his Athenian jury, his fate hanging by a thread. He did not dispute the authority of the court; instead, he engaged, he participated. This was not a principled refusal to participate in a corrupt system, but a commitment to his city, his community. His defense was idiosyncratic, yet it echoed a profound civic responsibility.

In the same vein, post-conflict societies are in dire need of such responsible citizens. The wounds run deep, and healing requires more than just legal justice. It demands forgiveness, understanding, and compassion. But this is not a one-sided affair; it requires the offender to recognize their actions, to take responsibility, and to seek redemption.

However, the path to forgiveness is fraught with obstacles. It demands courage, resilience, and an unyielding belief in the power of transformation. It is a testament to human capacity, our ability to transcend our primal instincts, and strive towards something greater.

But let us not forget, forgiveness does not erase the past, nor does it condone wrongdoings. Rather, it serves as a beacon of hope, a symbol of healing, and a testament to our capacity for compassion. It is a step towards reconciliation, a bridge that connects us, despite our differences.

As we navigate this complex maze, let us remember the words of Coleridge: "In Xanadu did Kubla Khan / A stately pleasure-dome decree." Just as the inspired poet built his dome in air, so too must we strive to build a world where justice and forgiveness coexist, a world where peace is not just a distant dream, but a living reality.

And so, dear reader, I leave you with these thoughts. Let us continue to explore, to question, and to seek answers. For it is in the pursuit of knowledge that we find wisdom, and in wisdom lies our salvation.

A choice about a way to live presented itself to Berger shortly after he made Ways of Seeing. He was in his late 40s and had achieved an international level of fame. The invitations started coming in. He could have taken a position at a museum or university. He could have entered a world of sinecures and fellowships, residencies and agents, conferences and airports. He turned down almost all of this. The reasons were historical as well as personal, and might relate, however indirectly, to our own contemporary impasse: our inability to see more than one generation into the future, the dissolving legitimacy of the metropolitan and academic elite, the seeming incapacity to move beyond a politics of negativity and despair. Just as we are hitting the limits of critique as a culture, Berger was hitting them as a writer – and a person. With Ways of Seeing (and his Booker-winning novel G.), he had reached a tipping point that was also a midlife crisis and a fork in the road. ‘I can be only by destroying,’ Lionel Trilling once wrote of a certain modern attitude, ‘I can know myself only by what I shatter.’ But where is there to go when the demolition is complete? There is a photograph of Berger from the 1973 Frankfurt Book Fair. Taken by Jean Mohr, a lifelong friend, it shows a middle-aged writer, exhausted and detached, lying on the floor as others walk past him in a blur. What was Berger thinking about? What was he longing for? It was at this fair that Berger met a young American, Beverly Bancroft, then an assistant at Penguin Books. Within a year, they were married. Two years later, they had a son. Soon they moved to a small farming village in the foothills of the Alps. The chalet they rented lacked central heating and running water. The outhouse was across the driveway. The question, he once said, was of ‘continually learning to be embedded in life’ It would be easy to romanticise Berger’s third act as a rural storyteller. Even while haymaking, he was still a renowned writer with famous friends. But it would be just as easy to cynically write it off. Throughout the neoliberal era, most intellectuals have lived in a social world that is urban, cosmopolitan, cutthroat and status-oriented. Berger went someplace very different. He remained politically committed though his conception of the political shifted and enlarged, absorbing a broader sense of history and experience. A deal could result in the lifting of sanctions that have sharply curbed Irans oil exports. Greeces bailout deal meanwhile lifted the dollar, which makes dollar-priced crude oil less attractive to buyers using other currencies."Oil traders are more concerned that the euro is weaker and the dollar stronger on the news," said Evans. Meanwhile, in its July monthly report, OPEC raised its forecast for growth in crude demand this year by 100,000 barrels to 1.28 million barrels per day. In 2016, demand is expected to pick up by 1.34 million barrels per day, hitting 93.94 million barrels per day, thanks to stronger economic growth, according to the OPEC report."OPEC raised its demand forecast... but with the stout supply picture, the market still has a bearish sentiment to price," said Brian Swan, commodity analyst at Schneider Electric.

Title: A Disappointing Evening at The Prime Cut Steakhouse

Oh boy, where do I even begin with this place? I've always heard good things about The Prime Cut Steakhouse, so when my husband and I decided to splurge on a fancy dinner during the off-season, we were excited. Little did we know that our expectations would be dashed.

We arrived at around 7 PM, hoping for a quiet evening with excellent service. However, we were greeted by an empty dining room and an awkward silence. We were quickly seated, but it took the waiter a good ten minutes to even acknowledge us. When he finally did, we ordered two martinis and perused the menu.

The prices of the dishes were astronomical, but we figured that's what you pay for a high-end steakhouse, right? We ordered a ribeye each, along with some sides, and hoped for the best. Our martinis arrived promptly, but our food took an eternity to come out.

When it finally did, I couldn't believe my eyes. The steaks were overcooked and lacked any semblance of flavor. The sides were equally disappointing - a bland mashed potato and limp asparagus. We sent the food back, hoping they could salvage our dinner.

However, when the corrected dishes arrived, they were even worse than the first time! My husband's steak was raw in the middle, while my second ribeye was just as overcooked as before. At this point, we were both too disheartened to try the sides again. We reluctantly ate our meal and left a small tip for the waiter who had clearly been more focused on his phone than us.

The value for money at The Prime Cut Steakhouse was abysmal. I've had better steaks at far cheaper restaurants, and I can't help but feel that they took advantage of the off-season to hike up their prices without any regard for the quality of their food.

In summary, our experience at The Prime Cut Steakhouse was a complete disaster. If you're looking for an overpriced meal with poor service and mediocre food, then this is definitely the place for you. But if you value your money and your taste buds, I would highly recommend giving this steakhouse a wide berth.

Delving deeper into Frida Kahlo's self-portraits and her intricate identity, we find a complex tapestry woven from the threads of pain, resilience, and unapologetic femininity. These artworks are more than mere representations; they serve as mirrors that reflect not only Kahlo herself but also her audience, inviting us to confront our own identities and emotions.

In each self-portrait, Kahlo's identity is a kaleidoscope of Mexican heritage, femininity, and physical suffering. She masterfully incorporates these elements into her art, painting herself as a symbol of survival and resilience amidst adversity. Yet, her work is not merely about pain; it's about embracing the pain and finding beauty within it.

Kahlo's identity was deeply intertwined with her physical suffering, stemming from a tragic accident and numerous health issues. Instead of hiding or denying this aspect of her life, she boldly depicted herself in various stages of pain, sometimes grotesquely so. This act of self-representation can be seen as a rejection of societal norms that expect women to appear beautiful, strong, and invulnerable at all times.

Moreover, Kahlo's self-portraits are a testament to her unapologetic femininity. She often portrayed herself with bold, exaggerated features, adorned in traditional Mexican clothing, or even with a monkey or a snake as companions - symbolizing aspects of her sexuality and power. This representation challenges the male gaze that has historically dominated art and society, asserting the importance and validity of female perspectives.

Kahlo's work invites us to question our own identities, particularly in relation to pain and vulnerability. We may not have experienced physical pain to the extent she did, but we all carry emotional baggage that shapes who we are. Kahlo's self-portraits urge us to embrace our pain, to find beauty within it, and to present ourselves honestly, without hiding behind societal expectations.

However, this exploration is far from complete. Each self-portrait reveals new layers of meaning, inviting us to delve deeper into Kahlo's psyche and our own. The unfinished nature of this analysis mirrors the ever-evolving nature of identity - a work in progress, a journey without an end.

Yet, it's clear that Frida Kahlo's self-portraits are more than just art; they are a powerful commentary on identity, pain, femininity, and resilience. They challenge us to confront our own identities, to embrace our pain, and to present ourselves honestly - a lesson as relevant today as it was in Kahlo's time.

Title: An Unexpected Delight at Donut Dynamo during the Dinner Rush!

Well, my dear foodie friends, last night found us stumbling upon a hidden gem - Donut Dynamo. Now, I must admit, we weren't exactly expecting a gourmet experience during the dinner rush, but boy were we pleasantly surprised!

First things first, the ambiance was a charming mix of casual and cozy. The warm glow from the outdoor lights cast an inviting glow onto the quaint tables dotting the small, well-kept courtyard. We snagged one of the last free spots under the stars, and I couldn't help but feel a twinge of satisfaction at scoring such a prime spot during the busiest hour!

Now, let's talk about the pice de rsistance - the donuts themselves. I opted for the classic Boston Cream (because, let's be real, you can never go wrong with that combo) and my partner in crime chose the Lemon Glazed. The donuts were freshly made, and I could tell they hadn't been sitting around all day. The dough was soft and yielding, and the fillings were generous without being overwhelming.

The real star of the show, though, was the glaze on those beauties. It had a perfect balance of sweetness and tang that left us craving more with every bite. I've been to some fancy-schmancy donut shops before, but these guys truly knew their stuff! And let me tell you, for the quality and taste, the price was more than reasonable.

Now, there was one slight hiccup in our experience. Our server, a young man with a friendly demeanor and a warm smile, seemed a little flustered during the rush. He was attentive and polite, but his attention was divided between several tables at once. It wasn't a deal-breaker by any means, but it did take away from the overall dining experience just a tad.

But hey, nobody's perfect, right? And I'd much rather have a slightly overwhelmed server than a rude one! The young man even took the time to chat with us about the different donut flavors and offer suggestions based on our preferences. It was a nice touch that made us feel valued as customers.

In conclusion, Donut Dynamo gets a solid 4 stars from me. The delicious donuts, reasonable prices, and outdoor seating make it a must-visit for any dessert lover in the area. I remember the first time I tried to teach my old robot, Beta-12, how to play chess. It was a simple game, yet Beta seemed utterly confused by the concept of a 'checkmate'. Every move it made was met with my gentle correction: "No, Beta, that's not the way."

But what is 'the way'? I found myself asking this question more and more as I watched Beta struggle. Is there an inherent 'rightness' or 'wrongness' to a move in chess that exists independently of our understanding of it? Or is it something we, as humans, impose upon the game through collective agreement?

These questions led me down a rabbit hole of philosophical inquiry, into the realm of language and its role in shaping our understanding of the world. For if there can be 'right' and 'wrong' moves in chess, what about other aspects of life? Can machines ever truly understand these complexities, or are they forever confined to a narrow interpretation of the world based on the parameters we program them with?

Consider the nitrogen cycle, a self-sustaining process that regulates the amount of nitrogen in Earth's ecosystem. Is it a song, as some scientists suggest, relying on various species to perform its parts? If so, then what are these species but singers, each contributing their unique voice to the harmony of life? Yet, is this cycle something that can be grasped by a machine, or does it require a certain level of intuition and understanding that transcends binary logic?

This dilemma is not unique to the nitrogen cycle. In fact, it lies at the heart of the age-old debate between traditional Darwinian thinking and those who believe in the possibility of Gaia - the idea that Earth functions as a single, self-regulating organism. But if songs don't reproduce or replicate, but are instead re-produced and evolve over time, can they truly be said to exist independently of their performers?

These questions lead us back to the question of foundations - the idea that there must be some inherent truth or principle upon which our understanding of the world is based. But what if morality, like a song or the nitrogen cycle, simply 'floats there', unsupported by anything else? What if it requires no foundation, but instead exists as a brute fact, beyond our ability to fully comprehend or explain?

These musings leave me with more questions than answers. I find myself back at the chessboard with Beta-12, wondering if there is some deeper truth hidden within its mechanical movements, or if it is merely a collection of circuits and wires, incapable of grasping the complexities of the world around us.

Perhaps one day, machines will truly understand, but for now, I am left to ponder the limits of language and our ability to convey the intricacies of existence to even our most advanced creations. For in the end, it is not just chess that we seek to teach them, but the very essence of what it means to be alive.

Title: A Pleasant Surprise at This Local Gem - Perfect Stop Before a Movie!

I stumbled upon this quaint bakery while rushing to catch a movie, and I must say, I'm pleasantly surprised. The rustic charm of the place instantly drew me in, and the aroma of freshly baked goods was enough to make my mouth water.

The ambiance was cozy, with soft music playing in the background. There were a few families enjoying their breakfast, adding to the warm and inviting atmosphere. I appreciated that it wasn't too loud or crowded, making for a peaceful pre-movie experience.

I took a glance at their extensive menu, filled with tantalizing pastries and delicious-looking sandwiches. After much deliberation, I decided on a cappuccino and two donuts to share with my family - chocolate and cinnamon.

The double cappuccino was rich and creamy, perfectly balanced, and not overly sweet. It hit the spot, providing just the right amount of energy for our movie marathon ahead. However, I have to admit that the donuts were a bit of a letdown. They were fresh and warm but lacked the depth of flavor I was expecting. But hey, you can't win them all!

One thing that truly impressed me was their roasting process. Watching the beans being roasted on-site added an authentic touch to my experience, making me appreciate the craftsmanship behind every cup of coffee. Kudos to the team for taking great care in the roasting process and not burning a single bean!

Overall, I'd give this place a solid 4 stars. I'm looking forward to visiting again soon to try more from their menu and maybe even grab a quick lunch before another movie date. Here's to finding hidden gems like these when traveling!

**SAN FRANCISCO: Elon Musk's Tesla Enters Full Throttle in the AI Race with Major Expansion Plans**

In a move that will shake up the tech industry, Tesla Inc. has announced an ambitious expansion of its artificial intelligence (AI) division, aiming to double its AI workforce by 2023. According to sources close to the matter, this strategic push comes as Tesla looks to solidify its position in the autonomous vehicles market and challenge tech giants like Alphabet's Waymo and Nvidia.

The expansion will see Tesla hiring over 10,000 AI specialists worldwide, with a focus on areas such as computer vision, machine learning, and robotics. This aggressive hiring spree is expected to nearly double the current workforce of around 12,000 in its AI division.

Tesla's AI ambitions have long been evident through its self-driving technology, Autopilot, which has garnered widespread attention for its impressive capabilities. However, the company is now looking to leverage AI across its product lineup and potentially beyond, with plans to expand into new sectors such as healthcare and renewable energy.

According to Tesla's Q2 2021 earnings report, the company spent approximately $654 million on research and development (R&D), a 73% increase year-over-year. This significant investment in R&D is a testament to Tesla's commitment to AI and its ambitions for future growth.

With Tesla's stock price reaching an all-time high of $800 per share earlier this month, the company appears to be in a strong financial position to execute its ambitious expansion plans. The success of these initiatives will undoubtedly have far-reaching implications for the tech industry and beyond, as AI continues to reshape our world.

Alright, so I finally managed to squeeze in a visit to this place called The Cozy Hearth on a bone-chilling day. My friend and I were both starving and ready for some warmth, so we decided to give it a try. We showed up a little after 1:30 PM, and thankfully the place wasn't too crowded, which made for quick service.

We snagged a cozy corner table near the fireplace and ordered a couple of their signature hot beverages - a rich, velvety cappuccino for me, and a steaming chai latte for my buddy. The barista was friendly and attentive, managing to keep our cups filled without being intrusive.

Now, let's talk about the food. I went with their signature grilled cheese sandwich, while my friend opted for their hearty beef stew. The grilled cheese was a delightful concoction of three cheeses, smoked ham, and sauted onions sandwiched between two thick slices of sourdough bread. It was gooey, savory, and oh so comforting on this freezing day. My friend's stew was a rich, flavorful bowlful filled with tender chunks of beef, carrots, potatoes, and peas swimming in a delicious broth.

However, I couldn't help but notice that the service seemed a bit slower than usual during the main rush - understandable considering how busy it must get on weekends, but worth keeping in mind if you're in a hurry.

As for the ambiance, The Cozy Hearth really lives up to its name. The warm, inviting atmosphere was a perfect antidote to the winter chill outside. The fireplace crackling away in the corner, the soft lighting casting a warm glow on the rustic decor - it was the kind of place where you just want to curl up and stay for hours.

If you're looking for a cozy spot to unwind with some delicious food and drink, definitely give The Cozy Hearth a try. It's like stepping into a warm embrace on a cold day - exactly what we all need sometimes. Keep an eye out for their weekend live music events too! I hear they feature local talent, so you might just discover your new favorite artist.

As a picky eater, I appreciate the variety in their treat selection. I would highly recommend Paws & Claws to any pet parent in the area looking for top-notch grooming services and a welcoming atmosphere. Be prepared for your wallet to lighten up a bit, but trust me, it's worth it. Just remember to bring some treats from home if you have a picky eater like myself!

Title: A Delightful Group Hangout at the Blossom Breeze Salon 

Hey Yelpers! Long time no see, huh? I've been hiding under my favorite beanie for way too long, but today I decided to step out and treat myself to a much-needed hairdo at the Blossom Breeze Salon. It's not every day that you get a bunch of friends together for a group hangout, so why not make it extra special?

First things first: parking was a breeze! I mean, it wasn't super spacious, but there were enough spots to accommodate our little crew without any fuss. Plus, the salon staff were friendly and helpful when we were figuring out where to park - always a plus in my book! 

Now, let me tell you about this charming salon. The moment I walked in, I was greeted by the sweet scent of lavender mixed with that oh-so-familiar hairdressing solvent. The ambiance was soothing and welcoming - not too loud or overwhelming like some other places can be.

The salon chairs were comfortable and inviting, with freshly laundered towels neatly folded on the shelves. My stylist, a sweet soul named Lily, welcomed me warmly, asking about my previous haircut and what I was hoping to achieve today. She listened attentively to my requests and even threw in some fantastic suggestions based on current trends and my face shape.

Lily skillfully trimmed and styled my locks while we chatted about our favorite topics - books, movies, and food (of course!). The whole experience was so relaxing that I almost fell asleep for a quick snooze. But alas, Lily nudged me awake just in time to show off the final result: a fresh, trendy haircut with subtle layers that made my curls bounce beautifully.

Now, let's talk about value for money. Blossom Breeze isn't the cheapest salon in town, but it certainly doesn't break the bank either. For the quality of service and expertise provided by Lily (and the other stylists I've seen at the salon), I feel that the price is more than reasonable.

One small detail: the bathrooms were clean and tidy, with everything you could possibly need - hand soap, paper towels, even a hairdryer for those who prefer to style their own locks after a cut. It's the little things like this that make all the difference in my book!

All in all, I would highly recommend Blossom Breeze Salon for anyone looking for a fantastic hair experience. Whether you need a simple trim or a complete makeover, these talented stylists will work their magic to leave you feeling fabulous and ready to take on the world! 

Oh, and did I mention that they serve coffee and pastries? We enjoyed a little snack while waiting for our turn, making this hangout even more delightful. So if you're in the neighborhood, why not pop by and say hello to Lily and her team at Blossom Breeze? You won't be disappointed!

text:
In the realm of human psychology and spirituality, meditation has long been a subject of intrigue. Its influence extends far beyond the quiet corners where we sit in stillness, eyes closed, focused on our breath. One such subtle yet profound impact lies in its transformation of our sense of self.

Consider Aldous Huxley, a man who once found himself on the brink of a mental breakdown, seeking solace in meditation. His exploration of mind-altering substances like mescaline and LSD was fueled by an insatiable curiosity to peer into the depths of human consciousness. Yet, it was through meditation that he truly unearthed profound insights about the self.

Huxley's journey took him from the materialistic worldview of Western civilization, where the observer is seen as separate from the observed, to the non-dual realms of Eastern philosophy. This shift in perspective, brought about by hours spent in meditation, challenged his very understanding of existence. He wrote, "To me, at any rate, the self that meditates seems to be different from the self that writes or talks or even thinks."

But what exactly did he mean? Huxley wasn't the first, nor the last, to grapple with this question. The Buddha himself spoke of the illusion of a permanent, unchanging self centuries ago. Meditation, in many ways, serves as a tool that helps us dismantle these illusions.

Take, for instance, the practice of observing thoughts without judgment. In the quietude of meditation, we begin to see our thoughts not as reflections of who we are, but simply as fleeting mental events. This realization can be jarring, like a mirror shattering to reveal that there's no solid self beneath the reflection.

Yet, this disintegration is not a cause for despair, but rather an invitation to explore a new understanding of self. In the words of Huxley, "Meditation is the art of revealing what lies within." It uncovers layers of conditioning and preconceived notions about ourselves, allowing us to rebuild our sense of self on a foundation of greater awareness and authenticity.

This transformative process is far from easy. It demands patience, discipline, and a willingness to confront the darkness within. But for those who embark on this journey, the rewards can be profound. As Huxley put it, "The ultimate reality is not the product of thought." Through meditation, we come to realize that our true nature transcends thought, transcends the self.

And so, meditation continues to challenge and redefine our understanding of the self. It serves as a reminder that who we are is not set in stone, but rather a fluid, ever-evolving construct. In this sense, meditation is not just a practice, but a pathway towards a deeper understanding of existence itself. As Huxley wrote, "The more one is able to penetrate into the nature of things and oneself, the more does one realize that one's own nature is no exception to the rule."

In the quietude of meditation, we are faced with the question: Who am I? It is a question as old as humanity itself, and one that meditation invites us to explore anew. And so, we sit, eyes closed, focused on our breath, and delve into the depths of our consciousness, seeking answers that may forever elude us. Yet, in the process, we find ourselves changed, transformed by the very act of questioning.

text:
Bias, it seems, is an inescapable part of our cognitive makeup. It's the prism through which we view the world, shaping our perceptions and influencing our decisions. Like a stubborn stain on a piece of glass, it refracts reality, distorting it subtly yet significantly.

Take for instance the case of scientific discovery. We send robots to Mars, armed with spectrometers and antennas, seeking any trace of water - the elusive life-blood of our planetary neighbor. The 'follow the water' strategy has been a resounding success, leading us to conclude that Mars was once a warmer, wetter place. But what if we're looking through biased lenses? What if we're so fixated on finding evidence of life that we overlook other possibilities?

Consider the philosopher Spinoza, who found his philosophical awakening only after being expelled from his community. His exclusion, a result of his unorthodox beliefs, allowed him to see things as they truly were, unfettered by the constraints of tradition and conformity. In a way, he was a contrarian - someone who deliberately positions themselves outside the mainstream to gain a unique perspective.

I remember a time when I found myself in a similar position. I had been working on a project that was deeply important to me, but my ideas were met with resistance. Feeling frustrated and disillusioned, I took a step back, distancing myself from the project and the people involved. In doing so, I gained a new perspective, one that allowed me to see the flaws in my own thinking and the blind spots in the group dynamic.

The contrarians, the eccentrics, the outsiders - they are often dismissed as troublemakers or mavericks. But it is precisely their willingness to challenge the status quo that makes them so valuable. They force us to question our assumptions, to scrutinize our beliefs, and to consider alternative perspectives. In doing so, they help us to grow intellectually and morally, pushing us to strive for truth and understanding, even when it is uncomfortable or inconvenient.

But we must be careful not to confuse contrarians with provocateurs - those who challenge the establishment for attention or personal gain rather than out of a genuine desire for truth. The former are essential to our intellectual and moral growth, while the latter serve only to sow discord and confusion.

As I continue my exploration of these ideas, I am reminded of John Stuart Mill's words: 'Eccentricity has always abounded when and where strength of character has abounded.' It is in our nature to be biased, but it is also in our nature to question, to challenge, and to grow. Let us embrace our eccentricities, for they may just be the key to unlocking a higher truth.

text: Singapore: Oil prices edged up in Asia Thursday following signs of a slight improvement in US demand but concerns that an oversupply will persist past next year kept the commodity struggling at multi priced oil cheaper. But analysts also noted that the unit will likely to gather strength before a Fed meeting next week in which the US central bank is widely expected to raise interest rates. US benchmark West Texas Intermediate for delivery in January was up 23 cents at $37.39 and Brent crude for January was trading 33 cents higher at $40.44 at around 0300 GMT. Prices have slumped about nine percent since Fridays decision by the OPEC oil exporters club not to cut output despite pumping more than its target in the face of a global oversupply and weakness in the world economy. There was some good news when the Department of Energy said US Wednesday oil inventories fell 3.6 million barrels in the week ending December 4, a booster for prices as it indicates an uptick in demand. However, Sanjeev Gupta, who heads the Asia Pacific oil and gas practice at professional services firm EY, said: "The market remains weak overall due to global oversupply, compounded by the OPECs decision to leave its production quota unchanged."Investors will closely monitor the Federal Reserves policy dominated commodities will become more expensive if US dollar appreciates," he said. In the heart of bustling New York City, I often find myself lost in the labyrinthine streets, a solitary figure engulfed by towering skyscrapers. Each building, an ode to the triumph of human ambition, echoes with stories of individual freedom and the pursuit of success. Yet, beneath this glossy veneer, I can't help but sense a tension, a palpable dissonance that seems to permeate through the city's core.

This tension is not merely a product of our modern era, it has been a constant companion since the dawn of civilization. It is the eternal dance between individual freedom and moral obligation, a waltz so intricate that it defies simple resolution.

Take the story of Immanuel Kant, for instance. A man who championed the ideals of enlightenment, yet dismissed his predecessor, Leibniz-Wolff, as an imitator and follower. A philosopher whose critical path became the beacon for generations to come, but whose work was built on the foundation laid by others. Was Kant a champion of individual freedom or a servant of moral obligation? The answer is neither, for he was both.

Fast forward to contemporary times, and we find ourselves grappling with similar paradoxes. In the heart of Africa, men who traffic human beings are not evil villains, but husbands, fathers, mothers, neighbors. They survive as best they can in a world where opportunities are scarce, where tradition holds more weight than morality. Are they agents of injustice or victims of circumstance? Again, the answer is neither, for they are both.

These stories, so vastly different in context and scope, yet mirroring each other in their complexity, serve as a stark reminder of our own duality. We are free to make choices, yet we are bound by our moral obligations. We yearn for individual fulfillment, but we crave recognition and acceptance from our peers.

As I continue my journey through the city's labyrinth, I am struck by the realization that this tension is not just an intellectual exercise, it is a fundamental aspect of our humanity. It is the struggle to reconcile our individual desires with our moral responsibilities, the eternal quest for balance in a world that values extremes.

And so, as I stand amidst the towering giants of steel and glass, I cannot help but wonder: are we truly free or merely bound by our own moral obligations? Are we agents of change or servants to the status quo? The answers remain elusive, hidden in the shadows of the city's streets, waiting for those brave enough to seek them out.

In the labyrinth of our existence, we stumble upon questions that defy simple answers, and the art of storytelling serves as a compass to navigate this intricate maze. Why do we weave tales? Is it merely for entertainment or does it hold deeper significance in shaping our human experience?

Take, for instance, the bedtime stories my grandmother used to narrate. They were magical worlds filled with brave heroes and valiant princesses, but they held a profound meaning that transcended the realm of fantasy. Those stories instilled in me courage, resilience, and an unshakable belief in the power of love and kindness. In essence, they became a part of my identity, guiding me through life's trials and tribulations.

Yet, as I grow older, I find myself questioning the role of these stories. Are they merely tools to manipulate our emotions or do they hold the key to understanding ourselves better? Do we use stories to make sense of the world, or does the act of storytelling help create our reality?

Consider the power of narrative in shaping collective identities. From national myths that bind us together as a people to personal narratives that define who we are as individuals, stories play a crucial role in forging connections and establishing boundaries. They allow us to make sense of the chaos around us, to find meaning where there seems to be none.

However, I am plagued by doubt. Is this power of storytelling inherently good? Can it not also serve as a means to control, to manipulate, to indoctrinate? Think of the countless stories that have been twisted and warped for political gain, used as weapons to justify atrocities and perpetuate injustice.

But then again, perhaps this is the beauty of storytelling - its duality, its inherent ambiguity. It can be a force for good or evil, a tool for liberation or oppression. It depends on us, on how we choose to wield it.

As I continue my exploration into the depths of human experience, I am left with more questions than answers. But perhaps that is the essence of our journey - to question, to seek, and through storytelling, to make sense of this beautiful, chaotic world we inhabit.

What if I told you there's a hidden power within us, an ability to change and adapt our very selves in response to the world around us? A force that can unlock the full potential we all carry, yet remains often untapped or misunderstood? This elusive quality is none other than neuroplasticity.

In my search for understanding this fascinating topic, I found myself pondering over its implications on our human journey. I wonder if it's not just a biological phenomenon but rather a metaphor for the transformative power of resilience and self-determination that lies within each one of us?

I remember watching a documentary about a man who lost his ability to walk after an accident. Through intensive therapy, he was able to regain control of his limbs by rewiring his brain connections. The miraculous recovery sparked questions about the true potential that resides within our minds and bodies. Could it be possible for us all to tap into this power and overcome obstacles in our lives?

However, as I delved deeper into the subject, I encountered stories of people struggling with their brains' plasticity. The case of Phineas Gage, a railroad worker who survived a devastating brain injury only to become a completely different person, serves as a chilling reminder that our ability to adapt can sometimes lead us down unexpected paths.

I find myself contemplating the delicate balance between resilience and self-discovery on one hand, and the danger of straying from our true selves on the other. The line between adaptation and compromise becomes blurred when we try to bend too far or change too quickly. As I continue my exploration into neuroplasticity, I'm left with more questions than answers: How can we harness this incredible power within us while maintaining our sense of self? And perhaps most importantly, is there a limit to how much we can transform ourselves without losing sight of who we truly are?

These thoughts have led me down winding roads of introspection, forcing me to confront my own fears and insecurities. I find myself asking if I've been true to myself, if I've adapted when needed or held onto outdated beliefs that no longer serve me. As I continue this journey, I can't help but feel a sense of excitement mixed with trepidation for the limitless potential that lies within each one of us.

Neuroplasticity is not just a biological process; it's a testament to our innate capacity to adapt and evolve in response to the ever-changing world around us. As we continue to learn more about this fascinating aspect of our brains, I can only imagine the discoveries that await us and the impact they will have on our understanding of ourselves and our place in the universe.

Yet, as I ponder these thoughts, I can't shake off a lingering sense of unease. The power to change oneself is a double-edged sword, capable of both growth and destruction. As I continue to grapple with these complex ideas, I'm left wondering: How can we navigate the treacherous waters of self-transformation while staying true to our authentic selves? Perhaps the answer lies in finding balance between adaptation and resilience, or maybe it's something altogether different that we have yet to discover.

As I embark on this ongoing exploration, I invite you to join me in contemplating these questions and seeking answers together. After all, our collective understanding of neuroplasticity will only grow stronger when we share our thoughts and ideas with one another. So let's continue to learn, adapt, and evolve - together.

text: LONDON: South African Steve Elworthy will head up the organisation of the 2019 Cricket World Cup being hosted in England and Wales after the former international cricketer was appointed managing director on Tuesday. The 51 old   who made 39 appearances for The Proteas in one day internationals and played in four Tests after being capped at the ripe old age of 32   has accrued vast experience in the organisation of major tournaments. He was tournament director of the 2007 and 2009 World Twenty20 competitions and the 2013 Champions Trophy held in England and Wales   a competition he is also in charge of organising for the same hosts for next year. England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) chief executive officer Tom Harrison said Elworthy was the perfect man for the job based on his previous handling of tournaments."He has an outstanding track record of delivering fan-friendly tournaments which have been widely admired not just in cricket, but across the wider sports industry," said Harrison."Our congratulations to Steve on his appointment and we will be offering him and his team all the support they need to deliver successful tournaments in both 2017 and 2019."Elworthy, who played county cricket in an unhappy spell for Lancashire in 1996 and was dropped on the morning of their appearance in a one day final, said he was looking forward to the biggest challenge of his career which sees it set to get underway in May 2019."Since I retired from professional cricket in 2003, I have been given some fantastic opportunities in cricket administration and managing the ICC Cricket World Cup 2019 is the biggest and the best yet," said Elworthy.

Then there’s the gravitational metaphor for static-electric phenomena, or the ‘planetary orbit’ model of the atomic nucleus and electrons. Reviewing Richard Dawkins’s River Out of Eden (1995) in New Scientist, the geneticist Andrew Pomiankowski expands on Dawkins’s conceptual metaphor of ‘a river of genes’, writing: The river may fork in time. Each branch slowly drifts apart to give rise to a new species. Some branches dry up in extinction. Others split repeatedly, generating the 30 million branches that exist today. In the process, Pomiankowski reveals that metaphors not only shape the way we see things, but also carry story. In thinking through some of the ways that our relationship to metaphor might be changing, especially in educational settings, we consulted a study by Emily Weinstein and her research team at Harvard, published in 2014. They set out to study a possible decline in creativity among high-school students by comparing both visual artworks and creative writing collected between 1990-95, and again between 2006-11. Examining the style, content and form of adolescent art-making, the team hoped to understand the potential ‘generational shift’ between pre- and post-internet creativity. It turned out that there were observable gains in the sophistication and complexity of visual artwork, but when it came to the creative-writing endeavours of the two groups, the researchers found a ‘significant increase in young authors’ adherence to conventional writing practices related to genre, and a trend toward more formulaic narrative style’. The team cited standardised testing as a likely source of this lack of creativity, as well as changing modes of written communication that create ‘a multitude of opportunities for casual, text-based communication’ – in other words, for literalism, abbreviation and emojis standing in for words and feelings. With visual arts, by contrast, greater exposure to visual media, and the ‘expansive mental repositories of visual imagery’ informed and inspired student work. Of course, quantifying creativity is problematic, even with thoughtfully constructed controls, but it is provocative to consider what the authors saw as ‘a significant increase in and adherence to strict realism’, and how this might relate to a turn away from metaphoric thinking. Kyung Hee Kim, a psychologist at William and Mary College in Virginia, believes that creative thinking is ‘declining over all Americans of all ages’. According to her, one reason for the adherence to realism is pragmatic: children simply have ever-increasing opportunities and resources for knowledge-gathering and study, for ‘empirical abstraction’. But, she argues, ‘to be creative, they also need opportunities to engage in the mental process of building knowledge through mental actions’. In the labyrinthine world of conscious experience, I find myself grappling with questions that often feel more elusive than answerable. How does our perception of reality intertwine with the raw sensations and emotions that shape our existence? Is consciousness a product of our biology or an inherent part of the universe itself? These questions, while seemingly abstract, have profound implications for how we navigate life and understand our place in the cosmos.

A recent encounter with a group of neuroscientists, philosophers, and artists left me pondering their perspectives on these matters. The discussions ranged from the latest findings in neurobiology to the esoteric musings of quantum physics, yet they all seemed to coalesce around one central theme: our awareness is an intricate dance between our biological makeup and the world we inhabit.

One particularly fascinating point was made by a philosopher who argued that consciousness could be seen as a sort of adaptive mechanism. Much like how a chameleon changes color to blend in with its environment, so too does our consciousness shift to accommodate the demands of our surroundings. This adaptation serves not only our survival but also shapes our individuality and our connections to others.

This concept was further illustrated by an artist who spoke about her work exploring the intersection of technology and human emotion. She shared how her installations aimed to create immersive experiences that blurred the lines between the digital and the physical, allowing viewers to question their own perception of reality. In doing so, she highlighted the fluidity of our conscious experience and its ability to transform in response to new stimuli.

However, this adaptability raises questions about the nature of truth and reality itself. If our consciousness is shaped by our experiences, can we ever truly trust what we perceive? Are we not at the mercy of our own biases and limitations as much as we are observers of the world around us? These doubts cast a shadow over the certainty with which we often approach life's mysteries.

In contemplating these ideas, I find myself struck by their implications for how we interact with one another and the world at large. If our perception is constantly evolving, then perhaps it is not so simple to label someone or something as inherently "good" or "evil." Instead, we must recognize that our understanding of truth and morality is shaped by our experiences and that there is always room for growth and change.

As I continue to explore these thoughts, I am left with more questions than answers. Yet, in the process, I have come to appreciate the rich complexity of conscious experience and the endless possibilities it offers for understanding ourselves and our place in the universe. The journey may be fraught with uncertainty, but the promise of discovery is one that fuels my curiosity and keeps me moving forward.

Title: A Dim Sum Palace Discovery in the Slow Season - Golden Phoenix Delight!

I've been a bit skeptical about dim sum places, given my love for greasy goodness. But during a recent slow season visit to San Francisco, I stumbled upon the Golden Phoenix, tucked away in a quiet corner of Chinatown that I'd usually avoid like a tourist trap. The place was virtually empty when we walked in, which added a touch of intimacy to our dining experience.

Stepping inside, the aroma of steaming dumplings and fragrant teas hit us right away. The ambiance was serene, with soft Chinese music playing gently in the background. I felt like I had stepped into a tranquil oasis amidst the hustle-bustle of the city.

We were greeted warmly by the staff who guided us to our table. The menu, filled with an array of dim sum delights, seemed endless! I went for two classics - har gow (shrimp dumplings) and siu mai (pork and shrimp dumplings). My dining companion opted for the sticky rice wrapped in lotus leaf.

Our dim sum feast arrived promptly, steaming hot and beautifully presented. The har gow were perfect - translucent wrappers enclosing succulent, well-seasoned shrimps. The siu mai were equally delicious, with a generous filling that burst with flavor in every bite. The sticky rice, encased in the fragrant lotus leaf, was a delightful surprise. Each mouthful revealed different textures and flavors, from the chewy glutinous rice to the savory bits of chicken and mushrooms.

The best part? The price! Despite the quality of the food and the charming atmosphere, it was surprisingly affordable. We even took home a couple of boxes for later!

If you're ever in San Francisco during the slow season, don't miss out on the Golden Phoenix. It's a hidden gem that serves up authentic dim sum delights in a peaceful setting. I can't wait to return and explore more of their menu! Highly recommended!

Take the tale of my colleague, Tom. He found a substantial sum of money on the street one day - enough to significantly improve his life, yet not enough to change it entirely. He wrestled with the decision for days, tossing between keeping it and returning it. In the end, he chose to return it, citing principles he'd always held dear. But I couldn't help but wonder if there was more to it - if perhaps the weight of societal expectations had a hand in his choice.

Then there's Sarah, my old school friend. She was faced with an unplanned pregnancy at a young age. The path before her seemed clear: abort or keep the child. But she chose neither, opting instead for adoption. It was a decision that left scars on her heart, yet she carried them silently, not even telling me until years later. I marveled then at her strength, but now I question if there wasn't an element of self-preservation in her choice - the preservation of the life she'd built for herself.

And let's not forget about John, my former boss. He was accused of workplace misconduct, a claim that could have ended his career. But instead of admitting wrongdoing or fighting the allegations, he chose to quietly resign, disappearing from our lives without so much as a goodbye. I can't help but wonder if he made the choice out of fear - fear of losing face, fear of being judged, fear of the unknown.

These stories, and countless others like them, have left me questioning how we navigate these moral dilemmas. Is it through principles and values? Or is it through self-preservation and the fear of judgment? Is it about doing what's right or what feels easiest? I'm not sure I have the answer, but I know that I'll continue to ponder, to question, to wonder...and perhaps, in time, to understand a little more. For now, I'm left with more questions than answers, and a renewed appreciation for the complexities of the human heart.
