The New Aesthetic was accused of being apolitical — fascinated by the oddities and wonders being thrown up by drones and surveillance cameras without thinking about the politics behind them. This is plain wrong: politics seeps from nearly every pore of the New Aesthetic. It was often hard to see, but that’s what Bridle wanted to expose. The question is one of viewpoint. ‘As soon as you get CCTV, drones, satellite views and maps and all that kind of stuff,’ Bridle said, ‘you’re setting up an inherent inequality in how things are seen, and between the position of the viewer and the viewed. There are inherent power relations in that and technology makes them invisible. When you have a man in a watchtower, you look up at him, and that’s an obvious vision of power. When the man is in a bunker far away and you have just a little camera on a stalk … most people seem to be fine with that.’ The New Aesthetic is about seeing, then. And to see and be seen is to engage in those power relations. It might be that the New Aesthetic, as the writers Madeline Ashby and Rahel Aima have both said, is simply men waking up to the sensation of being under surveillance, something women have long experienced in the form of the ‘male gaze’. The riotous spread of new technologies of seeing — not just thousands of new cameras, but also recognition algorithms and other software and hardware that can detect a presence or identify an individual — is subjecting these power relations to constant flux, and the outcomes could be dangerous. The New Aesthetic was the outer froth of this silent social turmoil — the project was to make it legible. ‘By legibility I mean our own ability to read these systems, how much they can affect the way we see and act in the world, and the differing positions of power we have in the world based on how legible those systems are,’ Bridle said. ‘The programmers have a huge amount of agency in the world, because they can deconstruct, reverse engineer and write and construct and create these systems. text:
So, I've been pondering this thing about biology and human behavior. It's like peeling an onion, isn't it? Every layer you think you've understood just reveals another depth to explore. Humans, we're complex creatures, aren't we? We're not just our DNA sequences or our hormonal reactions; we're also our experiences, our cultures, and yes, our biases.

Take race, for example. In some circles, it's a hot button issue, yet experiments show that it's less of a determining factor than we might think. People can be influenced by something as simple as a shirt color, if told that it signifies group membership. And let's not forget about accents. Remember when I was in London a few years back? I made friends with a girl who had an accent different from mine. Yet, the fact that we were both British, or rather, Londoners, bonded us more than our ethnicities ever could.

But here's the kicker - just because race may not be as influential as we think doesn't mean it's insignificant. It's still a part of our identity, a part that shapes how we perceive and interact with the world. And that's where it gets tricky, because perception is a powerful thing. We see what we expect to see, we hear what we expect to hear, and sometimes, those expectations are shaped by our biology, our culture, or both.

Take gender stereotypes, for instance. They're a tricky beast, aren't they? Some argue that they influence our behavior in profound ways, shaping who we become. But then there are studies that seem to contradict this, studies that show that these stereotypes may not have the impact many think they do.

I remember a conversation I had with my sister about this once. She's a scientist, always digging into these things. We were discussing a study that suggested women perform worse on math tests when reminded of gender stereotypes. Yet, other studies seem to indicate that this effect is weak at best. My sister argued that maybe it's not the reminder of the stereotype that affects performance, but rather, the internalized expectations we have about ourselves and our capabilities.

It's a complex issue, isn't it? One that doesn't lend itself to simple answers or definitive conclusions. But then again, aren't most things in life like that? We're constantly learning, constantly adjusting our perspectives based on new evidence and experiences. And that's okay. Because maybe the journey is more important than the destination. Maybe it's in the questioning, the exploring, that we truly find ourselves.

text: JOHANNESBURG: South Africa on Monday banned its national cricket and rugby federations from hosting or bidding for international tournaments for at least a year due to their failure to increase representation of black players. The ministers decision was a major blow to the South African Rugby Unions intention to bid for the 2023 Rugby World Cup. The bidding process starts next month. South Africas government has been pushing for more black players to be included in the nations most popular sports but more than two decades after the end of apartheid whites still make up the bulk of players in athletics, cricket, netball and rugby."I have therefore resolved to revoke the privilege of Athletics South Africa (ASA), Cricket South Africa (CSA), Netball South Africa (NSA) and South African Rugby (SARU) to host and bid for major and mega international tournaments," Mbalula said in a statement. The soccer federation was the only one of the five to meet its transformation targets, he said. Mbalula said he would review the decision when considering the results on the 2016-17 Transformation Barometer, which could be at the end of next year.

In the labyrinthine world we inhabit, where technology and psychology intertwine like ivy on an ancient oak, art serves as a beacon of solace, a mirror reflecting our inner turmoil, and a bridge connecting us to our fellow travelers. Art, much like the enigmatic works of Ryan Trecartin or Nam June Paik, transcends the boundaries of time and space, challenging traditional assumptions with each stroke, each pixel, each note.

Contemplating this kaleidoscope of creativity, one cannot help but wonder: are we comfortable that todays art is still using 19th-century technology? Does the electrifying vibrancy of contemporary culture leave us as discomfited as did the audiences for James Joyce or Pina Bausch? I fear were missing out, and thats a shame.

Speaking as an observer rather than a creator, I am enormously intrigued by the canvas that today's artists have to paint. Their art, the art of the future, will be a symphony of time and location, built on personal, historical, and geographic data that can shift and morph like chameleon skin to adapt the experience to wherever one encounters it.

At the same time, physical surfaces are likely to become more receptive to digital information, both visually and at a sensory level, so we can expect the physical world around us to become more malleable and transitional, depending on us. The physicality of art will blur into the digital realm, creating a harmony that resonates within our souls.

I suspect, too, that we will see a huge rise in authenticated art; cultural experiences related to our personal history within a particular artwork or experience. Whether this happens within the hallowed halls of a gallery or simply out in the real world, art might well come shopping with us. The dichotomy between passive and engaged consumption of culture will grow ever wider.

As we traverse this landscape of change, one cannot help but ponder: what of freedom and belonging? Is there tension between these two fundamental human needs in the digital age? Art may well serve as a balm for this schism, offering both the freedom to explore and express oneself, and the sense of belonging that comes from shared understanding.

Perhaps, in this new world order, we will find solace not just in paintings but in immersive installations, in games culture, in the very air around us. We may yet come to appreciate Yung Jake's insane, eccentric world as a reflection of our own complexities: multi-linear, time-agnostic, fragmented and discordant.

And so, we stand at the precipice of a new era, armed with curiosity and an open heart. Let us embrace this journey, for it is through art that we may find ourselves, and each other, in this ever-evolving tapestry of human experience.

text: The famous sculptor and painter was also renowned for his temper. When the handsome young Raphael first arrived on the Rome scene and was quickly commissioned by Pope Julius II, Michelangelo labelled him a bitter rival and proceeded to repeatedly accuse him of plagiarism. At one point, Michelangelo worked on his ceiling masterpiece behind a partition in order to hide it from Raphael. The latter, no shrinking wallflower himself, managed to arrange a view of it and later, in his fresco The School of Athens, incorporated a seated figure taken straight from Michelangelo’s work. Thanks to these machinations, the rivalry between the two giants became one of the most famous in the annals of art. It wasn’t until the establishment of science societies in the late 16th century that major scientific rivalries reared their head. Perhaps the most notable early outbreak was the fierce war between Newton and Leibniz, each of whom claimed to be the first to invent calculus – today widely considered to have been developed independently by each of them. The feud caused such a rift between the English and European mathematics communities that, for more than a century, almost no scientific knowledge was exchanged between them. In the early 18th century, Newton balked at nothing in his campaign for priority over the invention of calculus: in 1712, the Royal Society of London published a document granting Newton ownership of the invention and discrediting Leibniz. The paper, however, should be taken with an exceptionally large grain of salt, since Newton, who was president of the society at the time, personally appointed all the committee members and even wrote large parts of the document himself. The two colossi of mathematics never met in person and it is not clear that Leibniz was ever exposed to Newton’s work. One can only imagine how a productive exchange of their ideas, disputed over a public platform, could have enhanced the introduction of calculus and the scientific developments that followed. ‘The aim of argument, or of discussion, should not be victory but progress,’ said the French 19th-century essayist Joseph Joubert. Once the new societies and their publications made information more accessible, rivalry between scientists, research institutes and even states began to drive new discoveries. Journalistic interest in the drama enabled more public exposure to science. In one notable case, the dispute between Thomas Huxley and Richard Owen, two of the leading biologists in 19th‑century Britain, shined an important spotlight on Charles Darwin’s theory of evolution, little‑known to the general public at the time. One of the stormiest scientific rivalries of recent years raged between the paleoanthropologists Donald Johanson and Richard Leakey over the discovery of some of the oldest fossils from pre‑human species. Dear Reader,

Have you ever found yourself standing before a painting, a sculpture, a symphony, and felt the hairs on your arms stand on end? A timeless beauty that seems to transcend the boundaries of time and space. Why does this art resonate so deeply within us, while much of what we create is forgotten within a generation?

Consider the Crusades, a tumultuous period in human history marked by faith, ambition, and bloodshed. Yet, amidst the chaos, a distinct ritual emerged - the taking of the cross. This act, filled with penance, hope, and fear, was not just a declaration of war but also a purification rite for potential crusaders. The cruciform cloth, sewn onto their garments, became a symbol of their commitment, a tangible reminder of their vows.

Fast forward to the modern era, where technology has become the new 'holy cross.' We are witnessing an unprecedented environmental crisis, a potential apocalypse brewing in the heart of our technological civilization. And yet, we continue to push for progress, to create ever-more powerful technologies, despite the risks they pose to our very existence.

The longtermists among us argue that technology is morally neutral, just a tool in the hands of its users. But what happens when these tools are wielded by imperfect beings? When the consequences are not just good or bad but existential?

Perhaps the problem lies not with the tools we create but with our own hearts, with our unwillingness to confront the fundamental flaws within ourselves. We seek to conquer the external world, manipulate our biology, and yet, in doing so, we risk destroying the very biosphere that sustains us.

Is it not ironic that as we strive to create timeless art - be it technology or culture - we forget the lessons of our past? That we repeat the same mistakes, over and over again, until they become ingrained in our collective unconsciousness?

And yet, amidst this chaos, there are glimmers of hope. Artists who dare to challenge the status quo, scientists who strive to understand the intricate workings of the universe, activists who fight for a better world. They remind us that we are more than just tools, more than just creators of art or technology. We have the power to shape our own destiny, to create a future worth living in.

But will we choose to heed their call? Or will we continue down this path of self-destruction, forgetting the lessons of the past and repeating the mistakes of our ancestors? The choice is ours, dear reader. The power to create timeless art, or to let it slip away into the sands of time.

As I write these words, I cannot help but wonder - will this essay be forgotten within a generation? Or will it resonate with someone, somewhere, in some distant future? Only time will tell. But for now, I am left with one question ringing in my ears - what will you create today, and will it stand the test of time?

We remember them more vividly and they play a larger role in shaping our lives. Farewells, accidents, bad parenting, financial losses and even a random snide comment take up most of our psychic space, leaving little room for compliments or pleasant experiences to help us along life’s challenging path. The staggering human ability to adapt ensures that joy over a salary hike will abate within months, leaving only a benchmark for future raises. We feel pain, but not the absence of it. Hundreds of scientific studies from around the world confirm our negativity bias: while a good day has no lasting effect on the following day, a bad day carries over. We process negative data faster and more thoroughly than positive data, and they affect us longer. Socially, we invest more in avoiding a bad reputation than in building a good one. Emotionally, we go to greater lengths to avoid a bad mood than to experience a good one. Pessimists tend to assess their health more accurately than optimists. In our era of political correctness, negative remarks stand out and seem more authentic. People – even babies as young as six months old – are quick to spot an angry face in a crowd, but slower to pick out a happy one; in fact, no matter how many smiles we see in that crowd, we will always spot the angry face first. The machinery by which we recognise facial emotion, located in a brain region called the amygdala, reflects our nature as a whole: two-thirds of neurons in the amygdala are geared toward bad news, immediately responding and storing it in our long-term memory, points out neuropsychologist Rick Hanson, Senior Fellow of the Greater Good Science Center at University of California, Berkeley. This is what causes the ‘fight or flight’ reflex – a survival instinct based on our ability to use memory to quickly assess threats. Good news, by comparison, takes 12 whole seconds to travel from temporary to long-term memory. Our ancient ancestors were better off jumping away from every stick that looked like a snake than carefully examining it before deciding what to do. Our gloomy bent finds its way into spoken language, with almost two thirds of English words conveying the negative side of things. In the vocabulary we use to describe people, this figure rises to a staggering 74 per cent. And English is not alone. Aside from Dutch, all other languages lean toward the bleak. text:
Why do we create? Is it to express our innermost feelings, to share our deepest thoughts, or to simply make sense of the chaos that surrounds us? Artistic expression has always been a reflection of human emotion, a silent cry echoing through the ages. But what happens when these expressions raise uncomfortable questions, when they force us to confront the darker aspects of our humanity?

Take a look at Vincent Van Gogh's "Starry Night." The swirling blues and yellows are a dance of emotions, a visual representation of his turbulent mind. But what if I told you that the very same painting, with its tranquil night sky, was created during one of his most intense bouts of mental illness? Doesn't it make you question the nature of madness, and the beauty that can emerge from it?

Or consider Edvard Munch's "The Scream." The agony etched onto the figure's face is palpable, a scream that seems to echo through time. But what if I told you that Munch created this iconic image after witnessing a sunset that filled him with an overwhelming fear of existence? Doesn't it make you question the fragility of our sanity, and the terror that lurks beneath the surface?

These are just two examples, but they serve to illustrate a broader point. Artistic expression is not always pretty or comforting. It often reflects the ugliness within us, the pain we try to hide. It forces us to confront our darkest fears, to acknowledge our deepest insecurities.

But here's where I falter. Is this a good thing? Is it healthy to constantly expose ourselves to such darkness? Doesn't it risk driving us mad, like Van Gogh and Munch?

I don't have the answer. I only know that art has a power unlike any other. It can heal, it can inspire, but it can also wound deeply. And sometimes, that wound is necessary. Sometimes, we need to be reminded of our own vulnerability, our own humanity. Because it is in those moments of pain and uncertainty that we truly learn about ourselves.

So, I ask again, why do we create? Perhaps the answer lies not in what we create, but in what we become through the act of creation. We create to understand ourselves, to confront our fears, and ultimately, to survive. And isn't that the most human thing of all?

In the realm of historical narratives, a seismic shift occurred when Howard Zinn's 'A People's History' graced our bookshelves. This book wasn't just another addition to the library; it was a challenge to the status quo, a breath of fresh air that dared to question the accepted norms.

I remember first encountering it in a dusty corner of my local bookstore, its worn-out cover and faded spine suggesting that it had seen many hands before mine. I picked it up, flipped through the pages, and found myself captivated by stories that were unfamiliar yet strikingly resonant.

Zinn's narrative was a far cry from the sanitized versions of history I had grown accustomed to. Here was a history that acknowledged the struggles, the triumphs, and the sheer resilience of ordinary people. A history that put them at the center, not the margins.

The implications of this shift are profound. For one, it challenges the notion of history as an objective truth. If our understanding of the past can be so vastly different based on whose story we choose to listen to, then perhaps there's more to uncover, more perspectives to consider.

But more importantly, it forces us to question our own biases. It invites us to look beyond the victors' narratives, to see the world through the eyes of those who were often silenced or forgotten. It reminds us that history is not just a series of events, but a reflection of power dynamics, of struggles for justice and equality.

Yet, it's not all rosy. Zinn's narrative, like any other, is not without its criticisms. Some argue that it oversimplifies complex issues, reducing them to neat narratives of good versus evil. Others point out factual inaccuracies or omissions.

And yet, these criticisms seem almost trivial when placed against the broader impact of the book. It has sparked conversations, fueled debates, and inspired a new generation of historians to challenge the status quo. It has reminded us that history is not just a matter of dates and names, but a means of understanding our world, of finding meaning in our struggles and triumphs.

As I continue to navigate through its pages, I find myself asking questions, questioning my own beliefs, and challenging the narratives I've grown up with. Zinn's 'A People's History' is not just a book; it's a call to action, a reminder that our understanding of history can shape our future, and that we have the power to change it.

But as I close this brief reflection, I find myself at a loss for words. For how does one encapsulate the complexities of a book that has challenged my understanding of history, of power, of justice? How does one express the profound impact it has had on me, and on countless others?

Perhaps it's best to leave it at this: Howard Zinn's 'A People's History' is a testament to the power of storytelling, to the importance of challenging the status quo, to the resilience of the human spirit. It's a call to action, a reminder that our understanding of history can shape our future, and that we have the power to change it.

And as I continue my journey through its pages, I find myself filled with hope, with a renewed sense of purpose. For in these stories, I see not just the struggles and triumphs of the past, but a reflection of my own potential, a reminder of the power we all hold to shape our future.

And yet nobody down on the sidewalk, looking up and yelling ‘Don’t!’ and ‘Hang on!’, can understand the jump. Not really. You’d have to have personally been trapped and felt flames to really understand a terror way beyond falling. The suicidal person, in other words, is not misguided but rather literally facing different choices – ones unimaginable to those who do not also have flames slowly engulfing them. I don’t think I can really explain what reading all this meant to me. The book could see me like a mirror at that moment and describe it all right back. text: strong>KARACHI: State Bank of Pakistan has issued guidelines on prevention against cyber attacks. Through these guidelines, the SBP has advised banks/MFBs/DFIS to continuously improve their cyber security controls and procedures so as to anticipate, withstand, detect, and respond to cyber attacks.</strongSBPs cyber security instructions provide guidelines in the areas of risk ownership and management responsibility; periodic evaluation and monitoring of cyber security controls; regular independent assessment and tests, and industry collaboration and contingency planning. The new instructions require the Board of Directors (BoD) of the institutions to regularly evaluate the adequacy of cyber security systems and action plans with regard to emerging cyber threats. The senior management is required to ensure that an organizational plan of action for cyber security management exists in each institution and is regularly reviewed and updated for implementation. It may be noted here that cyber threats have become a global phenomenon and are continually growing in sophistication and impact, despite the advances in cyber effective delivery of services, these have also posed a number of new threats and risks. The banks/DFIs/MFBs are required to make necessary arrangements to comply with the instructions latest by December 31, 2016.

The electric vehicle manufacturer recorded a 45% rise in Q2 revenues compared to the same period last year, reaching $11.96 billion. However, this impressive figure was partially offset by a net loss of $438 million, widening from the previous quarter's $104 million deficit.

The regulatory challenges looming over Tesla stem primarily from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), which is investigating approximately 50,000 Model S and X vehicles for potential safety issues related to their Autopilot feature. In addition, the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has been scrutinizing Tesla's accounting practices and communications with investors.

Despite these hurdles, Tesla remains optimistic about its future prospects. The company aims to ramp up production of its Model Y SUV and is planning to expand its Supercharger network in Europe and China. Furthermore, Tesla has announced plans to establish a new Gigafactory in Austin, Texas, which is expected to produce both electric vehicles and batteries for energy storage.

As the electric vehicle market continues to grow, Tesla faces stiff competition from traditional automakers like General Motors and startups such as Rivian and Lucid Motors. Nevertheless, with its strong brand recognition and technological edge, Tesla remains a formidable player in the industry, seeking to lead the charge towards a sustainable future.

For Derrida, the idea of the secret is intimately bound up with the idea of ‘confession’, be it political, personal or religious. Confession need not be forced, of course. In fact, it can be an act of love. If I confess to you my ‘deepest secrets’, I am – in commonsense belief – ‘telling you who I really am’, or rather showing you who I really am, by performing a kind of revelation that makes me vulnerable and open, and inviting a reciprocal openness. I trust/love you so much that I have no secrets. More prosaically, it can also be an act of profound friendship – another of Derrida’s themes – and of bonding. When someone says: ‘Do you want to know a secret?’ we feel not only the thrill of the illicit, but a sense of being entered into a community, even if it is one of only two people. We share secrets. In each of these examples, confessions forced or otherwise, the tacit understanding is that the confession is indeed an act of revealing the self. In Nineteen Eighty-Four, Winston exposes his secret and destroys his ‘self’. If I confess to you my secrets, you enter into my most intimate selfhood, we share it. Thus, we have a commonsense – and legalistic – belief that there is a direct relationship between our secrets and our selves. This is also, traditionally, a religious belief, and the connection between religion and secrets is a powerful and enduring one. The Catholic rite of confession is an exemplary model of this. To the priest, I reveal my secrets, and therefore my true self, the self only God otherwise has access to. If I keep secrets from the confessional, the self I offer up to God and the self that God knows to be true are misaligned. I betray God because I withhold information from His representative/proxy on Earth. Thus, Catholic guilt – the guilt of non-confession, the guilt of the unmentioned transgressions in both thought and act. For Derrida, as the philosopher Agata Bielik-Robson points out in her excellent book Derrida’s Marrano Passover (2022), it was his interest in one particular religious group that opened up his own dialogue with the idea of secrets – a group so secret that it forgot that it disguised its own existence even to itself. Guiteau, who was clearly not of sound mind but had satisfactory intelligence, was convicted and hanged. The courts have been relatively unresponsive to medical expertise, even as the fields of psychiatry and psychology have added to our understanding of mental illness. The 16th-century Dutch physician Johann Weyer sought to challenge the Saxon Code of 1572 on its treatment of insanity, complaining that it didn’t reflect the realities of mental illness. The judge retorted: ‘Weyer is not a lawyer, but a physician – consequently, his view on the relationship between mental disease and transgressions of the written law are of no moment.’ Nearly 400 years later, in 1950, the schism between legal definitions of insanity and the psychiatric realities of mental illness continued; the US Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter noted ‘the pathological processes which give rise to the conflict between so-called legal and medical insanity’. In the ensuing almost 40 years, Toole writes, the ‘legal definition of exculpatory mental disease in the context of criminal liability has remained largely static, sheltered from the immediate influence of medical theory and advancements’. The current legal definition of insanity has no psychiatric correlate; it is a pure creature of law, owing nothing to psychiatry and the sciences of brain and behaviour. As the psychiatrist Gregory Zilboorg wrote in Mind, Medicine and Man (1943): Except for totally deteriorated, drooling, hopeless psychotics of long standing, and congenital idiots – who seldom commit murder or have the opportunity to commit murder – the great majority and perhaps all murderers know what they are doing, the nature and quality of their act, and the consequences thereof, and they are therefore legally sane, regardless of the opinion of any psychiatrist. The lack of a psychiatric correlate makes it look like psychiatrists and psychologists are unreliable or that mental illness is purely subjective; the fact is, mental health experts often can’t agree on whether a defendant fits into the legal definition of insanity, even though they agree about the defendant’s diagnosis and degree of psychiatric impairment. As the forensic psychiatrist Thomas Gutheil at Harvard Medical School said in 1998: ‘Psychiatric testimony is about meeting the legal criteria for an insanity-related defence, not about mental illness.’ In ‘On Responsibility’ (1996), the federal judge Richard Lowell Nygaard wrote: With contemporary psychiatric and psychological data, the legitimate experts, who must testify about mental conditions, are unable to make accurate and scientific determinations fit into the rigid legal definitions the law imposes … Trials in which a jury must decide upon the accused’s guilt based upon the contradictory testimony of experts result in ‘battles of experts’ that virtually assure arbitrary outcomes. We tend to measure the wickedness of an actor by our degree of outrage over the act When testifying

In the long term, analysts predict a more competitive landscape, with Samsung now boasting an even wider product portfolio. The combined resources could potentially lead to groundbreaking advancements in technology. However, concerns have been raised about potential monopolistic practices and the impact on competition in the tech industry.

The deal comes at a time when both companies are grappling with challenges. Apple's iPhone sales have plateaued, while Samsung has struggled to maintain its market share against emerging players like Huawei and Xiaomi. The merger could provide a much-needed boost for both companies, allowing them to innovate and grow in new areas.

As the dust settles, one thing is clear: the tech industry will never be the same. This acquisition marks a significant shift in the power dynamics of the industry, with Samsung now at the forefront of innovation. Only time will tell how this merger will reshape the future of technology.
